summary: This section outlines the three distinct phases of plant growth: the meristematic phase, the phase of elongation, and the phase of maturation. It describes the specific cellular characteristics, structural changes, and locations associated with each phase.
title: Phases of Growth

evidence_snippets.text: The increased growth per unit time is termed as growth rate.
In arithmetic growth, following mitotic cell division, only one daughter cell continues to divide while the other differentiates and matures.
In geometrical growth, both the progeny cells following mitotic cell division retain the ability to divide and continue to do so.
If we plot the parameter of growth against time, we get a typical sigmoid or S-curve.
Absolute growth rate: measurement and the comparison of total growth per unit time.
Relative growth rate: The growth of the given system per unit time expressed on a common basis

summary: This section defines growth rate and explains its mathematical representation through arithmetic and geometric growth models. It describes the characteristic sigmoid growth curve of living organisms and distinguishes between absolute and relative growth rates.
title: Growth Rates

evidence_snippets.text: Essential conditions for growth include water, oxygen, and nutrients.
Every plant organism has an optimum temperature range best suited for its growth.
Environmental signals such as light and gravity also affect certain phases or stages of growth.

summary: This section details the essential conditions and environmental factors required for plant growth. It explains the roles of water, oxygen, and nutrients, alongside environmental signals like temperature, light, and gravity.
title: Conditions for Growth

evidence_snippets.text: Cells derived from root apical meristems... undergo maturation to perform specific functions, a process called differentiation.
living differentiated cells to regain the capacity to divide under specific conditions, a phenomenon known as dedifferentiation.
new cells eventually lose their capacity to divide again... this subsequent maturation is called redifferentiation.
differentiation in plants is considered 'open' because the final structure of a cell or tissue at maturity is often determined by its location

summary: This section explains the developmental processes of differentiation, dedifferentiation, and redifferentiation in plant cells. It describes how cells mature, regain division capacity, and mature again, highlighting that plant differentiation is an open process determined by cell location.
title: Differentiation, Dedifferentiation and Redifferentiation

evidence_snippets.text: Development is broadly considered the sum of growth and differentiation.
Plants possess the ability to follow different developmental pathways... this ability is called plasticity.
heterophylly, where leaves of juvenile plants differ in shape from those of mature plants
Development in plants is governed by a combination of intrinsic and extrinsic factors.

summary: This section defines plant development as the comprehensive sum of growth and differentiation, outlining the cellular sequence from division to senescence. It introduces the concepts of plasticity and heterophylly as structural adaptations to environmental or life-stage changes, and identifies the intrinsic and extrinsic factors that control these processes.
title: Development

evidence_snippets.text: Plant Growth Regulators (PGRs) are small, simple molecules of diverse chemical compositions, often referred to as plant hormones or phytohormones.
The first group consists of plant growth promoters, which are involved in activities like cell division, enlargement, pattern formation...
The second group consists of growth inhibitors, which respond to wounds and biotic/abiotic stresses, often inducing dormancy and abscission

summary: This section defines Plant Growth Regulators (PGRs), also known as phytohormones, and describes their diverse chemical compositions. It classifies them into plant growth promoters and plant growth inhibitors based on their primary physiological functions.
title: Characteristics of Plant Growth Regulators

evidence_snippets.text: Auxins... help to initiate rooting in stem cuttings, an application widely used for plant propagation.
Gibberellins also promotes bolting (internode elongation just prior to flowering) in beet, cabbages
Cytokinins help overcome the apical dominance. They promote nutrient mobilisation which helps in the delay of leaf senescence.
Ethylene is highly effective in fruit ripening. It enhances the respiration rate during ripening of the fruits.
ABA stimulates the closure of stomata and increases the tolerance of plants to various kinds of stresses.

summary: This section details the physiological effects and agricultural applications of five major classes of plant growth regulators: auxins, gibberellins, cytokinins, ethylene, and abscisic acid. It explains how these hormones individually, synergistically, or antagonistically regulate plant growth, development, and responses to environmental stimuli.
title: Physiological Effects of Plant Growth Regulators

evidence_snippets.text: In plants, meristems are the sites of growth.
Following cell division in root and shoot apical meristem cells, the growth could be arithmetic or geometrical.
A differentiated cell may dedifferentiate and then redifferentiate.
Plant exhibit plasticity in development.
Intercellular intrinsic factors are the chemicals, called plant growth regulators (PGR).

summary: This section summarizes plant growth and development, highlighting the roles of meristems, phases of growth, and cellular processes like differentiation, dedifferentiation, and redifferentiation. It also outlines the influence of intrinsic plant growth regulators (PGRs) and extrinsic environmental factors on these developmental processes.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: The process of exchanging $O_2$ from the atmosphere with $CO_2$ produced by the cells is called breathing, which is commonly referred to as respiration.
Organisms utilize oxygen ($O_2$) to indirectly break down simple nutrient molecules like glucose, amino acids, and fatty acids to derive the energy

summary: This section introduces the biological processes of breathing and respiration, explaining how organisms exchange atmospheric oxygen for cellular carbon dioxide. It describes how oxygen is utilized in catabolic pathways to break down nutrients and derive energy for life activities.
title: Breathing and Exchange of Gases

evidence_snippets.text: The mechanisms of breathing vary significantly across different groups of animals, primarily depending on their habitats and levels of organization.
Each terminal bronchiole gives rise to a number of very thin, irregular-walled and vascularised bag-like structures called alveoli.
Humans have two lungs which are covered by a double layered pleura, with pleural fluid between them.
The respiratory system is divided into two functional parts.
The lungs are situated in the thoracic chamber, which is anatomically an air-tight chamber.

summary: This section introduces the diverse respiratory organs found across the animal kingdom and details the anatomy of the human respiratory system. It describes the structure of the bronchial tree, alveoli, pleural membranes, and the thoracic chamber, while outlining the functional divisions and basic steps of respiration.
title: Respiratory Organs

evidence_snippets.text: The movement of air into and out of the lungs is carried out by creating a pressure gradient between the lungs and the atmosphere.
On average, a healthy human breathes 12-16 times/minute.

summary: This section explains the physical mechanism of breathing, which consists of inspiration and expiration driven by pressure gradients between the lungs and the atmosphere. It details how the diaphragm and intercostal muscles alter thoracic volume to facilitate air movement, and introduces spirometry for measuring breathing volumes.
title: Mechanism of Breathing

evidence_snippets.text: Tidal Volume (TV) is the volume of air inspired or expired during a normal respiration. It is approximately 500 mL.
Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV) is the additional volume of air a person can inspire by a forcible inspiration.
Residual Volume (RV) is the volume of air remaining in the lungs even after a forcible expiration.

summary: This section defines and quantifies the various respiratory volumes and capacities, such as Tidal Volume, Reserve Volumes, and Residual Volume. It explains how these volumes combine to form clinical capacities like Vital Capacity and Total Lung Capacity to assess pulmonary function.
title: Respiratory Volumes and Capacities

evidence_snippets.text: Alveoli are the primary sites of exchange of gases.
Pressure contributed by an individual gas in a mixture of gases is called partial pressure
solubility of CO2 is 20-25 times higher than that of O2
The diffusion membrane is made up of three major layers

summary: This section explains the exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide between alveoli, blood, and tissues via simple diffusion. It details the role of partial pressure gradients, gas solubility, and the three-layered structure of the thin diffusion membrane.
title: Exchange of Gases

evidence_snippets.text: Blood is the medium of transport for $O_2$ and $CO_2$.
About 97 per cent of $O_2$ is transported by RBCs in the blood.
Nearly 20-25 per cent of $CO_2$ is transported by RBCs whereas 70 per cent of it is carried as bicarbonate.

summary: This section outlines the mechanisms of oxygen and carbon dioxide transport in human blood. It specifies the proportions of these gases carried by red blood cells, dissolved in plasma, and as bicarbonate.
title: Transport of Gases

evidence_snippets.text: O2 can bind with haemoglobin in a reversible manner to form oxyhaemoglobin.
A sigmoid curve is obtained when percentage saturation of haemoglobin with O2 is plotted against the pO2.
Every 100 ml of oxygenated blood can deliver around 5 ml of O2 to the tissues under normal physiological conditions.

summary: This section explains the transport of oxygen in the blood through its reversible binding with haemoglobin to form oxyhaemoglobin. It details the sigmoid oxygen dissociation curve and explains how physiological factors like partial pressure, pH, and temperature regulate oxygen binding in the alveoli and its release in tissues.
title: Transport of Oxygen

evidence_snippets.text: CO2 is carried by haemoglobin as carbamino-haemoglobin (about 20-25 per cent).
RBCs contain a very high concentration of the enzyme, carbonic anhydrase
every 100 ml of deoxygenated blood delivers approximately 4 ml of CO2 to the alveoli

summary: This section describes the mechanisms of carbon dioxide transport in the blood, primarily through bicarbonate ions and carbamino-haemoglobin. It explains how partial pressures of carbon dioxide and oxygen regulate these processes, enabling the delivery of carbon dioxide from tissues to the alveoli.
title: Transport of Carbon dioxide

evidence_snippets.text: A specialised centre in the medulla region of the brain, called the respiratory rhythm centre, is primarily responsible for this regulation.
Another centre in the pons region, known as the pneumotaxic centre, can moderate the functions of the respiratory rhythm centre.
A chemosensitive area situated adjacent to the rhythm centre is highly sensitive to CO2 and hydrogen ions (H+).
receptors associated with the aortic arch and carotid artery can recognise changes in CO2 and H+ concentration

summary: This section describes the neural and chemical mechanisms that regulate the human respiratory rhythm to meet tissue demands. It details the roles of the respiratory rhythm centre in the medulla, the pneumotaxic centre in the pons, and chemosensitive areas and arterial receptors that monitor carbon dioxide and hydrogen ion levels.
title: Regulation of Respiration

evidence_snippets.text: Asthma is a difficulty in breathing causing wheezing due to inflammation of the bronchi and bronchioles.
Emphysema is a chronic disorder in which alveolar walls are damaged, leading to a decrease in the respiratory surface area
Long-term exposure leads to inflammation and fibrosis (proliferation of fibrous tissues), causing serious lung damage.

summary: This section covers major disorders of the human respiratory system, including asthma, emphysema, and occupational respiratory disorders. It explains their physiological causes, symptoms, and impacts on the respiratory system.
title: Disorders of Respiratory System

evidence_snippets.text: Blood is a special connective tissue consisting of a fluid matrix, plasma, and formed elements.
ABO grouping is based on the presence or absence of two surface antigens on RBCs: A and B.
A significant medical concern arises from Rh incompatibility between an Rh-ve pregnant mother and her Rh+ve foetus.
Blood coagulation, or clotting, is a vital physiological response to injury that prevents excessive blood loss.

summary: This section details the composition and functions of blood, focusing on plasma and formed elements such as erythrocytes, leucocytes, and platelets. It also explains the ABO and Rh blood grouping systems, the clinical implications of Rh incompatibility, and the biochemical cascade of blood coagulation.
title: Blood

evidence_snippets.text: This leaked fluid is known as interstitial fluid or tissue fluid.
The lymphatic system is a specialized network of vessels that collects tissue fluid and returns it to the major veins.
The fluid within this system is called lymph.
fats are absorbed into the lymph through lacteals located in the intestinal villi.

summary: This section describes the formation, composition, and function of tissue fluid (interstitial fluid) and its role in nutrient and gas exchange. It also introduces the lymphatic system, detailing the composition of lymph, its role in immunity, and the absorption of fats through lacteals.
title: Lymph (Tissue Fluid)

evidence_snippets.text: Animals exhibit two primary types of circulatory patterns: open and closed.
Fishes have a two-chambered heart (one atrium and one ventricle) and exhibit single circulation.
Crocodiles, birds, and mammals have a four-chambered heart (two atria and two ventricles), which ensures complete double circulation

summary: This section introduces the two primary circulatory patterns, open and closed systems, and details the evolutionary diversity of vertebrate hearts. It explains how heart structure relates to single, incomplete double, and complete double circulation across different animal groups.
title: Circulatory Pathways

evidence_snippets.text: The human heart is a mesodermally derived organ located in the thoracic cavity between the lungs, slightly offset to the left.
it is protected by the pericardium, a double-walled membranous sac containing pericardial fluid that cushions the organ.
The heart is divided into four chambers: two small upper atria and two larger lower ventricles.
These valves ensure that blood moves strictly in one direction—from the atria to the ventricles and then into the major arteries
The SAN produces the highest frequency of action potentials... and is responsible for initiating and maintaining the heart's rhythmic contraction.

summary: This section details the anatomical structure, internal chambers, and valvular system of the human heart. It also explains the specialized nodal tissue, specifically the sino-atrial node, which acts as the pacemaker to regulate rhythmic cardiac contractions.
title: Human Circulatory System

evidence_snippets.text: The cardiac cycle begins with all four chambers in a relaxed state, known as joint diastole.
The cardiac cycle is the sequential event of systole and diastole that repeats cyclically.
During each cycle, each ventricle pumps out approximately 70 mL of blood, known as the stroke volume.
The first heart sound, 'lub', is associated with the closure of the tricuspid and bicuspid valves

summary: This section explains the cardiac cycle, detailing the sequential events of systole and diastole that occur during a single heartbeat. It defines key physiological parameters such as stroke volume and cardiac output, and describes the origin of the clinical heart sounds, 'lub' and 'dub'.
title: Cardiac Cycle

evidence_snippets.text: An electrocardiogram (ECG) is a graphical representation of the heart's electrical activity during a cardiac cycle
The P-wave represents atrial depolarization, leading to atrial contraction.
The QRS complex represents ventricular depolarization, which initiates ventricular contraction (systole).
The T-wave represents ventricular repolarization, marking the return of the ventricles to a relaxed state

summary: This section introduces the Electrocardiogram (ECG) as a graphical representation of the heart's electrical activity during a cardiac cycle. It explains how a standard ECG is recorded using electrical leads and details the physiological significance of the P-wave, QRS complex, and T-wave.
title: Electrocardiogram (ECG)

evidence_snippets.text: Blood flows through a closed system of arteries and veins.
These vessels consist of three layers: the inner tunica intima, the middle tunica media, and the outer tunica externa.
The heart drives two distinct circulatory pathways: pulmonary and systemic circulation.

summary: This section explains the mechanism of double circulation in humans, detailing the three-layered anatomy of blood vessels. It distinguishes between pulmonary and systemic circulation pathways and describes specialized vascular networks like the hepatic portal and coronary systems.
title: Double Circulation

evidence_snippets.text: The heart's normal activities are auto-regulated by specialized nodal tissue, classifying the heart as myogenic.
A neural center in the medulla oblongata can modulate cardiac function via the autonomic nervous system (ANS).
Neural signals from sympathetic nerves can increase the heart rate, the strength of ventricular contraction, and the overall cardiac output.
Conversely, parasympathetic neural signals decrease the heart rate, the speed of action potential conduction, and the cardiac output.
Additionally, hormones from the adrenal medulla can increase cardiac output.

summary: This section explains the regulation of cardiac activity, highlighting the intrinsic myogenic nature of the heart and its modulation by the autonomic nervous system and adrenal hormones.
title: Regulation of Cardiac Activity

evidence_snippets.text: Hypertension is defined as blood pressure consistently exceeding the normal range of 120/80 mmHg.
Coronary Artery Disease (CAD), also known as atherosclerosis, occurs when the lumen of the arteries supplying the heart muscle becomes narrower
Angina, or angina pectoris, is characterized by acute chest pain when the heart muscle does not receive enough oxygen.
Heart failure is a state where the heart fails to pump blood effectively enough to meet the body's requirements.

summary: This section details major disorders of the human circulatory system, including hypertension, coronary artery disease, angina, and heart failure. It outlines their physiological definitions, causes, and effects on the body.
title: Disorders of Circulatory System

evidence_snippets.text: Ammonia, urea and uric acid are the major forms of nitrogenous wastes excreted by the animals.
The process of excreting ammonia is Ammonotelism.
Terrestrial adaptation necessitated the production of lesser toxic nitrogenous wastes like urea and uric acid for conservation of water.
In most of the invertebrates, excretory structures are simple tubular forms whereas vertebrates have complex tubular organs called kidneys.

summary: This section introduces the major nitrogenous wastes excreted by animals—ammonia, urea, and uric acid—and classifies animals into ammonotelic, ureotelic, and uricotelic based on their mode of excretion. It also provides a comparative survey of various excretory structures, such as protonephridia, nephridia, Malpighian tubules, and antennal glands, across different animal groups.
title: Introduction to Excretion

evidence_snippets.text: In humans, the excretory system consists of a pair of kidneys, one pair of ureters, a urinary bladder and a urethra.
Inside the kidney, there are two zones: an outer cortex and an inner medulla.
Each kidney has nearly one million complex tubular structures called nephrons, which are the functional units.
Glomerulus alongwith Bowman’s capsule, is called the malpighian body or renal corpuscle.

summary: This section details the gross and internal anatomy of the human excretory system, focusing on the kidneys. It explains the structure of the nephron as the functional unit, including its vascular and tubular components, and distinguishes between cortical and juxtamedullary nephrons.
title: Human Excretory System

evidence_snippets.text: The production of urine is a complex process involving three primary stages: glomerular filtration, reabsorption, and secretion.
This process is called ultrafiltration because almost all plasma components, except proteins, pass into the lumen of the Bowman's capsule.

summary: Urine formation is a complex process consisting of three main stages: glomerular filtration, tubular reabsorption, and tubular secretion. The kidneys regulate the glomerular filtration rate (GFR) through mechanisms like the juxtaglomerular apparatus, ensuring efficient waste excretion and fluid balance.
title: Urine Formation

evidence_snippets.text: The PCT is lined with simple cuboidal brush border epithelium, which significantly increases the surface area for reabsorption.
The loop of Henle is crucial for maintaining high osmolarity in the medullary interstitial fluid.
The DCT is responsible for the conditional reabsorption of Na+ and water.
It allows for the reabsorption of large quantities of water, producing concentrated urine.

summary: This section details the specific physiological roles of different segments of the renal tubule in urine formation. It explains how the proximal convoluted tubule, Henle's loop, distal convoluted tubule, and collecting duct selectively reabsorb water and solutes while secreting ions to maintain homeostasis.
title: Function of the Tubules

evidence_snippets.text: Mammals have the capacity to produce concentrated urine, a process facilitated by the Henle's loop and vasa recta.
The flow of filtrate in the two limbs of Henle's loop occurs in opposite directions, creating a counter current.
This gradient is mainly caused by NaCl and urea.

summary: This section explains the counter current mechanism in mammalian kidneys, which is facilitated by Henle's loop and vasa recta. This mechanism maintains an osmolarity gradient in the medullary interstitium, enabling the concentration of urine.
title: Mechanism of Concentration of the Filtrate

evidence_snippets.text: The functioning of the kidneys is efficiently monitored and regulated by hormonal feedback mechanisms involving the hypothalamus, JGA and... the heart.
ADH facilitates water reabsorption from latter parts of the tubule, thereby preventing diuresis.
Angiotensin II, being a powerful vasoconstrictor, increases the glomerular blood pressure and thereby GFR.
ANF can cause vasodilation (dilation of blood vessels) and thereby decrease the blood pressure.

summary: This section details the hormonal feedback mechanisms that monitor and regulate kidney function. It explains the regulatory roles of Antidiuretic Hormone (ADH) from the hypothalamus, the Renin-Angiotensin mechanism involving the juxtaglomerular apparatus, and Atrial Natriuretic Factor (ANF) from the heart.
title: Regulation of Kidney Function

evidence_snippets.text: The process of release of urine is called micturition and the neural mechanisms causing it is called the micturition reflex.
An adult human excretes, on an average, 1 to 1.5 litres of urine per day. The urine formed is a light yellow coloured watery fluid which is slightly acidic (pH 6.0)
Analysis of urine helps in clinical diagnosis of many metabolic discorders as well as malfunctioning of the kidney.

summary: This section describes the process of micturition and its underlying neural mechanism, the micturition reflex. It also outlines the physical and chemical characteristics of normal human urine and explains how clinical urine analysis is used to diagnose metabolic disorders.
title: Micturition

evidence_snippets.text: Other than the kidneys, lungs, liver and skin also help in the elimination of excretory wastes.
Our lungs remove large amounts of CO2 (approximately 200mL/minute) and also significant quantities of water every day.
Liver, the largest gland in our body, secretes bile-containing substances like bilirubin, biliverdin, cholesterol...
Sweat produced by the sweat glands is a watery fluid containing NaCl, small amounts of urea, lactic acid, etc.
Sebaceous glands eliminate certain substances like sterols, hydrocarbons and waxes through sebum.

summary: This section details the excretory roles of accessory organs other than the kidneys, including the lungs, liver, skin, and salivary glands. It explains how these organs eliminate specific metabolic wastes such as carbon dioxide, bile pigments, urea, and sebum.
title: Role of other Organs in Excretion

evidence_snippets.text: Malfunctioning of kidneys can lead to accumulation of urea in blood, a condition called uremia
In such patients, urea can be removed by a process called hemodialysis.
Kidney transplantation is the ultimate method in the correction of acute renal failures
Renal calculi refers to the formation of a stone or insoluble mass of crystallised salts
Glomerulonephritis is defined as the inflammation of the glomeruli of the kidney.

summary: This section outlines various disorders of the human excretory system, including uremia, renal calculi, and glomerulonephritis. It also describes clinical interventions such as hemodialysis and kidney transplantation used to manage renal failure.
title: Disorders of the Excretory System

evidence_snippets.text: Ammonia, urea, and uric acid are the major nitrogenous wastes excreted.
Each kidney has over a million tubular structures called nephrons. The nephron is the functional unit of the kidney
Urine formation involves three main processes: filtration, reabsorption, and secretion.
About 1200 ml of blood is filtered... to form 125 ml of filtrate... per minute (Glomerular Filtration Rate or GFR).
A counter current mechanism operates between the two limbs of the loop of Henle and those of vasa recta.
Urine is stored in the urinary bladder till a voluntary signal from the CNS carries out its release... known as micturition.

summary: This section provides a comprehensive summary of excretion and osmoregulation in animals, focusing on the human excretory system. It outlines the structure of the nephron, the three-step process of urine formation, the counter-current mechanism for concentrating filtrate, and the physiological process of micturition.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: Locomotion is defined as voluntary movements that result in a change of place or location
Amoeboid movement is achieved through the formation of pseudopodia, which are created by the streaming of protoplasm
Ciliary movement occurs in most internal tubular organs that are lined with ciliated epithelium.
Muscular movement is utilized for moving limbs, jaws, tongues, and other body parts, relying on the contractile property of muscles.

summary: This section introduces the distinction between movement and locomotion in living organisms. It details the four primary types of movement: amoeboid, ciliary, muscular, and flagellar, providing examples of where each occurs in humans and other organisms.
title: Types of Movement

evidence_snippets.text: Muscles possess four primary properties: excitability, contractility, extensibility, and elasticity.
The functional unit of muscle contraction is the sarcomere, defined as the segment of a myofibril located between two consecutive 'Z' lines.
The endoplasmic reticulum of the muscle fibre is called the sarcoplasmic reticulum and acts as a storehouse for calcium ions.

summary: This section introduces muscle tissue, classifying it into skeletal, visceral, and cardiac types based on location, appearance, and regulation. It further details the anatomical structure of skeletal muscle, focusing on the organization of muscle fibers, myofibrils, contractile proteins, and the sarcomere.
title: Muscle

evidence_snippets.text: An actin (thin) filament consists of two 'F' (filamentous) actin strands wound helically.
The myosin (thick) filament is a polymerized protein made of monomeric units called meromyosins.
The globular head functions as an active ATPase enzyme and contains specific binding sites for both ATP and actin.

summary: This section details the molecular structure of the contractile proteins actin and myosin, which constitute the thin and thick filaments of muscle fibers. It explains the composition of actin filaments, including F-actin, tropomyosin, and troponin, as well as the structure of myosin filaments composed of meromyosin subunits.
title: Structure of Contractile Proteins

evidence_snippets.text: muscle fiber contraction occurs when thin filaments slide over thick filaments, reducing the overall length of the sarcomere
The junction where the neuron meets the muscle fiber's sarcolemma is the neuromuscular junction
Using energy from ATP hydrolysis, the myosin head binds to these exposed sites to form a cross bridge.
accumulation of lactic acid due to the anaerobic breakdown of glycogen stored within them, a condition known as muscle fatigue.

summary: This section explains the mechanism of muscle contraction through the sliding filament theory, detailing neural activation, calcium release, and the cross-bridge cycle. It also covers the processes of muscle relaxation, muscle fatigue, and the classification of red and white muscle fibers.
title: Mechanism of Muscle Contraction

evidence_snippets.text: In humans, the skeletal system is composed of 206 bones and a few cartilages.
It is divided into two principal divisions: the axial skeleton and the appendicular skeleton.
Together, the thoracic vertebrae, ribs, and sternum form the rib cage.

summary: The human skeletal system consists of 206 bones and a few cartilages, organized into the axial and appendicular skeletons. It provides a protective and supportive framework that enables body movement through articulation with muscles.
title: Skeletal System

evidence_snippets.text: Joints are points of contact between bones, or between bones and cartilages.
Fibrous joints do not allow any movement.
In cartilaginous joints, the bones involved are joined together with the help of cartilages.
Synovial joints are characterised by the presence of a fluid filled synovial cavity

summary: This section introduces joints as points of contact between bones or between bones and cartilages that act as fulcrums for movement. It classifies joints into three major structural categories—fibrous, cartilaginous, and synovial—explaining their structural characteristics, degree of mobility, and anatomical examples.
title: Joints

evidence_snippets.text: Myasthenia gravis: Auto immune disorder affecting neuromuscular junction leading to fatigue, weakening and paralysis of skeletal muscle.
Muscular dystrophy: Progressive degeneration of skeletal muscle mostly due to genetic disorder.
Tetany: Rapid spasms (wild contractions) in muscle due to low Ca++ in body fluid.
Arthritis: Inflammation of joints.
Osteoporosis: Age-related disorder characterised by decreased bone mass and increased chances of fractures
Gout: Inflammation of joints due to accumulation of uric acid crystals.

summary: This section outlines the common disorders affecting the human muscular and skeletal systems. It details the causes and symptoms of conditions such as myasthenia gravis, muscular dystrophy, tetany, arthritis, osteoporosis, and gout.
title: Disorders of Muscular and Skeletal System

evidence_snippets.text: Coordination is the process through which two or more organs interact and complement the functions of one another.
The neural system provides an organized network of point-to-point connections for quick coordination
The neural system of all animals is composed of highly specialised cells called neurons

summary: This section introduces coordination and homeostasis, explaining how the neural and endocrine systems jointly integrate organ activities. It also describes the evolutionary complexity of neural organization in animals, ranging from simple networks in Hydra to highly developed systems in vertebrates.
title: Neural System

evidence_snippets.text: The human neural system is divided into two main parts: (i) the central neural system (CNS) and (ii) the peripheral neural system (PNS).
The nerve fibres of the PNS are of two types: (a) afferent fibres and (b) efferent fibres.
The PNS is divided into two divisions: the somatic neural system and the autonomic neural system.

summary: This section outlines the structural and functional divisions of the human neural system, categorizing it into the central neural system (CNS) and the peripheral neural system (PNS). It details the classification of PNS nerve fibres into afferent and efferent types, and further divides the PNS into the somatic, autonomic, and visceral nervous systems.
title: Human Neural System

evidence_snippets.text: A neuron is a microscopic structure composed of three major parts: the cell body, dendrites, and the axon.
Short fibres which branch repeatedly and project out of the cell body also contain Nissl’s granules and are called dendrites.
Axons transmit nerve impulses away from the cell body to a synapse or to a neuro-muscular junction.
Based on the number of axons and dendrites, neurons are divided into three types: (i) multipolar, (ii) bipolar, and (iii) unipolar.
Myelinated nerve fibres are enveloped with Schwann cells, which form a myelin sheath around the axon.

summary: This section details the structure of a neuron, highlighting its three main components: the cell body, dendrites, and axon. It classifies neurons into multipolar, bipolar, and unipolar types, and distinguishes between myelinated and unmyelinated nerve fibres.
title: Neuron as Structural and Functional Unit of Neural System

evidence_snippets.text: Neurons are excitable cells because their membranes are in a polarised state.
The electrical potential difference measured across this resting plasma membrane is defined as the resting potential.
The ionic gradients across the resting membrane are maintained by the active transport of ions by the sodium-potassium pump
This reversal of polarity is called depolarisation, and the resulting electrical potential difference is known as the action potential
This sequence repeats along the entire length of the axon to conduct the impulse.
K+ ions rapidly diffuse out of the cell, which restores the resting potential of the membrane.

summary: This section explains the physiological mechanisms underlying the generation, propagation, and restoration of a nerve impulse along an axonal membrane. It details how ionic gradients, active transport, and selective permeability changes create resting and action potentials.
title: Generation and Conduction of Nerve Impulse

evidence_snippets.text: Nerve impulses are transferred between neurons at junctions called synapses.
In electrical synapses, the pre- and post-synaptic membranes are in very close proximity, allowing electrical current to flow directly.
Chemical synapses use neurotransmitters to bridge the synaptic cleft between neurons.
When an action potential reaches the axon terminal, it triggers synaptic vesicles to move toward and fuse with the plasma membrane.

summary: This section describes how nerve impulses are transmitted between neurons at junctions called synapses. It distinguishes between electrical and chemical synapses and details the step-by-step mechanism of chemical synaptic transmission.
title: Transmission of Impulses

evidence_snippets.text: The brain serves as the central information processing and command center for the body.
The brain is protected by the skull and three layers of membranes called cranial meninges.
The human brain is divided into three major regions: the forebrain, the midbrain, and the hindbrain.

summary: This section introduces the human brain as the central information processing and command center of the body. It describes the protective cranial meninges and outlines the three primary anatomical divisions: the forebrain, midbrain, and hindbrain.
title: Central Neural System

evidence_snippets.text: The forebrain consists of the cerebrum, thalamus, and hypothalamus.
These hemispheres are connected by a tract of nerve fibres called the corpus callosum.
The layer of cells which covers the cerebral hemisphere is called the cerebral cortex
The cerebrum wraps around a structure called the thalamus
Another very important part of the brain called the hypothalamus lies at the base of the thalamus.
inner parts of the cerebral hemispheres and a group of associated deep structures... form... the limbic system.

summary: This section details the anatomy and functional regions of the forebrain, which consists of the cerebrum, thalamus, and hypothalamus. It explains the roles of the cerebral cortex, corpus callosum, and the limbic system in regulating sensory, motor, emotional, and autonomic activities.
title: Forebrain

evidence_snippets.text: The midbrain is located between the thalamus/hypothalamus of the forebrain and the pons of the hindbrain.
A canal called the cerebral aqueduct passes through the midbrain.
The dorsal portion of the midbrain consists mainly of four round swellings (lobes) called corpora quadrigemina.

summary: This section describes the anatomical location, structure, and function of the midbrain. It highlights key features such as the cerebral aqueduct and the corpora quadrigemina, as well as its role in integrating sensory inputs.
title: Midbrain

evidence_snippets.text: The hindbrain comprises the pons, cerebellum, and medulla (also called the medulla oblongata).
Three major regions make up the brain stem: the midbrain, pons, and medulla oblongata.

summary: This section details the components and functions of the hindbrain, which consists of the pons, cerebellum, and medulla oblongata. It also defines the brain stem, explaining its composition and its role in connecting the brain to the spinal cord.
title: Hindbrain

evidence_snippets.text: Endocrine glands lack ducts and are hence called ductless glands.
Hormones are non-nutrient chemicals which act as intercellular messengers and are produced in trace amounts.
The neural system and the endocrine system jointly coordinate and regulate the physiological functions in the body.

summary: This section introduces chemical coordination, defining endocrine glands as ductless glands and hormones as non-nutrient intercellular messengers. It highlights how the endocrine and neural systems jointly coordinate and regulate physiological functions.
title: Endocrine Glands and Hormones

evidence_snippets.text: The endocrine glands and hormone-producing diffused tissues/cells located in different parts of our body constitute the endocrine system.
The hypothalamus is the basal part of the diencephalon, forebrain, and it regulates a wide spectrum of body functions.
The pituitary gland is located in a bony cavity called sella turcica and is attached to the hypothalamus by a stalk.

summary: This section introduces the human endocrine system, focusing on the anatomical structure and regulatory roles of the hypothalamus and the pituitary gland. It details the various hormones secreted by these glands, their physiological functions, and clinical disorders arising from their imbalance, such as gigantism, acromegaly, and diabetes insipidus.
title: Human Endocrine System

evidence_snippets.text: The pineal gland is located on the dorsal side of the forebrain and secretes a hormone called melatonin.
Melatonin plays a critical role in the regulation of the body's 24-hour (diurnal) rhythm.

summary: This section introduces the pineal gland, detailing its location on the dorsal side of the forebrain and its secretion of the hormone melatonin. It highlights melatonin's essential role in regulating the body's 24-hour diurnal rhythm, sleep-wake cycle, body temperature, and other physiological processes.
title: The Pineal Gland

evidence_snippets.text: The thyroid gland consists of two lobes located on either side of the trachea, interconnected by a thin flap of connective tissue called the isthmus.
These cells synthesise two primary hormones: tetraiodothyronine or thyroxine ($T_4$) and triiodothyronine ($T_3$).
A deficiency of iodine in the diet leads to hypothyroidism and the enlargement of the thyroid gland, a condition commonly called goitre.
Hyperthyroidism occurs when the rate of synthesis and secretion of thyroid hormones increases to abnormally high levels
the thyroid gland secretes a protein hormone called thyrocalcitonin (TCT), which plays a key role in regulating blood calcium levels.

summary: This section covers the anatomy, hormones, and physiological functions of the thyroid gland, including thyroxine (T4), triiodothyronine (T3), and thyrocalcitonin (TCT). It also details the clinical disorders associated with thyroid dysfunction, such as hypothyroidism (goitre, cretinism) and hyperthyroidism (Graves' disease).
title: Thyroid Gland

evidence_snippets.text: In humans, four parathyroid glands are located on the back side of the thyroid gland, with one pair in each of the two thyroid lobes.
These glands secrete a peptide hormone called parathyroid hormone (PTH).
Parathyroid hormone (PTH) increases Ca2+ levels in the blood, making it a hypercalcemic hormone.
Along with thyrocalcitonin (TCT), PTH plays a significant role in maintaining calcium balance in the body.

summary: This section covers the anatomy and function of the parathyroid glands, which secrete parathyroid hormone (PTH). It explains how PTH acts as a hypercalcemic hormone to increase blood calcium levels and regulate calcium balance in the body.
title: Parathyroid Gland

evidence_snippets.text: The thymus gland is a lobular structure situated between the lungs, behind the sternum on the ventral side of the aorta.
It plays a major role in the development of the immune system by secreting peptide hormones called thymosins.
Thymosins are essential for the differentiation of T-lymphocytes, which provide cell-mediated immunity
promote the production of antibodies for humoral immunity.
The thymus degenerates in older individuals, leading to decreased thymosin production and a subsequent weakening of immune responses

summary: This section introduces the thymus gland, describing its anatomical location and its role in secreting thymosins. It explains how these hormones regulate cell-mediated and humoral immunity, and details how the degeneration of the thymus in older individuals leads to a weakened immune response.
title: Thymus

evidence_snippets.text: Each gland is composed of two types of tissues: the centrally located adrenal medulla and the outer adrenal cortex.
The adrenal medulla secretes two hormones: adrenaline (epinephrine) and noradrenaline (norepinephrine), collectively known as catecholamines.
Corticoids involved in carbohydrate metabolism are called glucocorticoids. In the human body, cortisol is the main glucocorticoid.
Corticoids which regulate the balance of water and electrolytes in our body are called mineralocorticoids. Aldosterone is the main mineralocorticoid

summary: This section explains the anatomical structure and physiological functions of the adrenal gland, which is divided into the adrenal medulla and adrenal cortex. It details the roles of catecholamines in the fight-or-flight response, and the regulation of metabolism, electrolyte balance, and development by cortical hormones.
title: Adrenal Gland

evidence_snippets.text: Pancreas is a composite gland which acts as both exocrine and endocrine gland.
The endocrine pancreas consists of 'Islets of Langerhans'.
Glucagon is a peptide hormone and plays an important role in maintaining the normal blood glucose levels.
Insulin is a peptide hormone which plays a major role in the regulation of glucose homeostasis.
Prolonged hyperglycemia leads to a complex disorder called diabetes mellitus

summary: This section covers the pancreas as a composite gland, focusing on its endocrine function mediated by the Islets of Langerhans. It details the antagonistic roles of glucagon and insulin in maintaining blood glucose homeostasis, and discusses diabetes mellitus as a consequence of prolonged hyperglycemia.
title: Pancreas

evidence_snippets.text: Testis performs dual functions as a primary sex organ as well as an endocrine gland.
Androgens play a major stimulatory role in the process of spermatogenesis (formation of spermatozoa).

summary: This section describes the structure and dual functions of the testis as both a primary sex organ and an endocrine gland. It details the secretion of androgens, primarily testosterone, by Leydig cells and outlines their diverse physiological roles in male development, spermatogenesis, and metabolism.
title: Testis

evidence_snippets.text: Ovary is the primary female sex organ which produces one ovum during each menstrual cycle.
Ovary also produces two groups of steroid hormones called estrogen and progesterone.
After ovulation, the ruptured follicle is converted to a structure called corpus luteum, which secretes mainly progesterone.
Estrogens produce wide ranging actions such as stimulation of growth and activities of female secondary sex organs...
Progesterone supports pregnancy.

summary: This section explains the structure and endocrine functions of the ovary, the primary female sex organ. It details the synthesis, secretion, and physiological roles of the ovarian steroid hormones, estrogen and progesterone, in regulating female reproduction and secondary sexual characteristics.
title: Ovary

evidence_snippets.text: The atrial wall of our heart secretes a very important peptide hormone called atrial natriuretic factor (ANF), which decreases blood pressure.
The juxtaglomerular cells of kidney produce a peptide hormone called erythropoietin which stimulates erythropoiesis (formation of RBC).
Endocrine cells present in different parts of the gastro-intestinal tract secrete four major peptide hormones
Several other non-endocrine tissues secrete hormones called growth factors.

summary: This section details the hormones secreted by organs and tissues other than the primary endocrine glands, including the heart, kidneys, gastrointestinal tract, and non-endocrine tissues. It explains how these hormones, such as atrial natriuretic factor, erythropoietin, gastro-intestinal hormones, and growth factors, regulate blood pressure, red blood cell production, digestion, and tissue repair.
title: Hormones of Heart, Kidney and Gastrointestinal Tract

evidence_snippets.text: Hormones produce their effects on target tissues by binding to specific proteins called hormone receptors
On the basis of their chemical nature, hormones can be divided into four main groups
This leads to the generation of second messengers, such as cyclic AMP, IP_3, or Ca^{++}.
They bind to receptors located inside the cell, often within the nucleus, to form a hormone-receptor complex.

summary: This section explains how hormones regulate physiological functions by binding to specific membrane-bound or intracellular receptors. It classifies hormones based on their chemical nature and details the distinct signaling pathways of water-soluble and lipid-soluble hormones, including the role of second messengers and direct gene regulation.
title: Mechanism of Hormone Action

evidence_snippets.text: Hormones are special chemicals that provide coordination, integration, and regulation in the human body.
The endocrine system includes the hypothalamus, pituitary, pineal, thyroid, adrenal, parathyroid, thymus, and gonads.

summary: This section provides a comprehensive summary of the human endocrine system, detailing the major endocrine glands and their respective hormones. It outlines how these hormones regulate vital physiological processes, including metabolism, growth, calcium homeostasis, glucose levels, and reproduction.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: In Linnaeus' time a Two Kingdom system of classification with Plantae and Animalia kingdoms was developed
R.H. Whittaker (1969) proposed a Five Kingdom Classification.
All prokaryotic organisms were grouped together under Kingdom Monera
the unicellular eukaryotic organisms were placed in Kingdom Protista.
the fungi were placed in a separate kingdom – Kingdom Fungi.

summary: This section traces the historical development of biological classification systems, from Aristotle's early morphological groupings and Linnaeus's Two Kingdom system to R.H. Whittaker's Five Kingdom Classification. It highlights how changing criteria—such as cell structure, body organization, mode of nutrition, and evolutionary relationships—led to more accurate taxonomic groupings.
title: Biological Classification

evidence_snippets.text: Bacteria are the sole members of the Kingdom Monera.
Archaebacteria differ from other bacteria in having a different cell wall structure
There are thousands of different eubacteria or ‘true bacteria’.
Heterotrophic bacteria are most abundant in nature.
The Mycoplasma are organisms that completely lack a cell wall.

summary: This section introduces Kingdom Monera, which solely consists of bacteria, the most abundant microorganisms on Earth. It details their classification based on shape, metabolic diversity, and divides them into Archaebacteria, Eubacteria, and Heterotrophic bacteria, while also covering Mycoplasma and bacterial reproduction.
title: Kingdom Monera

evidence_snippets.text: All single-celled eukaryotes are placed under Protista, but the boundaries of this kingdom are not well defined.
In diatoms the cell walls form two thin overlapping shells, which fit together as in a soap box. The walls are embedded with silica
Most of them have two flagella; one lies longitudinally and the other transversely in a furrow between the wall plates.

summary: This section introduces Kingdom Protista, which encompasses all single-celled eukaryotic organisms. It details the classification, structural features, and ecological roles of its five major groups: Chrysophytes, Dinoflagellates, Euglenoids, Slime moulds, and Protozoans.
title: Kingdom Protista

evidence_snippets.text: The fungi constitute a unique kingdom of heterotrophic organisms.
Their bodies consist of long, slender thread-like structures called hyphae. The network of hyphae is known as mycelium.
The sexual cycle involves the following three steps: (i) Fusion of protoplasms... called plasmogamy. (ii) Fusion of two nuclei called karyogamy.

summary: This section covers Kingdom Fungi, detailing their heterotrophic nutrition, filamentous morphology, and cell walls composed of chitin. It explains their reproductive strategies, including the three-step sexual cycle (plasmogamy, karyogamy, and meiosis), and classifies them into Phycomycetes, Ascomycetes, Basidiomycetes, and Deuteromycetes.
title: Kingdom Fungi

evidence_snippets.text: Kingdom Plantae includes all eukaryotic chlorophyll-containing organisms commonly called plants.
The life cycle of plants is characterized by two distinct phases: the diploid sporophytic phase and the haploid gametophytic phase.

summary: This section introduces Kingdom Plantae, describing its members as eukaryotic, chlorophyll-containing, and primarily autotrophic organisms with cellulose cell walls. It also outlines the plant life cycle, which is characterized by the alternation of generations between diploid sporophytic and haploid gametophytic phases.
title: Kingdom Plantae

evidence_snippets.text: Kingdom Animalia is characterized by heterotrophic eukaryotic organisms that are multicellular and lack cell walls.
Their mode of nutrition is holozoic, involving the ingestion of food.

summary: This section outlines the defining characteristics of Kingdom Animalia, which comprises multicellular, heterotrophic, eukaryotic organisms that lack cell walls. It details their holozoic mode of nutrition, internal digestion, growth patterns, and reproductive processes.
title: Kingdom Animalia

evidence_snippets.text: In Whittaker's five-kingdom classification, there is no mention of lichens or acellular organisms like viruses, viroids, and prions.
Viruses are not considered truly 'living' because they do not possess a cell structure.
Viroids were discovered by T.O. Diener in 1971 as a new infectious agent smaller than viruses.
Prions are agents consisting of abnormally folded proteins that cause certain infectious neurological diseases.
Lichens are symbiotic associations, representing mutually useful relationships between algae and fungi.

summary: This section covers acellular infectious agents—viruses, viroids, and prions—as well as symbiotic lichens, which are excluded from Whittaker's five-kingdom classification. It details their structural characteristics, historical discoveries, associated diseases, and ecological roles.
title: Viruses, Viroids, Prions and Lichens

evidence_snippets.text: Biological classification was first proposed by Aristotle based on simple morphological characters.
Linnaeus later classified organisms into two kingdoms – Plantae and Animalia.
Whittaker proposed an elaborate five kingdom classification – Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae and Animalia

summary: This section summarizes the history and development of biological classification systems, from Aristotle's early morphological classification to Linnaeus's two-kingdom system and Whittaker's five-kingdom classification. It outlines the defining characteristics of the five kingdoms: Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae, and Animalia.
title: Summary of Biological Classification

evidence_snippets.text: The current scope of Kingdom Plantae, often referred to as the 'plant kingdom', includes Algae, Bryophytes, Pteridophytes, Gymnosperms, and Angiosperms.
These systems relied on gross superficial morphological characters such as habit, color, number, and shape of leaves.
These are based on natural affinities among organisms, considering not just external features but also internal features
These systems are based on evolutionary relationships between various organisms
Numerical Taxonomy: This is carried out using computers and is based on all observable characteristics.

summary: This section outlines the evolution of the classification of Kingdom Plantae, highlighting the transition from artificial and natural systems to modern phylogenetic systems. It also introduces modern taxonomic methods, including numerical taxonomy, cytotaxonomy, and chemotaxonomy, which help resolve classification ambiguities.
title: Plant Kingdom

evidence_snippets.text: Algae are chlorophyll-bearing, simple, thalloid, autotrophic, and largely aquatic organisms, found in both fresh water and marine environments.
The form and size of algae are highly variable. They can be colonial, such as Volvox, or filamentous, such as Ulothrix and Spirogyra.
Algae reproduce through vegetative, asexual, and sexual methods
At least a half of the total carbon dioxide fixation on earth is carried out by algae through photosynthesis.

summary: This section introduces algae, detailing their general characteristics, habitats, and diverse morphological forms. It explains their vegetative, asexual, and sexual reproductive methods, highlights their ecological and commercial importance, and outlines their classification into three main classes.
title: Algae

evidence_snippets.text: The members of chlorophyceae are commonly called green algae.
Most of the members have one or more storage bodies called pyrenoids located in the chloroplasts.
Green algae usually have a rigid cell wall made of an inner layer of cellulose and an outer layer of pectose.
Asexual reproduction is by flagellated zoospores produced in zoosporangia.

summary: This section covers Chlorophyceae, commonly known as green algae, detailing their cellular structure, pigmentation, and storage bodies. It also explains their cell wall composition, modes of vegetative, asexual, and sexual reproduction, and provides common examples.
title: Chlorophyceae

evidence_snippets.text: The members of phaeophyceae or brown algae are found primarily in marine habitats.
They vary in colour from olive green to various shades of brown depending upon the amount of the xanthophyll pigment, fucoxanthin present in them.
Food is stored as complex carbohydrates, which may be in the form of laminarin or mannitol.
The vegetative cells have a cellulosic wall usually covered on the outside by a gelatinous coating of algin.
The plant body is usually attached to the substratum by a holdfast, and has a stalk, the stipe and leaf like photosynthetic organ – the frond.
Asexual reproduction in most brown algae is by biflagellate zoospores that are pear-shaped and have two unequal laterally attached flagella.

summary: This section covers Phaeophyceae, commonly known as brown algae, detailing their marine habitats, morphology, pigmentation, and structural components like the holdfast, stipe, and frond. It also explains their food storage mechanisms, cell wall composition, and methods of vegetative, asexual, and sexual reproduction.
title: Phaeophyceae

evidence_snippets.text: The members of rhodophyceae are commonly called red algae because of the predominance of the red pigment, r-phycoerythrin in their body.
The food is stored as floridean starch which is very similar to amylopectin and glycogen in structure.
They reproduce asexually by non-motile spores and sexually by non-motile gametes. Sexual reproduction is oogamous

summary: This section introduces the class Rhodophyceae, commonly known as red algae, detailing their morphology, pigmentation, and habitat. It explains their reproductive methods, including vegetative fragmentation, non-motile asexual spores, and oogamous sexual reproduction, alongside key examples.
title: Rhodophyceae

evidence_snippets.text: Bryophytes are also called amphibians of the plant kingdom because these plants can live in soil but are dependent on water for sexual reproduction.
The main plant body of the bryophyte is haploid. It produces gametes, hence is called a gametophyte.
The sporophyte is not free-living but attached to the photosynthetic gametophyte and derives nourishment from it.
Species of Sphagnum, a moss, provide peat that have long been used as fuel

summary: This section introduces Bryophytes, known as the amphibians of the plant kingdom, which include liverworts and mosses. It describes their haploid gametophyte body, multicellular reproductive organs, and the dependent diploid sporophyte phase. Additionally, it highlights their ecological significance in plant succession and soil erosion prevention.
title: Bryophytes

evidence_snippets.text: The plant body of a liverwort is thalloid, e.g., Marchantia.
Asexual reproduction in liverworts takes place by fragmentation of thalli, or by the formation of specialised structures called gemmae
The sporophyte is differentiated into a foot, seta and capsule.

summary: This section describes the habitat, morphology, and reproductive mechanisms of liverworts, a group of bryophytes. It details their thalloid structure, asexual reproduction via gemmae, and sexual reproduction involving a sporophyte differentiated into a foot, seta, and capsule.
title: Liverworts

evidence_snippets.text: The predominant stage of the life cycle of a moss is the gametophyte which consists of two stages.
The first stage is the protonema stage, which develops directly from a spore.
The second stage is the leafy stage, which develops from the secondary protonema as a lateral bud.
Vegetative reproduction in mosses is by fragmentation and budding in the secondary protonema.
After fertilisation, the zygote develops into a sporophyte, consisting of a foot, seta and capsule.

summary: This section details the life cycle, structural stages, and reproductive mechanisms of mosses. It explains the two gametophytic phases—the protonema and leafy stages—as well as vegetative and sexual reproduction, including sporophyte structure and spore dispersal.
title: Mosses

evidence_snippets.text: Evolutionarily, they are the first terrestrial plants to possess vascular tissues – xylem and phloem.
in pteridophytes, the main plant body is a sporophyte which is differentiated into true root, stem and leaves
The spores germinate to give rise to inconspicuous, small but multicellular, free-living, mostly photosynthetic thalloid gametophytes called prothallus.
Genera like Selaginella and Salvinia which produce two kinds of spores, macro (large) and micro (small) spores, are known as heterosporous.
The development of the zygotes into young embryos take place within the female gametophytes. This event is a precursor to the seed habit

summary: This section introduces Pteridophytes, the first terrestrial plants with vascular tissues, detailing their morphology, habitat, and life cycle. It explains the alternation of generations between the dominant sporophyte and the gametophyte (prothallus), as well as the evolutionary significance of heterospory and the seed habit.
title: Pteridophytes

evidence_snippets.text: The gymnosperms (gymnos : naked, sperma : seeds) are plants in which the ovules are not enclosed by any ovary wall and remain exposed
Roots in some genera have fungal association in the form of mycorrhiza (Pinus), while in some others (Cycas) small specialised roots
sporophylls which are arranged spirally along an axis to form lax or compact strobili or cones.
Unlike bryophytes and pteridophytes, in gymnosperms, the male and the female gametophytes do not have an independent free-living existence.
Following fertilisation, zygote develops into an embryo and the ovules into seeds. These seeds are not covered.

summary: This section covers gymnosperms, which are characterized by naked seeds not enclosed by an ovary wall. It details their morphological adaptations, heterosporous reproduction involving male and female strobili, and their unique gametophyte development and fertilization processes.
title: Gymnosperms

evidence_snippets.text: In the angiosperms or flowering plants, the pollen grains and ovules are developed in specialised structures called flowers.
In angiosperms, the seeds are enclosed in fruits.
Dicotyledons are characterized by seeds having two cotyledons, while monocotyledons have only one cotyledon.

summary: This section introduces angiosperms, or flowering plants, which are characterized by having seeds enclosed within fruits and reproductive structures developed in flowers. It also outlines their classification into two major classes, dicotyledons and monocotyledons, based on cotyledon number.
title: Angiosperms

evidence_snippets.text: Plant kingdom includes algae, bryophytes, pteridophytes, gymnosperms and angiosperms.
Algae are chlorophyll-bearing simple, thalloid, autotrophic and largely aquatic organisms.
Bryophytes are plants which can live in soil but are dependent on water for sexual reproduction.

summary: This section provides a summary of the Plant Kingdom, focusing on the characteristics and classification of algae and bryophytes. It outlines the classification of algae into three main classes and describes the structural and reproductive features of bryophytes.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: fundamental features common to various individuals in relation to the arrangement of cells, body symmetry, nature of coelom
Though all members of Animalia are multicellular, they do not all exhibit the same pattern of cell organisation.
Animals are categorized by their body symmetry.
Animals are classified by the number of embryonic layers formed during development.
The presence or absence of a body cavity between the body wall and the gut wall, known as the coelom, is a vital classification feature.

summary: This section outlines the fundamental structural and anatomical features used to classify the animal kingdom. These key criteria include levels of cellular organization, body symmetry, embryonic germ layers, the presence or absence of a coelom, body segmentation, and the presence of a notochord.
title: Basis of Classification

evidence_snippets.text: The broad classification of Animalia, based on common fundamental features as mentioned in the preceding sections, is given in Figure 4.4.
This classification categorizes animals based on levels of organization (cellular vs tissue/organ/organ system), symmetry, and body cavity/coelom.

summary: This section provides an overview of the broad classification of Kingdom Animalia based on fundamental features. It outlines how animals are categorized into different phyla using levels of organization, body symmetry, and the presence or absence of a coelom.
title: Classification of Animals

evidence_snippets.text: Members of this phylum are commonly known as sponges. They are generally marine and mostly asymmetrical animals.
Sponges have a water transport or canal system. Water enters through minute pores (ostia) into a central cavity, spongocoel
Choanocytes or collar cells line the spongocoel and the canals.
The body is supported by a skeleton made up of spicules or spongin fibres.

summary: This section introduces Phylum Porifera, commonly known as sponges, detailing their cellular level of organization, asymmetrical body plan, and unique water canal system. It also describes their structural support via spicules or spongin, hermaphroditic reproduction, and key representative examples like Sycon and Spongilla.
title: Phylum - Porifera

evidence_snippets.text: They are aquatic, mostly marine, sessile or free-swimming, radially symmetrical animals.
Cnidarians exhibit two basic body forms called polyp and medusa.
Those cnidarians which exist in both forms exhibit alternation of generations (Metagenesis)

summary: This section introduces Phylum Coelenterata (Cnidaria), detailing their general characteristics, tissue-level organization, and specialized stinging cells called cnidoblasts. It also explains their two primary body forms, polyp and medusa, and the phenomenon of metagenesis.
title: Phylum - Coelenterata (Cnidaria)

evidence_snippets.text: Ctenophores, commonly known as sea walnuts or comb jellies are exclusively marine, radially symmetrical, diploblastic organisms
The body bears eight external rows of ciliated comb plates, which help in locomotion.
Bioluminescence (the property of a living organism to emit light) is well-marked in ctenophores.

summary: This section introduces Phylum Ctenophora, commonly known as sea walnuts or comb jellies. It details their key biological characteristics, including radial symmetry, diploblastic tissue organization, ciliated comb plates for locomotion, bioluminescence, and sexual reproduction.
title: Phylum - Ctenophora

evidence_snippets.text: They have dorso-ventrally flattened body, hence are called flatworms.
Specialised cells called flame cells help in osmoregulation and excretion.
Some members like Planaria possess high regeneration capacity.

summary: This section introduces Phylum Platyhelminthes, commonly known as flatworms, detailing their anatomical features such as bilateral symmetry, triploblastic nature, and acoelomate body plan. It highlights specialized structures like flame cells for excretion, parasitic adaptations, and reproductive characteristics, with examples like Taenia and Fasciola.
title: Phylum - Platyhelminthes

evidence_snippets.text: The body of the aschelminthes is circular in cross-section, which gives them the common name roundworms
In terms of body plan, they are bilaterally symmetrical, triploblastic, and pseudocoelomate animals.
The digestive system features a complete alimentary canal equipped with a well-developed muscular pharynx.
Aschelminthes are dioecious, meaning sexes are separate and males and females are distinct.

summary: This section introduces Phylum Aschelminthes, commonly known as roundworms, detailing their structural characteristics, anatomy, and physiology. It highlights their pseudocoelomate body plan, complete digestive tract, sexual dimorphism, and key representative species.
title: Phylum - Aschelminthes

evidence_snippets.text: A defining feature is that their body surface is distinctly marked out into segments or metameres; this characteristic gives the phylum its name
Aquatic annelids like Nereis have lateral appendages called parapodia, which assist in swimming.
The circulatory system in annelids is of the closed type.
Excretion and osmoregulation are handled by structures called nephridia (singular: nephridium).

summary: This section introduces Phylum Annelida, describing their metamerically segmented body plan, organ-system level of organization, and coelomate nature. It details their physiological features, including locomotion via muscles and parapodia, a closed circulatory system, nephridia for excretion, and sexual reproduction.
title: Phylum - Annelida

evidence_snippets.text: Phylum Arthropoda is the largest phylum of Animalia, encompassing over two-thirds of all named species on Earth
The body is protected by a chitinous exoskeleton and is divided into three regions: head, thorax, and abdomen.
They are characterized by jointed appendages (arthros meaning joint, poda meaning appendages).
The circulatory system is of the open type.
Excretion occurs through malpighian tubules.

summary: This section introduces Phylum Arthropoda, the largest phylum in the animal kingdom, detailing its structural features such as a chitinous exoskeleton and jointed appendages. It also covers their physiological systems, including open circulation and malpighian tubules, along with key examples of ecological and economic importance.
title: Phylum - Arthropoda

evidence_snippets.text: Mollusca is the second largest animal phylum
The body is typically covered by a calcareous shell and is unsegmented, with a distinct head, muscular foot, and visceral hump.
A soft and spongy layer of skin forms a mantle over the visceral hump.
The mouth contains a file-like rasping organ for feeding called a radula.
They are usually dioecious and oviparous with indirect development.

summary: This section introduces Phylum Mollusca, the second largest animal phylum, detailing its general body structure, physiological features, and reproductive characteristics. Key anatomical structures such as the mantle, visceral hump, muscular foot, and radula are explained alongside representative examples like Pila and Octopus.
title: Phylum - Mollusca

evidence_snippets.text: Echinoderms possess an endoskeleton of calcareous ossicles, which gives them their name (Echinodermata means spiny bodied
adults are radially symmetrical, while larvae are bilaterally symmetrical.
The most distinctive feature is the water vascular system, which aids in locomotion, capture and transport of food, and respiration.
Development is indirect, involving a free-swimming larva.

summary: This section introduces Phylum Echinodermata, highlighting their spiny-skinned calcareous endoskeleton and unique water vascular system. It details their distinctive developmental symmetry, physiology, reproductive characteristics, and representative examples.
title: Phylum - Echinodermata

evidence_snippets.text: Hemichordata was previously considered a sub-phylum under Chordata but is now classified as a separate phylum under non-chordata.
They possess a rudimentary structure in the collar region called a stomochord, which is similar to a notochord.
The body is cylindrical and composed of an anterior proboscis, a collar, and a long trunk
the excretory organ is the proboscis gland.

summary: This section introduces the phylum Hemichordata, describing its classification, anatomical structure, and physiological systems. It highlights key features such as the stomochord, a tripartite body division, and representative examples like Balanoglossus.
title: Phylum - Hemichordata

evidence_snippets.text: Animals belonging to phylum Chordata are fundamentally characterised by the presence of a notochord
Subphyla Urochordata and Cephalochordata are often referred to as protochordates
The notochord is replaced by a cartilaginous or bony vertebral column in the adult.
Division Agnatha (lacks jaw) and Gnathostomata (bears jaw).

summary: This section introduces Phylum Chordata, detailing its fundamental characteristics such as the presence of a notochord, dorsal hollow nerve cord, and pharyngeal gill slits. It compares chordates with non-chordates and outlines the classification of Chordata into subphyla, focusing on protochordates and the detailed division of subphylum Vertebrata.
title: Phylum - Chordata

evidence_snippets.text: All living members of the class Cyclostomata are ectoparasites on some fishes.
Cyclostomes have a sucking and circular mouth without jaws
Cranium and vertebral column are cartilaginous.
Cyclostomes are marine but migrate for spawning to fresh water.

summary: This section introduces the class Cyclostomata, detailing their characteristics as jawless, ectoparasitic marine vertebrates. It covers their unique anatomical features, such as a cartilaginous skeleton and lack of scales, as well as their migratory spawning life cycle.
title: Class - Cyclostomata

evidence_snippets.text: They are marine animals with streamlined body and have cartilaginous endoskeleton
Gill slits are separate and without operculum (gill cover).
The skin is tough, containing minute placoid scales.
Due to the absence of air bladder, they have to swim constantly to avoid sinking.
They are cold-blooded (poikilothermous) animals, i.e., they lack the capacity to regulate their body temperature.

summary: This section details Class Chondrichthyes, which consists of marine fishes with a cartilaginous endoskeleton. It covers their unique anatomical features, such as placoid scales and the lack of an operculum or air bladder, as well as their physiological and reproductive characteristics.
title: Class - Chondrichthyes

evidence_snippets.text: It includes both marine and fresh water fishes with bony endoskeleton.
They have four pairs of gills which are covered by an operculum on each side.
Air bladder is present which regulates buoyancy.
Skin is covered with cycloid/ctenoid scales.

summary: This section details the anatomical and physiological characteristics of Class Osteichthyes, commonly known as bony fishes. It covers their habitat, respiration via operculum-covered gills, buoyancy regulation using an air bladder, and reproductive traits, alongside common examples.
title: Class - Osteichthyes

evidence_snippets.text: amphibians can live in aquatic as well as terrestrial habitats
Alimentary canal, urinary and reproductive tracts open into a common chamber called cloaca
The heart is three-chambered (two auricles and one ventricle).

summary: This section details the biological characteristics of Class Amphibia, highlighting their adaptation to both aquatic and terrestrial habitats. It covers their anatomical features, organ systems, and reproductive traits, along with representative examples.
title: Class - Amphibia

evidence_snippets.text: The class name refers to their creeping or crawling mode of locomotion (Latin, repere or reptum, to creep or crawl).
Heart is usually three-chambered, but four-chambered in crocodiles.
They are mostly terrestrial animals and their body is covered by dry and cornified skin, epidermal scales or scutes.

summary: This section outlines the defining characteristics of Class Reptilia, including their creeping locomotion, dry cornified skin with scales, and typically three-chambered heart. It also covers their reproductive features, poikilothermic nature, and provides key examples of reptiles.
title: Class - Reptilia

evidence_snippets.text: The characteristic features of Aves (birds) are the presence of feathers and most of them can fly
Endoskeleton is fully ossified (bony) and the long bones are hollow with air cavities (pneumatic).
The digestive tract of birds has additional chambers, the crop and gizzard.
They are warm-blooded (homoiothermic) animals, i.e., they are able to maintain a constant body temperature.
Respiration is by lungs. Air sacs connected to lungs supplement respiration.

summary: This section outlines the defining characteristics of Class Aves (birds), focusing on their adaptations for flight such as feathers, wings, and pneumatic bones. It also details their specialized digestive, circulatory, respiratory, and reproductive systems, accompanied by representative examples.
title: Class - Aves

evidence_snippets.text: The most unique mammalian characteristic is the presence of milk producing glands (mammary glands) by which the young ones are nourished.
The skin of mammals is unique in possessing hair.
They are homoiothermous.
They are viviparous with few exceptions and development is direct.

summary: This section outlines the defining characteristics of Class Mammalia, emphasizing unique features such as mammary glands, hair, and external ears. It also provides examples of mammalian species and includes a comprehensive summary table of the salient features of all animal phyla.
title: Class - Mammalia

evidence_snippets.text: The animal kingdom is broadly classified based on basic fundamental features such as the level of organisation
Porifera includes multicellular animals with a cellular level of organisation and characteristic flagellated choanocytes.
Phylum Chordata includes animals that possess a notochord at some stage of their life cycle
Vertebrates are divided into Agnatha (jawless, e.g., Class Cyclostomata) and Gnathostomata (jawed).
Tetrapoda includes classes Amphibia, Reptilia, Aves, and Mammalia, all typically possessing two pairs of limbs.

summary: This section provides a concise overview of animal classification, highlighting the fundamental features used to categorize the animal kingdom. It summarizes the distinguishing characteristics of major non-chordate phyla and outlines the subdivisions of Phylum Chordata, including the classes of Tetrapoda.
title: Summary of Animal Classification

evidence_snippets.text: Morphology refers to the study of these external structures.
The underground part of the plant is known as the root system, while the portion that grows above the ground is called the shoot system

summary: This section introduces plant morphology as the study of external structures in higher plants, specifically angiosperms. It outlines the division of a typical flowering plant into the underground root system and the above-ground shoot system.
title: Morphology of Flowering Plants

evidence_snippets.text: The primary roots and its branches constitute the tap root system, as seen in the mustard plant.
These roots originate from the base of the stem and constitute the fibrous root system, as seen in the wheat plant.
roots arise from parts of the plant other than the radicle and are called adventitious roots
The main functions of the root system are absorption of water and minerals from the soil, providing a proper anchorage
