Oral administration of small doses of either progestogens or progestogen-estrogen combinations is used by females in the form of tablets
Surgical methods, also called sterilisation, are terminal methods to prevent any more pregnancies by blocking gamete transport.

summary: This section explores the causes and consequences of the global and national population explosion and outlines strategies for population control. It details the characteristics of an ideal contraceptive and classifies various birth control methods, including natural, barrier, intra-uterine, oral, injectable, emergency, and surgical techniques.
title: Population Stabilisation and Birth Control

evidence_snippets.text: Intentional or voluntary termination of pregnancy before full term is called medical termination of pregnancy (MTP) or induced abortion.
The Medical Termination of Pregnancy (Amendment) Act, 2017 was enacted to reduce illegal abortions and maternal mortality.
MTPs are considered relatively safe during the first trimester (up to 12 weeks of pregnancy).
misuse of amniocentesis to determine the sex of the unborn child; if the foetus is found to be female, it is often followed by MTP

summary: This section defines Medical Termination of Pregnancy (MTP), also known as induced abortion, and discusses its global context and safety timelines. It details the legal framework of the MTP Amendment Act in India, highlighting the conditions under which it is permitted. Additionally, it addresses the critical issues of illegal abortions, the misuse of amniocentesis for female foeticide, and the preventive measures required to protect maternal health.
title: Medical Termination of Pregnancy (MTP)

evidence_snippets.text: Infections or diseases that are transmitted through sexual intercourse are collectively referred to as sexually transmitted infections (STI)
hepatitis-B, genital herpes, and HIV infections are currently not curable.
If left untreated, these infections can lead to severe complications such as pelvic inflammatory diseases (PID)

summary: This section defines sexually transmitted infections (STIs) and outlines their common examples, modes of transmission, and curability. It also discusses the symptoms, severe complications of untreated STIs, and key preventive measures to avoid infection.
title: Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs)

evidence_snippets.text: Infertility is defined as the inability of a couple to produce children despite unprotected sexual co-habitation.
When corrective treatments are not possible, couples can be assisted through assisted reproductive technologies (ART).
in vitro fertilisation (IVF), where fertilisation occurs outside the body in simulated laboratory conditions, followed by embryo transfer (ET).

summary: This section defines infertility and explores various Assisted Reproductive Technologies (ART) used to treat it. It details procedures such as IVF, ET, ZIFT, IUT, GIFT, ICSI, and artificial insemination, while also discussing social aspects and legal adoption.
title: Infertility

evidence_snippets.text: Reproductive health refers to total well-being in physical, emotional, behavioural, and social aspects of reproduction.
propagation of various contraceptive methods (natural, barrier, IUDs, pills, etc.).
Medical Termination of Pregnancy (MTP) is legal in India and is performed to terminate unwanted pregnancies
Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs) can lead to complications like Pelvic Inflammatory Diseases (PIDs), stillbirths, and infertility.
Infertility is defined as the inability to conceive or produce children even after 2 years of unprotected sexual cohabitation.
Assisted Reproductive Technologies (ART) like IVF, ZIFT, GIFT, ICSI, and AI are available to help infertile couples

summary: This section summarizes reproductive health, emphasizing India's national strategies, population control through contraceptive methods, and the legality of Medical Termination of Pregnancy (MTP). It also highlights the complications of Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs) and the application of Assisted Reproductive Technologies (ART) to address infertility.
title: Summary of Reproductive Health

evidence_snippets.text: Genetics is the branch of biology that scientifically investigates the inheritance and variation of characters from parents to offspring.
Inheritance is defined as the process by which characters are passed on from parent to progeny; it serves as the fundamental basis of heredity.
Variation refers to the degree by which progeny differ from their parents.

summary: This section introduces genetics as the scientific study of inheritance and variation. It defines key terms such as inheritance and variation, and provides a historical perspective on how humans exploited natural variation through artificial selection.
title: Principles of Inheritance and Variation

evidence_snippets.text: Gregor Mendel conducted hybridisation experiments on garden peas for seven years (1856-1863) and proposed the laws of inheritance
A true-breeding line is one that, having undergone continuous self-pollination, shows the stable trait inheritance
Mendel selected 14 true-breeding pea plant varieties, as pairs which were similar except for one character with contrasting traits.

summary: This section introduces Gregor Mendel's pioneering experimental approach to understanding inheritance using hybridization experiments on garden peas. It details his use of statistical analysis, selection of fourteen true-breeding pea plant varieties, and the study of seven pairs of contrasting traits.
title: Mendel’s Laws of Inheritance

evidence_snippets.text: Mendel crossed tall and dwarf pea plants to study the inheritance of one gene
Genes which code for a pair of contrasting traits are known as alleles
TT and tt are called the genotype of the plant while the descriptive terms tall and dwarf are the phenotype.
The Punnett Square... is a graphical tool used to calculate the probability of all possible genotypes in a genetic cross.

summary: This section explains the inheritance of a single gene through Mendel's monohybrid crosses with pea plants. It introduces fundamental genetic concepts including genes, alleles, genotypes, phenotypes, Punnett squares, test crosses, and Mendel's laws of inheritance.
title: Inheritance of One Gene

evidence_snippets.text: The Law of Dominance states that: (i) traits are controlled by discrete units called factors; (ii) these factors occur in pairs
in a pair of dissimilar factors, one member (dominant) masks the expression of the other (recessive).
This law explains why only one parental trait appears in the $F_1$ generation and why both traits appear in a $3:1$ ratio in the $F_2$ generation.

summary: This section introduces Mendel's Law of Dominance, which explains how traits are controlled by paired discrete units called factors. It details how a dominant factor masks a recessive factor in a dissimilar pair, explaining the phenotypic outcomes of the F1 and F2 generations.
title: Law of Dominance

evidence_snippets.text: During gamete formation, the factors or alleles of a pair segregate from each other so that each gamete receives only one of the two factors.
Homozygous parents produce identical gametes, while heterozygous parents produce two types of gametes in equal proportions

summary: This section explains Mendel's Law of Segregation, which states that allele pairs separate during gamete formation so that each gamete carries only one allele. It emphasizes that alleles do not blend and describes how homozygous and heterozygous parents produce gametes.
title: Law of Segregation

evidence_snippets.text: the F1 generation had a phenotype that did not resemble either of the two parents and was in between the two.
the phenotypic ratio changed from the 3:1 dominant:recessive ratio to 1:2:1.
The unmodified (functioning) allele... is the dominant allele, while the modified allele is the recessive allele.

summary: This section explains incomplete dominance, where the F1 generation exhibits an intermediate phenotype between those of the two parents, as demonstrated by flower color inheritance in snapdragons. It also provides a molecular explanation of dominance, describing how mutations modify alleles to produce functional, non-functional, or no enzymes, thereby determining dominant and recessive traits.
title: Incomplete Dominance

evidence_snippets.text: In co-dominance, the $F_1$ generation resembles both parents.
ABO blood grouping is also an example of multiple alleles, where more than two alleles govern the same character.
dominance is not an autonomous feature of a gene; it depends on the gene product and the specific phenotype chosen for examination.

summary: This section explains co-dominance using the human ABO blood grouping system, which also serves as an example of multiple alleles. It further discusses how dominance is a relative feature rather than an autonomous property of a gene, depending on the specific phenotype under examination.
title: Co-dominance

evidence_snippets.text: Mendel crossed pea plants differing in two characters, such as seed colour (yellow vs. green) and seed shape (round vs. wrinkled).
When the F_1 plants were self-hybridised, Mendel observed that the traits segregated independently.
This independent segregation of traits results in a combined phenotypic ratio of 9:3:3:1 for the four possible combinations

summary: This section explains the inheritance of two genes through Mendel's dihybrid crosses in pea plants, using seed color and shape as examples. It demonstrates how traits segregate independently in the F2 generation, resulting in a characteristic 9:3:3:1 phenotypic ratio.
title: Inheritance of Two Genes

evidence_snippets.text: The ratio of 9:3:3:1 observed in dihybrid crosses can be derived as a combination series of two 3:1 monohybrid ratios.
segregation of one pair of characters is independent of the other pair of characters
Thus there are four genotypes of gametes: RY, Ry, rY and ry, each with a frequency of 25 per cent

summary: This section explains Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment, which states that the segregation of one pair of traits is independent of another during gamete formation. It demonstrates how the 9:3:3:1 dihybrid ratio is derived from two independent 3:1 monohybrid ratios and details the genetic mechanism of gamete production in dihybrids.
title: Law of Independent Assortment

evidence_snippets.text: Sutto n unite d the knowledg e of chromosoma l segregatio n with Men delia n principle s an d calle d it the chromosoma l theor y of inheritanc e.
Walter Sutton and Theodore Boveri noted that the behaviour of chromosomes was parallel to the behaviour of genes
structures in the nucleus that appeared to double and divide just before each cell division. These were called chromosomes
Thi s in depen den t alignmen t an d subsequen t segregatio n provi de the chromosoma l basi s fo r Men del's La w of In depen den t Assortmen t.

summary: This section details the historical rediscovery of Mendel's laws and the formulation of the Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance by Sutton and Boveri. It explains how the parallel behavior of chromosomes and genes during meiosis provides the physical basis for Mendelian inheritance.
title: Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance

evidence_snippets.text: Morgan coined the term 'linkage' to describe the physical association of genes on a chromosome
the term 'recombination' to describe the generation of non-parental gene combinations.
used the frequency of recombination between gene pairs on the same chromosome as a measure of the distance between genes

summary: This section details T.H. Morgan's experimental verification of the chromosomal theory of inheritance using Drosophila melanogaster, which led to the discovery of linkage and recombination. It explains how linkage prevents independent assortment and how recombination frequencies are utilized to construct genetic maps.
title: Linkag e an d Recombinatio n

evidence_snippets.text: Such traits are generally controlled by three or more genes and are thus called as polygenic traits.
In a polygenic trait, the phenotype reflects the contribution of each allele, meaning the effect of each allele is additive.

summary: This section introduces polygenic inheritance, where traits are controlled by multiple genes and exhibit a continuous gradient of phenotypes rather than distinct alternate forms. It explains how the additive effect of alleles and environmental factors influence these traits, using human skin color and height as primary examples.
title: Polygenic Inheritance

evidence_snippets.text: While most genes affect a single phenotype, a pleiotropic gene can exhibit multiple phenotypic expressions.
The underlying mechanism of pleiotropy is usually the effect of a gene on metabolic pathways that contribute to different phenotypes.
A key example is the disease phenylketonuria in humans, caused by a mutation in the gene coding for the enzyme phenylalanine hydroxylase.

summary: This section introduces pleiotropy, a genetic phenomenon where a single gene influences multiple phenotypic traits. It explains the underlying metabolic mechanism and illustrates this concept using the human genetic disorder phenylketonuria.
title: Pleiotropy

evidence_snippets.text: He named this structure the X body, which later investigations confirmed was a chromosome, subsequently named the X-chromosome.
In many insects, sex determination is of the XO type, where all eggs carry an X-chromosome, but only some sperms carry an X-chromosome.
In other species, including humans and Drosophila, the XY type is observed.
males produce two different types of gametes (either with/without X, or with X/Y), a phenomenon called male heterogamety.
females produce two different types of gametes. This is called female heterogamety.

summary: This section explains the genetic and chromosomal basis of sex determination, tracing its discovery from Henking's identification of the X body. It details different mechanisms of sex determination, including XO, XY, and ZW systems, and distinguishes between male and female heterogamety.
title: Sex Determination

evidence_snippets.text: Humans follow the $XY$ type of sex determination.
Out of $23$ pairs of chromosomes, $22$ pairs are autosomes and are identical in both sexes.
The $23$rd pair consists of sex chromosomes: $XX$ in females and $XY$ in males.
the genetic makeup of the sperm determines the sex of the child.

summary: This section explains the XY mechanism of sex determination in humans, where females have XX sex chromosomes and males have XY. It details how the genetic makeup of the sperm determines the sex of the offspring with equal probability.
title: Sex Determination in Humans

evidence_snippets.text: Sex determination in honey bees is based on the number of sets of chromosomes received.
Females (queens or workers) develop from the union of a sperm and an egg and are diploid (32 chromosomes).
Males (drones) develop from unfertilized eggs via parthenogenesis and are haploid (16 chromosomes).

summary: This section explains the haplodiploid sex-determination system in honey bees, where sex is determined by the number of chromosome sets. Females are diploid and develop from fertilized eggs, while males are haploid and develop from unfertilized eggs via parthenogenesis.
title: Sex Determination in Honey Bee

evidence_snippets.text: Mutation is a phenomenon which results in alteration of DNA sequences and consequently results in changes in the genotype and the phenotype
loss (deletions) or gain (insertion/duplication) of a segment of DNA, result in alteration in chromosomes.
Mutation also arise due to change in a single base pair of DNA. This is known as point mutation.
Deletions and insertions of base pairs of DNA, causes frame-shift mutations
chemical and physical factors that induce mutations. These are referred to as mutagens.

summary: This section introduces mutation as a key driver of genetic variation, defining it as an alteration in DNA sequences that impacts genotype and phenotype. It details different types of mutations, including chromosomal aberrations, point mutations, and frame-shift mutations, and identifies physical and chemical mutagens that induce these changes.
title: Mutation

evidence_snippets.text: Such an analysis of traits in a several generations of a family is called the pedigree analysis.
Mendelian disorders are mainly determined by alteration or mutation in the single gene.
Colour Blindness is a sex-linked recessive disorder due to defect in either red or green cone of eye
Haemophilia is a sex linked recessive disease, which shows its transmission from unaffected carrier female to some of the male progeny.
Sickle-cell anaemia is an autosome linked recessive trait that can be transmitted from parents to the offspring
Thalassemia is an autosome-linked recessive blood disease transmitted from parents to offspring

summary: This section introduces genetic disorders in humans, classifying them into Mendelian and chromosomal disorders. It explains pedigree analysis as a tool to trace inheritance patterns and details the genetic and molecular mechanisms of major Mendelian disorders, including colour blindness, haemophilia, sickle-cell anaemia, phenylketonuria, and thalassemia.
title: Genetic Disorders

evidence_snippets.text: Chromosomal disorders are caused by the absence, excess, or abnormal arrangement of one or more chromosomes.
Aneuploidy is the gain or loss of a chromosome(s) due to the failure of chromatid segregation during the cell division cycle.
Polyploidy is an increase in a whole set of chromosomes in an organism, often resulting from the failure of cytokinesis after the telophase stage of cell division.
Down’s syndrome is caused by the presence of an additional copy of chromosome 21 (trisomy of 21).
Klinefelter’s syndrome is caused by an additional copy of the X-chromosome, resulting in a karyotype of 47, XXY.
Turner’s syndrome is caused by the absence of one of the X chromosomes, resulting in a 45, X0 karyotype.

summary: This section explains chromosomal disorders arising from abnormal chromosome numbers or arrangements. It defines aneuploidy and polyploidy, and describes the genetic basis and physical characteristics of Down's, Klinefelter's, and Turner's syndromes.
title: Chromosomal Disorders

evidence_snippets.text: Gregor Mendel systematically studied inheritance in pea plants and proposed the 'Mendel’s Laws of Inheritance'.
Post-Mendelian studies revealed phenomena like incomplete dominance and co-dominance.
The Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance correlates Mendel’s laws with the behavior of chromosomes during meiosis.
In humans, females have XX and males have XY sex chromosomes... In birds (chickens), males are ZZ and females are ZW.
Mutation is a change in genetic material... Down’s syndrome is due to trisomy of chromosome 21... In Turner’s syndrome, one X chromosome is missing

summary: This section summarizes the principles of genetics, covering Mendel's laws of inheritance, extensions like co-dominance, and the chromosomal theory of inheritance. It also outlines the mechanisms of sex determination and the genetic basis of mutations and chromosomal disorders.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and ribonucleic acid (RNA) are the two types of nucleic acids found in living systems.
DNA is a long polymer of deoxyribonucleotides. Its length is defined by the number of nucleotides (or base pairs, bp) present in it

summary: This section introduces DNA and RNA as the two primary nucleic acids, explaining their roles as genetic material and functional molecules. It also defines DNA length in terms of nucleotide or base pair count, providing characteristic examples from various organisms.
title: The DNA

evidence_snippets.text: A nucleotide consists of three components: a nitrogenous base, a pentose sugar (ribose in RNA, deoxyribose in DNA), and a phosphate group.
Two nucleotides join through a 3'-5' phosphodiester linkage to form a dinucleotide
James Watson and Francis Crick proposed the Double Helix model for DNA structure
Central Dogma in molecular biology, which states that genetic information flows from DNA to RNA to Protein

summary: This section explains the chemical structure of polynucleotide chains, detailing the components of nucleotides and their linkages. It describes the structural features of the DNA double helix, including complementary base pairing, antiparallel polarity, and the Central Dogma of molecular biology.
title: Structure of Polynucleotide Chain

evidence_snippets.text: length of the DNA double helix in a typical mammalian cell... is approximately 2.2 metres
negatively charged DNA is held together with some positively charged proteins in a region called the 'nucleoid'
negatively charged DNA is wrapped around this positively charged histone octamer to form a structure called a nucleosome
Euchromatin: Loosely packed regions... transcriptionally active. Heterochromatin: Densely packed regions... transcriptionally inactive.

summary: This section explains the packaging mechanisms of the long DNA double helix in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes. It details the formation of nucleosomes from histones, the organization of chromatin into 'beads-on-string' structures, and the distinction between transcriptionally active euchromatin and inactive heterochromatin.
title: Packaging of DNA Helix

evidence_snippets.text: By 1926, the quest reached the molecular level. Scientists like Mendel, Sutton, and Morgan had narrowed the search to chromosomes in the nucleus
In 1928, Frederick Griffith conducted experiments with Streptococcus pneumoniae, the bacterium causing pneumonia.
They discovered that DNA alone from S bacteria caused R bacteria to become transformed.

summary: This section details the historical search for the genetic material, focusing on Frederick Griffith's transformation experiments with Streptococcus pneumoniae. It also covers the subsequent biochemical characterization by Avery, MacLeod, and McCarty, which experimentally demonstrated that DNA is the transforming principle.
title: The Search for Genetic Material

evidence_snippets.text: The unequivocal proof that DNA is the genetic material came from the experiments of Alfred Hershey and Martha Chase (1952).
They worked with bacteriophages—viruses that infect bacteria.
Viruses grown in the presence of radioactive phosphorus contained radioactive DNA but not radioactive protein
The steps shown are 1. Infection, 2. Blending, and 3. Centrifugation.

summary: This section describes the Hershey-Chase experiment (1952), which provided the definitive proof that DNA is the genetic material. By using radioactive isotopes of phosphorus and sulfur to label bacteriophages, they demonstrated that only viral DNA enters E. coli cells during infection.
title: The Genetic Material is DNA

evidence_snippets.text: A molecule that acts as a genetic material must fulfill the following criteria: (i) It should be able to generate its replica...
DNA is chemically less reactive and structurally more stable than RNA.
because RNA is unstable, it mutates at a faster rate. Consequently, viruses having an RNA genome... mutate and evolve faster
DNA is preferred for the storage of genetic information due to its superior stability, whereas RNA is better suited for the transmission

summary: This section establishes the criteria for a molecule to act as genetic material and compares DNA and RNA. It explains that while DNA is preferred for the stable, long-term storage of genetic information, RNA is better suited for its transmission and direct protein synthesis.
title: Properties of Genetic Material (DNA versus RNA)

evidence_snippets.text: Evidence suggests that RNA was the first genetic material.
RNA has the unique ability to act as both a genetic material and a catalyst.
DNA evolved from RNA through chemical modifications that increased its stability.

summary: This section introduces the RNA World hypothesis, highlighting RNA as the first genetic material and a biological catalyst. It explains how DNA evolved from RNA through chemical modifications that provided greater stability and repair mechanisms.
title: RNA World

evidence_snippets.text: The proposed scheme, known as semiconservative DNA replication, suggests that the two strands of the DNA helix separate
each resulting DNA molecule consists of one parental (original) strand and one newly synthesized strand.

summary: This section introduces the semiconservative model of DNA replication proposed by Watson and Crick. It explains how the two strands of the DNA double helix separate to act as templates for synthesizing new complementary strands.
title: Replication

evidence_snippets.text: Matthew Meselson and Franklin Stahl provided experimental proof for semiconservative replication using Escherichia coli.
This 'heavy' DNA could be distinguished from normal DNA ($^{14}N$) using cesium chloride ($CsCl$) density gradient centrifugation
These experiments proved that DNA in chromosomes also replicates semiconservatively.

summary: This section describes the experimental validation of semiconservative DNA replication. It details Meselson and Stahl's density-gradient centrifugation experiment using E. coli and Taylor's chromosome replication experiment using radioactive thymidine.
title: The Experimental Proof

evidence_snippets.text: The primary enzyme is DNA-dependent DNA polymerase, which uses a DNA template to catalyze the polymerization of deoxynucleotides.
Deoxyribonucleoside triphosphates (dNTPs) serve a dual purpose: they act as substrates for polymerization and provide energy for the reaction.
Instead, replication occurs within a small opening of the DNA helix called the replication fork.
replication is discontinuous, producing small fragments that are later joined by the enzyme DNA ligase.
Replication does not start randomly; it begins at specific regions called the origin of replication.

summary: This section details the enzymatic machinery and energetics of DNA replication, focusing on the primary role of DNA-dependent DNA polymerase. It explains the formation of the replication fork, the mechanisms of continuous and discontinuous synthesis, the necessity of an origin of replication, and the coordination of replication with the eukaryotic cell cycle.
title: The Machinery and the Enzymes

evidence_snippets.text: Transcription is the biological process of copying genetic information from one strand of DNA into a complementary strand of RNA.
adenosine (A) pairs with uracil (U) instead of thymine (T).
the two complementary RNA molecules produced would bind to each other to form double-stranded RNA

summary: This section defines transcription as the selective copying of genetic information from a single DNA strand into a complementary RNA strand. It also explains the evolutionary and functional reasons why only one of the two DNA strands is transcribed.
title: Transcription

evidence_snippets.text: A transcription unit in DNA is primarily defined by three distinct regions: (i) A Promoter... (ii) The Structural gene... and (iii) A Terminator
the DNA strand with 3' -> 5' polarity serves as the template strand.
The other strand, which has 5' -> 3' polarity and a sequence identical to the resulting RNA... is called the coding strand.
The promoter is located upstream (towards the 5'-end of the coding strand) and provides the binding site for RNA polymerase.
The terminator is located downstream (towards the 3'-end of the coding strand) and defines the end of the transcription process.

summary: This section defines the structure and components of a transcription unit in DNA, which includes the promoter, structural gene, and terminator. It explains the functional distinction between the template and coding strands based on their polarity and the direction of transcription.
title: Transcription Unit

evidence_snippets.text: A gene is the functional unit of inheritance.
A cistron is defined as a segment of DNA coding for a polypeptide.
In eukaryotes, monocistronic structural genes often have interrupted coding sequences, known as a split-gene arrangement.

summary: This section defines the gene as the functional unit of inheritance and introduces the concept of a cistron, distinguishing between monocistronic and polycistronic transcription units. It also explains the split-gene arrangement in eukaryotes, highlighting the differences between coding exons and non-coding introns.
title: Transcription Unit and the Gene

evidence_snippets.text: In bacteria, three major types of RNA are essential for protein synthesis: mRNA (messenger RNA)... tRNA (transfer RNA)... and rRNA (ribosomal RNA)
The process of transcription in bacteria involves three main stages: Initiation, Elongation, and Termination.
It associates transiently with initiation-factor (\sigma) and termination-factor (\rho) to initiate and terminate the transcription
In eukaryotes, there are two additional complexities. First, there are at least three RNA polymerases in the nucleus
They undergo splicing, where introns are removed and exons are joined... hnRNA undergoes capping... and tailing

summary: This section details the three major types of RNA (mRNA, tRNA, and rRNA) and explains the mechanism of transcription in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes. It describes the stages of initiation, elongation, and termination, highlighting the roles of sigma and rho factors in bacteria, and post-transcriptional modifications like splicing, capping, and tailing in eukaryotes.
title: Types of RNA and the process of Transcription

evidence_snippets.text: He suggested a triplet code, as a permutation of 4^3 would generate 64 codons, which is more than enough to cover all 20 amino acids.
The code is degenerate, meaning some amino acids are coded by more than one codon.
Point mutations involve a change in a single base pair in the DNA sequence.
Frameshift mutations occur when the insertion or deletion of one or more bases alters the reading frame of the genetic message.
tRNA features an anticodon loop with bases complementary to the mRNA codons and an amino acid acceptor end

summary: This section explains the genetic code, detailing how triplet codons translate nucleotide sequences into amino acids. It covers the experimental deciphering, key features of the code (such as degeneracy and universality), the impact of point and frameshift mutations, and the role of tRNA as an adapter molecule.
title: Genetic Code

evidence_snippets.text: Translation is the process by which amino acids are polymerized to form a polypeptide chain.
The ribosome is the cellular machinery responsible for protein synthesis.
mRNA contains sequences that are not translated into protein, known as untranslated regions (UTRs).

summary: This section explains translation, the process of synthesizing polypeptide chains from mRNA templates. It details the activation of amino acids, the catalytic role of ribosomes, and the three stages of translation: initiation, elongation, and termination.
title: Translation

evidence_snippets.text: In eukaryotes, this regulation can be exerted at four distinct levels: (i) transcriptional level... (ii) processing level... (iii) transport level... (iv) translational level.
gene expression is regulated by metabolic, physiological, or environmental conditions.
In prokaryotes, the control of the rate of transcriptional initiation is the primary site for gene expression regulation.

summary: This section explains the multi-level regulation of gene expression in eukaryotes and prokaryotes, driven by metabolic, physiological, and environmental conditions. It introduces the mechanisms of prokaryotic transcriptional control involving promoters, operators, activators, and repressors within operons.
title: Regulation of Gene Expression

evidence_snippets.text: The lac operon consists of one regulatory gene, the i gene, and three structural genes (z, y, a).
Lactose is the substrate for beta-galactosidase and regulates the switching on and off of the operon; thus, it is termed the inducer.
Regulation of the lac operon by a repressor is referred to as negative regulation.

summary: This section explains the structure, function, and regulation of the lac operon in bacteria. It details how the regulatory i gene and structural genes (z, y, a) coordinate lactose metabolism through negative regulation by a repressor and activation by an inducer.
title: The Lac operon

evidence_snippets.text: The Human Genome Project (HGP) was a 'mega project' launched in 1990 to determine the complete DNA sequence of the human genome.
Bioinformatics, which uses computational tools to manage and analyze biological data.
One approach focused on identifying all the genes that are expressed as RNA (referred to as Expressed Sequence Tags (ESTs)).
sequencing the whole set of genome that contained all the coding and non-coding sequence, and later assigning different regions in the sequence
The commonly used hosts were bacteria and yeast, and the vectors were called as BAC (bacterial artificial chromosomes), and YAC
The fragments were sequenced using automated DNA sequencers that worked on the principle of a method developed by Frederick Sanger.

summary: The Human Genome Project (HGP) was a massive international scientific initiative launched in 1990 to sequence the entire human genome, completed in 2003. It utilized methodologies like Expressed Sequence Tags (ESTs) and Sequence Annotation, relying on cloning vectors (BACs and YACs) and automated Sanger sequencing. The project catalyzed the field of bioinformatics to manage and analyze the vast amount of genomic data generated.
title: Human Genome Project

evidence_snippets.text: The human genome contains 3164.7 million bp.
The total number of genes is estimated at 30,000—much lower than previous estimates
Less than 2 per cent of the genome codes for proteins.
Repetitive sequences are stretches of DNA sequences that are repeated many times
single-base DNA differences (SNPs – single nucleotide polymorphism, pronounced as 'snips') occur in humans.

summary: This section outlines the key observations and salient features of the human genome discovered through the Human Genome Project. It details statistics on genome size, gene count, protein-coding regions, repetitive DNA sequences, and single nucleotide polymorphisms.
title: Salient Features of Human Genome

evidence_snippets.text: One of the greatest impacts of having the HG sequence may well be enabling a radically new approach to biological research.
With whole-genome sequences and new high-throughput technologies, we can approach questions systematically and on a much broader scale.
They can study all the genes in a genome, for example, all the transcripts in a particular tissue or organ or tumor

summary: This section outlines the transformative impact of the Human Genome sequence on biological research, shifting the focus from studying individual genes to analyzing entire genomes systematically. It highlights how high-throughput technologies enable the study of interconnected gene and protein networks to understand biological systems.
title: Applications and Future Challenges

evidence_snippets.text: DNA fingerprinting is a very quick way to compare the DNA sequences of any two individuals.
DNA fingerprinting involves identifying differences in some specific regions in DNA sequence called as repetitive DNA
An inheritable mutation observed in a population at high frequency is referred to as DNA polymorphism.
VNTR is a type of mini-satellite where a small DNA sequence is repeated tandemly.

summary: This section introduces DNA fingerprinting, a rapid technique used to compare DNA sequences among individuals by identifying variations in repetitive DNA. It details the role of DNA polymorphism, satellite DNA, and Variable Number of Tandem Repeats (VNTRs), alongside the experimental methodology and forensic applications of this technology.
title: DNA Fingerprinting

evidence_snippets.text: Replication is semi-conservative.
Transcription converts DNA to RNA; in eukaryotes, introns are spliced out to form functional mRNA.
The genetic code is a triplet system where mRNA codons specify amino acids.
Translation occurs at ribosomes with tRNA acting as an adapter.
Gene expression is regulated, exemplified by the bacterial Lac operon.
DNA fingerprinting identifies individuals based on DNA polymorphism

summary: This section provides a comprehensive summary of molecular biology principles, including the structure and replication of DNA, transcription, translation, and gene regulation. It also highlights key applications and projects such as the Human Genome Project and DNA fingerprinting.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: The Big Bang theory explains the origin of the universe as a singular, unimaginable physical explosion.
He demonstrated that life comes only from pre-existing life.
the formation of life was preceded by chemical evolution—the formation of diverse organic molecules from inorganic constituents.
In 1953, S.L. Miller, an American scientist, provided experimental evidence for chemical evolution.

summary: This section covers the origin of the universe and Earth, detailing early atmospheric conditions and theories on how life began. It contrasts historical ideas like spontaneous generation and panspermia with Louis Pasteur's biogenesis, and explains chemical evolution supported by the Oparin-Haldane hypothesis and Miller's experiment.
title: Origin of Life

evidence_snippets.text: Conventional religious literature describes the theory of special creation
He proposed that there has been a gradual evolution of life forms.
The fitness, according to Darwin, refers ultimately and only to reproductive fitness.
He called it natural selection and implied it as a mechanism of evolution.
All the existing life forms share similarities and share common ancestors.

summary: This section contrasts the traditional Theory of Special Creation with the scientific theory of gradual evolution proposed by Charles Darwin and Alfred Wallace. It explains how natural selection, driven by reproductive fitness and variation within populations, leads to the evolution of life forms sharing common ancestry over billions of years.
title: Evolution of Life Forms - A Theory

evidence_snippets.text: Fossils are remains of hard parts of life-forms found in rocks.
Homology is based on divergent evolution.
analogous structures are a result of convergent evolution
This camouflage mechanism allowed better-adapted moths to survive and increase in population size.

summary: This section explores the diverse lines of evidence supporting the theory of evolution, including paleontological, embryological, comparative anatomical (homology and analogy), biochemical, and anthropogenic evidence. It highlights key concepts such as divergent and convergent evolution, and illustrates natural selection in action through the classic case of industrial melanism in moths.
title: What are the Evidences for Evolution?

evidence_snippets.text: Adaptive radiation is defined as the process of evolution of different species in a given geographical area starting from a point and literally radiating
During his journey to the Galapagos Islands, Darwin observed an amazing diversity of creatures, particularly small black birds now known as Darwin’s Finches.
Australian marsupials provide another example of adaptive radiation. A number of different marsupials, each distinct from the other, evolved from an ancestral stock
When more than one adaptive radiation occurs in an isolated geographical area representing different habitats, it results in convergent evolution.

summary: This section defines adaptive radiation as the evolutionary process where multiple species radiate from a common ancestor to adapt to diverse habitats. It illustrates this concept using Darwin's finches and Australian marsupials, and explains how multiple adaptive radiations in an isolated area lead to convergent evolution.
title: What is Adaptive Radiation?

evidence_snippets.text: Evolution by natural selection, in a true sense, would have started when cellular forms of life with differences in metabolic capability originated
Branching descent and natural selection are the two key concepts of the Darwinian Theory of Evolution.
Lamarck proposed that the evolution of life forms occurred, but he argued it was driven by the use and disuse of organs.
Darwin's theory of natural selection was significantly influenced by the work of Thomas Malthus on populations.

summary: This section explains the origin and mechanisms of biological evolution, focusing on Darwinian theory and its key concepts of branching descent and natural selection. It also discusses the genetic basis of fitness, Lamarck's theory of use and disuse, and the influence of Malthusian population principles on Darwin's work.
title: Biological Evolution

evidence_snippets.text: de Vries brought forth the idea of mutations—large differences arising suddenly in a population.
He believed that mutation, rather than the minor heritable variations discussed by Darwin, is the primary cause of evolution.
de Vries believed mutation caused speciation and hence called it saltation (single-step large mutation).

summary: This section explains the mechanism of evolution through Hugo de Vries' Mutation Theory, contrasting it with Darwinian evolution. It introduces mutations as random, directionless, single-step large variations (saltation) that drive speciation.
title: Mechanism of Evolution

evidence_snippets.text: The Hardy-Weinberg principle states that allele frequencies in a population are stable and remain constant from generation to generation.
Thus, the frequencies can be represented as: $p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1$.
If the change in allele frequency happens by chance, it is called genetic drift.

summary: This section introduces the Hardy-Weinberg principle, which states that allele frequencies in a population remain constant across generations under genetic equilibrium. It presents the mathematical representation of this equilibrium and details the evolutionary factors, including modes of natural selection, that disrupt it.
title: Hardy-Weinberg Principle

evidence_snippets.text: About 2000 million years ago (mya), the first cellular forms of life appeared on Earth.
The first organisms that invaded land were plants.
These animals called lobefins evolved into the first amphibians that lived on both land and water.
About 65 mya, the dinosaurs suddenly disappeared from the earth.
The first mammals were like shrews, with small-sized fossils.

summary: This section outlines the major milestones in the evolutionary history of life on Earth, spanning from the origin of the first cellular forms and early plants to the diversification of vertebrates, reptiles, dinosaurs, and mammals. It highlights key transitions, such as the invasion of land by plants and animals, and the impact of geological events like continental drift on mammalian distribution.
title: A Brief Account of Evolution

evidence_snippets.text: About 15 mya, primates called Dryopithecus and Ramapithecus existed.
Two mya, Australopithecines lived in East African grasslands.
The first human-like being, the hominid, was called Homo habilis.
Homo erectus lived about 1.5 mya and possessed a large brain of around 900 cc.
The Neanderthal man, with a brain size of 1400 cc, lived in the near east and central Asia
During the ice age between 75,000-10,000 years ago, modern Homo sapiens arose.

summary: This section traces the evolutionary lineage of humans from early primates like Dryopithecus and Ramapithecus to modern Homo sapiens, highlighting key hominid stages and their physical and behavioral traits. It also summarizes the fundamental principles of organic evolution, including natural selection and speciation.
title: Origin and Evolution of Man

evidence_snippets.text: health was defined as a state of body and mind where there was a balance of certain 'humors'.
It is defined as a state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being, rather than merely the absence of disease
An antibody molecule, a critical part of the immune response, is a Y-shaped protein consisting of two light chains and two heavy chains
infectious diseases, which are easily transmitted from one person to another (e.g., AIDS)
non-infectious diseases, which are not transmissible (e.g., cancer).

summary: This section introduces the concept of human health, tracing its transition from historical humor-based hypotheses to modern biological definitions. It outlines the factors affecting health, methods for maintaining wellness, and the classification of diseases into infectious and non-infectious categories.
title: Introduction to Human Health and Disease

evidence_snippets.text: A variety of organisms, including bacteria, viruses, fungi, protozoans, and helminths, can cause diseases in humans and are known as pathogens.
The Plasmodium parasite follows a complex life cycle requiring two hosts: humans and mosquitoes.
Maintenance of personal and public hygiene is very important for prevention and control of many infectious diseases.

summary: This section introduces common human diseases caused by various pathogens, including bacteria, viruses, fungi, protozoans, and helminths. It details their symptoms, modes of transmission, and life cycles, while outlining essential strategies for prevention, vector control, and immunization.
title: Common Diseases in Humans

evidence_snippets.text: This overall ability of the host to fight the disease-causing organisms, conferred by the immune system is called immunity.
The body is able to differentiate between 'self' and 'nonself' cells, and the cell-mediated immune response (CMI) is primarily responsible for the rejection of grafts.
When ready-made antibodies are directly given to protect the body against foreign agents, it is called passive immunity.
The principle of immunisation or vaccination is based on the property of 'memory' of the immune system.
Allergy is the exaggerated response of the immune system to certain antigens present in the environment.
Lymphoid organs are where the origin, maturation, and proliferation of lymphocytes occur.

summary: This section covers the human immune system, distinguishing between active and passive immunity, and explaining vaccination, allergies, and autoimmune diseases. It also details the roles of primary and secondary lymphoid organs, including MALT, in lymphocyte maturation and activation.
title: Immunity

evidence_snippets.text: Innate immunity is non-specific type of defence, that is present at the time of birth.
Physical barriers: Skin on our body is the main barrier which prevents entry of the micro-organisms.
Physiological barriers: Acid in the stomach, saliva in the mouth, tears from eyes—all prevent microbial growth.
Cellular barriers: Certain types of leukocytes (WBC) of our body... can phagocytose and destroy microbes.
Cytokine barriers: Virus-infected cells secrete proteins called interferons which protect non-infected cells

summary: This section introduces innate immunity as a non-specific defense system present at birth. It details the four types of barriers that prevent the entry and growth of foreign agents: physical, physiological, cellular, and cytokine barriers.
title: Innate Immunity

evidence_snippets.text: Acquired immunity, on the other hand is pathogen specific. It is characterised by memory.
Subsequent encounter with the same pathogen elicits a highly intensified secondary or anamnestic response.
The B-lymphocytes produce an army of proteins... These proteins are called antibodies.
Each antibody molecule has four peptide chains, two small called light chains and two longer called heavy chains.
Because these antibodies are found in the blood, the response is also called as humoral immune response.
The second type is called cell-mediated immune response or cell-mediated immunity (CMI). The T-lymphocytes mediate CMI.

summary: This section introduces acquired immunity, which is pathogen-specific and characterized by immunological memory. It details the roles of B-lymphocytes and T-lymphocytes, the structural composition of antibodies, and the distinction between humoral and cell-mediated immune responses.
title: Acquired Immunity

evidence_snippets.text: AIDS stands for Acquired Immuno Deficiency Syndrome, indicating a deficiency of the immune system acquired during an individual's lifetime
It is caused by the Human Immuno deficiency Virus (HIV), a retrovirus with an envelope enclosing an RNA genome.
RNA genome of the virus replicates to form viral DNA with the help of the enzyme reverse transcriptase.
leading to a progressive decrease in the number of helper T-lymphocytes in the body of the infected person.
A widely used diagnostic test for AIDS is enzyme linked immuno-sorbent assay (ELISA).

summary: This section covers Acquired Immuno Deficiency Syndrome (AIDS), detailing its transmission routes, the replication mechanism of HIV within host cells, and its impact on helper T-lymphocytes. It also discusses the clinical manifestations, diagnosis via ELISA, and the critical role of prevention and social support in managing the disease.
title: AIDS

evidence_snippets.text: In cancer cells, these regulatory mechanisms break down.
Cancer cells lose this property and continue to divide, forming masses of cells called tumors.
metastasis, the most feared characteristic of malignant tumors.

summary: This section covers the biological basis of cancer, explaining how the breakdown of cell growth regulation and loss of contact inhibition lead to tumor formation. It distinguishes between benign and malignant tumors, identifies various physical, chemical, and biological carcinogens, and outlines modern methods for cancer detection, diagnosis, and treatment.
title: Cancer

evidence_snippets.text: The most frequently abused substances include opioids, cannabinoids, and coca alkaloids
Heroin, often referred to as 'smack', is chemically known as diacetylmorphine.
Cannabinoids are a group of chemicals that interact with cannabinoid receptors, primarily in the brain.
Cocaine... functions by interfering with the transport of the neurotransmitter dopamine.
Tobacco... contains nicotine, an alkaloid that triggers the adrenal glands to release adrenaline and nor-adrenaline

summary: This section covers the classification, sources, and physiological effects of commonly abused substances, including opioids, cannabinoids, cocaine, hallucinogens, and tobacco. It explains how these drugs interact with specific receptors and neurotransmitters in the human body, leading to physical and psychological health impairments.
title: Drugs and Alcohol Abuse

evidence_snippets.text: Adolescence is a transitional period and process between the ages of 12 and 18 during which a child matures into an adult
Common motivators for drug and alcohol use among youngsters include curiosity, a desire for adventure, and experimentation.

summary: This section defines adolescence as a critical transitional phase between childhood and adulthood marked by significant biological and behavioral changes. It also examines the primary psychological, social, and environmental factors that motivate adolescents to experiment with drugs and alcohol.
title: Adolescence and Drug/Alcohol Abuse

evidence_snippets.text: Addiction is a psychological attachment to these effects, which drives individuals to continue substance use
With repeated use, the tolerance level of the receptors in our body increases.
Dependence is the tendency of the body to manifest a characteristic and unpleasant withdrawal syndrome

summary: This section explains the psychological and physiological mechanisms of substance abuse, focusing on addiction, tolerance, dependence, and withdrawal syndrome. It describes how repeated drug or alcohol use leads to receptor tolerance and physical dependence, resulting in severe withdrawal symptoms upon discontinuation.
title: Addiction and Dependence

evidence_snippets.text: Excessive doses can lead to coma and death due to respiratory failure, heart failure, or cerebral hemorrhage.
Common warning signs of abuse among youth include a drop in academic performance, unexplained absence from school or college...
Individuals who take drugs intravenously... are at a much higher risk of acquiring serious infections like AIDS and Hepatitis B.
Chronic use of drugs and alcohol can lead to long-term effects such as... liver damage known as cirrhosis.
Sportspersons may misuse narcotic analgesics, anabolic steroids, diuretics, and certain hormones to enhance performance...

summary: This section details the immediate and long-term physical, psychological, and social consequences of drug and alcohol abuse. It highlights warning signs in youth, the severe risks of intravenous drug use such as AIDS and Hepatitis B transmission, and the damaging side effects of anabolic steroid misuse in sports.
title: Effects of Drug/Alcohol Abuse

evidence_snippets.text: The principle 'prevention is better than cure' is vital, as habits like smoking and drug use are most likely to be adopted during adolescence.
Key measures for prevention and control include: (i) Avoid undue peer pressure... (ii) Education and counselling... (v) Seeking professional and medical help

summary: This section outlines strategies for the prevention and control of drug and alcohol abuse among adolescents. It highlights the critical roles of parents, teachers, and peers, and details specific measures such as counseling, recognizing danger signs, and seeking professional medical assistance.
title: Prevention and Control

evidence_snippets.text: Health is defined as a state of complete physical, mental, social, and psychological well-being
The immune system is central to preventing disease, utilizing innate defences... and acquired immunity.
AIDS caused by the human immuno-deficiency virus (HIV) is fatal but can be prevented

summary: This section summarizes the concepts of health, infectious diseases, and the human immune system, including innate and acquired immunity. It also highlights major health challenges such as AIDS, cancer, and drug and alcohol abuse, emphasizing prevention and treatment.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: The discovery of the five major groups of PGRs was accidental.
F.W. Went successfully isolated this substance, named auxin, from the tips of oat (Avena sativa) seedling coleoptiles.
Miller et al. (1955), later identified and crystallised the cytokinesis promoting active substance that they termed kinetin.

summary: This section details the historical and accidental discoveries of the five major groups of Plant Growth Regulators (PGRs). It highlights key experiments and researchers, including the Darwins' work on phototropism leading to auxin discovery, and the identification of gibberellins, cytokinins, abscisic acid, and ethylene.
title: The Discovery of Plant Growth Regulators

evidence_snippets.text: Microbes are the major components of biological systems on Earth, existing alongside macroscopic plants and animals.
microbes thrive in extreme environments where no other life-forms can survive
Microbes are highly diverse, encompassing protozoa, bacteria, fungi, and microscopic agents like viruses, viroids, and prions
bacteria and many fungi can be grown on nutritive media to form colonies.

summary: This section introduces microbes as omnipresent and highly diverse biological entities capable of surviving in extreme environments. It outlines the major groups of microbes, including bacteria, fungi, viruses, viroids, and prions, and explains how they can be cultured on nutritive media for scientific study.
title: Introduction to Microbes

evidence_snippets.text: conversion of milk to curd by Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB), such as Lactobacillus.
bread is made by fermenting dough with baker's yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae).
The large holes in 'Swiss cheese' are caused by the production of large amounts of CO2 by the bacterium Propionibacterium sharmanii.

summary: This section explores the essential role of microorganisms in preparing everyday household food products. It details how bacteria and fungi are utilized in the production of curd, fermented foods like bread and dosa, traditional drinks like toddy, and various types of cheese.
title: Microbes in Household Products

evidence_snippets.text: To produce these on a large scale, microbes must be grown in very large vessels known as fermentors.
Antibiotics are chemical substances produced by some microbes that can kill or retard the growth of other disease-causing microbes.
Cyclosporin A, produced by the fungus Trichoderma polysporum, is used as an immunosuppressive agent in organ-transplant patients.
Statins, produced by the yeast Monascus purpureus, are used as blood-cholesterol lowering agents.

summary: This section details the industrial applications of microbes in synthesizing beverages, antibiotics, enzymes, and bioactive molecules. It explains the role of large-scale fermentors and highlights specific microbial species used for commercial and medical purposes.
title: Microbes in Industrial Products

evidence_snippets.text: Municipal waste-water, also known as sewage, contains large amounts of organic matter and pathogenic microbes.
Primary treatment involves the physical removal of large and small particles from sewage through filtration and sedimentation.
BOD refers to the amount of oxygen that would be consumed if all the organic matter in one liter of water were oxidized by bacteria.

summary: This section explains how municipal wastewater (sewage) is treated in Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs) using natural heterotrophic microbes. It details the physical processes of primary treatment and the biological processes of secondary treatment, including the roles of aerobic flocs, Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) reduction, anaerobic sludge digestion, and biogas production.
title: Microbes in Sewage Treatment

evidence_snippets.text: Biogas is a fuel gas mixture, predominantly methane ($CH_4$), produced through microbial activity.
These bacteria are known as methanogens, with *Methanobacterium* being a common example.
A biogas plant consists of a concrete tank (10-15 feet deep) where bio-wastes and a slurry of cattle dung (gobar) are collected.

summary: This section explains the microbial production of biogas, a methane-rich fuel gas mixture generated by anaerobic bacteria known as methanogens. It describes the structure and operation of a typical biogas plant utilizing cattle dung slurry, alongside the institutional history of biogas technology development in India.
title: Microbes in Production of Biogas

evidence_snippets.text: Biocontrol refers to the use of biological methods to manage plant diseases and pests, offering a sustainable alternative to toxic chemical insecticides
The bacterium Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) is used to control butterfly caterpillars
Baculoviruses, specifically those in the genus Nucleopolyhedrovirus, are excellent candidates for Integrated Pest Management (IPM).

summary: This section introduces biocontrol as a sustainable alternative to chemical pesticides, highlighting organic farming's reliance on natural predation. It details specific biological control agents, including Bacillus thuringiensis, Trichoderma, and Baculoviruses, which target pests without harming non-target organisms.
title: Microbes as Biocontrol Agents

evidence_snippets.text: Biofertilisers are organisms that enrich the nutrient quality of the soil.
The main sources of biofertilisers are bacteria, fungi and cyanobacteria.
Fungi are also known to form symbiotic associations with plants (mycorrhiza).
In paddy fields, cyanobacteria serve as an important biofertiliser.

summary: This section introduces biofertilisers as eco-friendly alternatives to chemical fertilisers in organic farming. It highlights how specific bacteria, fungi (mycorrhiza), and cyanobacteria enrich soil nutrients through nitrogen fixation and mineral absorption.
title: Microbes as Biofertilisers

evidence_snippets.text: Bacteria called lactic acid bacteria (LAB) grow in milk to convert it into curd.
Antibiotics like penicillins produced by useful microbes are used to kill disease-causing harmful microbes.
microbes are being used to treat sewage (waste water) by the process of activated sludge formation
Methanogens produce methane (biogas) while degrading plant waste.
Microbes can also be used to kill harmful pests, a process called as biocontrol.
There is a need these days to push for use of biofertilisers in place of chemical fertilisers.

summary: This section summarizes the beneficial applications of microbes in human welfare, including food production, industrial synthesis, sewage treatment, and biogas generation. It also highlights their roles as biocontrol agents and biofertilizers to promote sustainable agricultural practices.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: Biotechnology refers to the techniques of using live organisms or enzymes from organisms to produce products and processes useful to humans.
restriction enzymes of the E. coli bacterium... have the capability of cutting DNA strands in a specific fashion, leaving what are known as 'sticky ends'
plasmids—small ringlets of DNA that float freely in the cytoplasm... and replicate independently of the chromosomal DNA.
The integration of natural science and organisms, cells, parts thereof, and molecular analogues for products and services.

summary: This section introduces biotechnology by tracing its historical foundation through the pioneering work of Herbert Boyer and Stanley Cohen on recombinant DNA. It defines biotechnology in both traditional and modern contexts, including the official definition provided by the European Federation of Biotechnology (EFB).
title: Introduction to Biotechnology

evidence_snippets.text: Modern biotechnology is based on two core techniques: Genetic engineering and Bioprocess engineering.
The first recombinant DNA molecule was constructed in 1972 by Stanley Cohen and Herbert Boyer.

summary: This section introduces the two core techniques of modern biotechnology: genetic engineering and bioprocess engineering. It explains the conceptual advantages of genetic engineering over traditional hybridization and details the historical construction of the first artificial recombinant DNA molecule.
title: Principles of Biotechnology

evidence_snippets.text: Recombinant DNA technology (genetic engineering) can be accomplished only if we have the key tools: restriction enzymes, polymerase enzymes, ligases, vectors, and the host organism.

summary: This section introduces the essential tools required to perform recombinant DNA technology. These key tools include restriction enzymes, polymerase enzymes, ligases, vectors, and the host organism.
title: Tools of Recombinant DNA Technology

evidence_snippets.text: In 1963, two enzymes responsible for restricting the growth of bacteriophage in Escherichia coli were isolated.
Restriction enzymes belong to a larger class of enzymes called nucleases.
These fragments can be separated by a technique known as gel electrophoresis.

summary: This section explains the discovery, naming, and mechanism of restriction enzymes, which cut DNA at specific palindromic sequences to produce sticky ends. It also describes how the resulting DNA fragments are separated using agarose gel electrophoresis, visualized, and purified via elution for recombinant DNA construction.
title: Restriction Enzymes

evidence_snippets.text: Plasmids and bacteriophages are used as vectors due to their ability to replicate within bacterial cells independent of chromosomal DNA control.
The origin of replication (ori) is a specific DNA sequence where replication starts.
A selectable marker helps in identifying and eliminating non-transformants and selectively permitting the growth of transformants.
The tumor-inducing (Ti) plasmid of Agrobacterium tumefaciens has been disarmed to serve as a cloning vector

summary: This section details the essential characteristics of cloning vectors, including the origin of replication, selectable markers, and cloning sites. It explains how recombinants are selected using antibiotic resistance or chromogenic substrates, and describes specialized vectors like the Ti plasmid and retroviruses for plants and animals.
title: Cloning Vectors

evidence_snippets.text: Since DNA is a hydrophilic molecule, it cannot pass through cell membranes.
To force bacteria to take up the plasmid, cells must first be made ‘competent’.
incubating them with the DNA on ice, followed by a brief heat shock at 42°C, and then returning them to ice.
micro-injection, where recombinant DNA is directly injected into the nucleus of an animal cell.
biolistics or the gene gun method is used, where cells are bombarded with high-velocity micro-particles of gold or tungsten coated with DNA.

summary: This section outlines the techniques used to introduce recombinant DNA into host cells. It details chemical treatment and heat shock for bacterial transformation, alongside alternative methods such as micro-injection, biolistics (gene gun), and disarmed pathogen vectors.
title: Competent Host (For Transformation with Recombinant DNA)

evidence_snippets.text: Recombinant DNA technology involves a specific sequence of steps: isolation of DNA, fragmentation of DNA by restriction endonucleases...

summary: This section outlines the sequential steps involved in recombinant DNA technology. It covers the entire workflow from the initial isolation and fragmentation of DNA to the final extraction of the desired product.
title: Processes of Recombinant DNA Technology

evidence_snippets.text: To cut DNA with restriction enzymes, it must be in pure form, free from other macromolecules.
Cells are broken open using enzymes like lysozyme (for bacteria), cellulase (for plant cells), or chitinase (for fungus).
purified DNA is precipitated by adding chilled ethanol. This appears as a collection of fine threads that can be removed by spooling

summary: This section describes the process of isolating and purifying DNA from various cell types. It details the enzymatic breakdown of cell walls and other macromolecules, followed by the precipitation and collection of pure DNA using chilled ethanol and spooling.
title: Isolation of the Genetic Material (DNA)

evidence_snippets.text: Physics is a quantitative science, based on the measurement of physical quantities.
The International System of Units (SI), based on seven base units, is the currently internationally accepted unit system
Dimensional analysis is a powerful tool used to check the dimensional consistency of equations

summary: This section provides a comprehensive summary of units and measurement in physics, covering fundamental and derived quantities within the International System of Units (SI). It outlines the rules for scientific notation, significant figures, and algebraic treatment of units, while highlighting dimensional analysis as a tool for checking equation consistency.
title: Summary of Units and Measurement

evidence_snippets.text: Temperature is a measure of 'hotness' of a body
This chapter explores what heat is, how it is measured, and the various processes by which heat flows from one body to another.

summary: This introductory section provides an overview of the concepts of heat and temperature, distinguishing between common-sense notions and precise physical definitions. It previews the chapter's focus on heat measurement, heat transfer processes, and practical thermal phenomena.
title: Introduction

evidence_snippets.text: Purified DNA is incubated with restriction enzymes at optimal conditions.
Agarose gel electrophoresis is used to monitor the progression of the digestion
Once the source DNA and vector are cut, the 'gene of interest' and the cut vector are mixed with ligase to prepare the recombinant DNA.

summary: This section explains the process of cutting DNA at specific locations using restriction enzymes and monitoring the digestion progress via agarose gel electrophoresis. It also details how the resulting gene of interest and cut vector DNA are joined using DNA ligase to form recombinant DNA.
title: Cutting of DNA at Specific Locations

evidence_snippets.text: Temperature is a relative measure or indication of hotness or coldness.
Heat is defined as the form of energy transferred between two (or more) systems or a system and its surroundings by virtue of temperature difference.
The SI unit of heat energy is the joule (J), while the SI unit of temperature is the Kelvin (K).
heat transfer occurs until they reach the same temperature.

summary: This section defines temperature and heat, establishing their respective SI units of Kelvin and Joule. It also explains how temperature differences drive heat transfer between systems and their surroundings until thermal equilibrium is achieved.
title: Temperature and Heat

evidence_snippets.text: PCR stands for Polymerase Chain Reaction. It is used to synthesize multiple copies of a DNA segment in vitro
The process involves three main steps: (i) Denaturation, (ii) Annealing, and (iii) Extension
Taq polymerase, isolated from Thermus aquaticus
Repeating this cycle 30 times can amplify the DNA segment approximately 1 billion times.

summary: This section explains the Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) process used to amplify a gene of interest in vitro. It details the three main steps of PCR—denaturation, annealing, and extension—and highlights the role of the thermostable Taq polymerase in achieving exponential DNA amplification.
title: Amplification of Gene of Interest using PCR

evidence_snippets.text: Temperature is measured using a thermometer, which utilizes physical properties that change sufficiently with temperature.
To calibrate a thermometer, two fixed reference points are needed where physical phenomena occur at constant temperatures
On the Celsius scale, the ice and steam points are $0^\circ$C and $100^\circ$C, respectively, with 100 equal intervals.
On the Fahrenheit scale, these points are $32^\circ$F and $212^\circ$F, with 180 equal intervals.
The relationship for converting between Fahrenheit temperature ($t_F$) and Celsius temperature ($t_C$) is given by a linear equation

summary: This section explains the principles of thermometry, focusing on how thermometers are calibrated using fixed reference points like the ice and steam points of water. It also introduces the Celsius and Fahrenheit temperature scales, detailing their intervals and the linear relationship used to convert between them.
title: Measurement of Temperature

evidence_snippets.text: There are several methods for introducing ligated DNA into recipient cells.
Once recipient cells are made 'competent', they take up DNA from their surroundings.
the ampicillin resistance gene serves as a selectable marker, allowing for the identification and selection of transformed cells.

summary: This section explains how recombinant DNA is introduced into competent host cells and details the selection process of transformants using selectable markers, such as antibiotic resistance genes.
title: Insertion of Recombinant DNA into the Host Cell/Organism

evidence_snippets.text: Combining these for a given quantity of low-density gas leads to the ideal-gas equation: PV = \mu RT
If the straight line is extrapolated to the axis where pressure would theoretically reach zero, it intersects at -273.15 degrees C
The temperature -273.15 degrees C is designated as absolute zero and forms the foundation of the Kelvin temperature scale

summary: This section derives the ideal-gas equation by combining Boyle's and Charles' laws for low-density gases. It explains the operation of constant-volume gas thermometers and defines absolute zero and the Kelvin temperature scale based on the extrapolation of pressure-temperature relationships.
title: Ideal-Gas Equation and Absolute Temperature

evidence_snippets.text: If a protein-encoding gene is expressed in a heterologous host... it is called a recombinant protein.
In this setup, used medium is drained from one side while fresh medium is added from the other.
To produce products in large quantities (100-1000 litres), bioreactors are required.
A stirred-tank reactor is usually cylindrical or has a curved base to facilitate the mixing of contents.

summary: This section describes the methods used to obtain foreign gene products on both small and large scales. It explains the expression of recombinant proteins, the use of continuous culture systems to maintain exponential growth, and the design and operation of bioreactors, particularly stirred-tank bioreactors, for mass production.
title: Obtaining the Foreign Gene Product

evidence_snippets.text: The increase in the dimensions of a body due to the increase in its temperature is called thermal expansion.
Water exhibits anomalous behaviour: it contracts on heating between 0 °C and 4 °C.
If the thermal expansion of a rod is prevented by fixing its ends rigidly, the rod acquires a compressive strain... The corresponding stress is called thermal stress.

summary: This section introduces thermal expansion, defining its linear, area, and volume forms along with their respective coefficients. It explores the anomalous expansion of water, derives the expansion coefficient for ideal gases, and explains the generation of thermal stress when expansion is constrained.
title: Thermal Expansion

evidence_snippets.text: These processes, collectively referred to as downstream processing, include separation and purification.
The product is then formulated with suitable preservatives.
Strict quality control testing is required for each product, and these processes vary depending on the nature of the product.

summary: This section explains downstream processing, which encompasses the post-biosynthetic stages of separation, purification, and formulation of a product. It also highlights the necessity of clinical trials for drug formulations and strict quality control testing before marketing.
title: Downstream Processing

evidence_snippets.text: The quantity of heat required to raise the temperature of a substance depends on several factors.
S = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta T}
Specific heat capacity, s, is the property of a substance that determines the change in its temperature when a given quantity of heat is absorbed
Water has an exceptionally high specific heat capacity (4186.0 J kg^{-1} K^{-1}) compared to most other substances.

summary: This section introduces heat capacity, specific heat capacity, and molar specific heat capacity as measures of how substances absorb or reject heat. It also discusses the factors affecting heat absorption and the practical applications of water's high specific heat capacity.
title: SPECIFIC HEAT CAPACITY

evidence_snippets.text: Biotechnology involves large-scale production and marketing of products using live organisms or enzymes.
Modern biotechnology relies on altering DNA chemistry (genetic engineering) using tools like restriction endonucleases, DNA ligase, and vectors.
The process includes isolating DNA, cutting it, amplifying the gene of interest, ligating it into a vector, transferring it to a host
purifying the product via downstream processing and bioreactors.

summary: This section provides a comprehensive summary of biotechnology, highlighting the role of genetic engineering in altering DNA chemistry. It outlines the key tools and sequential steps involved in recombinant DNA technology, concluding with downstream processing and the use of bioreactors for product purification.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: Chemistry is the branch of science that studies the preparation, properties, structure, and reactions of material substances.
Chemistry developed mainly in the form of Alchemy and Iatrochemistry during 1300-1600 CE.
In ancient India, chemistry was known as 'Rasayan Shastra', 'Rastantra', 'Ras Kriya', or 'Rasvidya'.
he proposed that matter is made of indivisible particles called 'Paramānu', which are eternal, indestructible
These reduced metal particles, called 'bhasmas', have been scientifically proven to contain nanoparticles.

summary: This section traces the historical evolution of chemistry from ancient alchemical traditions to modern science, highlighting significant contributions from ancient Indian scholars, literature, and archaeological findings. It defines chemistry as the study of the composition, structure, properties, and transformations of matter, and introduces early concepts of atomic theory and metallurgy.
title: DEVELOPMENT OF CHEMISTRY

evidence_snippets.text: Calorimetry means the measurement of heat.
the heat lost by the hot body is equal to the heat gained by the colder body, provided no heat escapes to the surroundings.

At steady state, heat lost by the aluminium sphere = heat gained by water + heat gained by calorimeter.

summary: This section defines calorimetry as the measurement of heat and explains the principle of calorimetry, where heat lost equals heat gained in an isolated system. It describes the structure of a calorimeter and demonstrates its application in determining specific heat capacity.
