title: RESOLUTION OF VECTORS

evidence_snippets.text: The analytical method involves adding vectors by combining their respective components.
R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 2AB \cos \theta} ... This is the Law of Cosines.
The Law of Cosines and Law of Sines can be applied to find the resultant velocity of objects.

summary: This section introduces the analytical method of vector addition by algebraically combining components in two and three dimensions. It also derives the Law of Cosines and Law of Sines to determine the magnitude and direction of a resultant vector, demonstrating their application to real-world velocity problems.
title: VECTOR ADDITION – ANALYTICAL METHOD

evidence_snippets.text: The position vector r of a particle P in an x-y plane is r = xi + yj.
the direction of velocity at any point on the path of an object is tangential to the path at that point
Instantaneous acceleration a is the limit of average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero

summary: This section extends the kinematic concepts of position, displacement, velocity, and acceleration to two-dimensional motion in a plane. It defines these quantities in vector component form and explains how to determine their magnitudes, directions, and graphical representations.
title: MOTION IN A PLANE

evidence_snippets.text: The distribution of electrons among various molecular orbitals is called the electronic configuration of the molecule.
Bond order (b.o.) = \frac{1}{2}(N_b - N_a)
The bond length decreases as bond order increases.
If all the molecular orbitals in a molecule are doubly occupied, the substance is diamagnetic... if one or more molecular orbitals are singly occupied it is paramagnetic

summary: This section explains how the electronic configuration of a molecule determines its stability, bond order, bond length, and magnetic properties. It defines bond order and relates it to bond strength and length, while also explaining how electron occupancy in molecular orbitals dictates magnetic behavior.
title: Electronic Configuration and Molecular Behaviour

evidence_snippets.text: v = v_0 + a t
motion in a plane (two dimensions) can be treated as two separate, simultaneous one-dimensional motions

summary: This section derives the vector equations of motion for an object moving in a plane with constant acceleration. It establishes that two-dimensional motion can be resolved into two independent, simultaneous one-dimensional motions along perpendicular axes.
title: MOTION IN A PLANE WITH CONSTANT ACCELERATION

evidence_snippets.text: Projectile motion is the result of two independent, simultaneous components: a horizontal component with zero acceleration and a vertical component with constant acceleration.
the path of the projectile is a parabola
The total time Tf during which the projectile is in flight... is known as the time of flight

summary: This section introduces projectile motion as the combination of independent horizontal motion with constant velocity and vertical motion with constant acceleration due to gravity. It derives the parabolic trajectory equation and formulas for key parameters including time of flight, maximum height, and horizontal range.
title: PROJECTILE MOTION

evidence_snippets.text: electronic configuration of hydrogen molecule is H2 : (σ 1s)2
The bond order of He2 is 1/2(2 - 2) = 0. He2 molecule is therefore unstable and does not exist.
double bond in C2 consists of both pi bonds because of the presence of four electrons in two pi molecular orbitals.
it contains two unpaired electrons in π* 2px and π* 2py molecular orbitals, therefore, O2 molecule should be paramagnetic
for B2, C2, and N2, there is 2s-2p mixing which changes the energy order of σ 2pz and π 2p orbitals

summary: This section applies molecular orbital theory to analyze the bonding, electronic configurations, and magnetic properties of homonuclear diatomic molecules from H2 to Ne2. It demonstrates how bond order calculations predict molecular stability and explains the physical basis of phenomena like the paramagnetism of oxygen and 2s-2p orbital mixing.
title: BONDING IN SOME HOMONUCLEAR DIATOMIC MOLECULES

evidence_snippets.text: hydrogen bond can be defined as the attractive force which binds a hydrogen atom of one molecule with the electronegative atom (F, O or N)
When hydrogen is bonded to a strongly electronegative element 'X', the electron pair shared between the two atoms moves far away
Intermolecular hydrogen bond: It is formed between two different molecules of the same or different compounds.

summary: This section introduces hydrogen bonding, explaining its definition, cause of formation, and classification. It details how highly electronegative atoms bonded to hydrogen create polar molecules that engage in intermolecular or intramolecular hydrogen bonding.
title: HYDROGEN BONDING

evidence_snippets.text: The VSEPR model is a tool for predicting molecular geometry based on the repulsion between electron pairs.
Valence Bond (VB) theory explains covalent bonding through the lens of atomic orbital overlap and system energetics.
This process involves the mathematical mixing of atomic orbitals to create new, equivalent hybrid orbitals like $sp$, $sp^2$, and $sp^3$.
Molecular Orbital (MO) theory views bonding as the combination of atomic orbitals to form molecular orbitals that encompass the entire molecule.

summary: This section provides a comprehensive summary of chemical bonding theories and molecular structures. It covers core concepts such as Lewis structures, VSEPR theory, Valence Bond Theory, hybridisation, Molecular Orbital Theory, and hydrogen bonding.
title: Summary of Chemical Bonding and Molecular Structure

evidence_snippets.text: Thermodynamics is the study of energy transformations.
Universe = System + Surroundings
The mathematical statement of the first law of thermodynamics: ΔU = q + w

summary: This section introduces fundamental thermodynamic terms, defining the system, surroundings, and boundaries, and classifying systems as open, closed, or isolated. It explains state functions like temperature, volume, and internal energy, and establishes the IUPAC sign conventions for heat and work. Finally, it presents the First Law of Thermodynamics, expressing the conservation of energy mathematically as the change in internal energy equal to heat plus work.
title: Thermodynamic Terms

evidence_snippets.text: Many chemical reactions involve mechanical work, specifically pressure-volume ($PV$) work.
A process is reversible if it can be reversed at any moment by an infinitesimal change, proceeding through a series of equilibrium states.
Free expansion is defined as the expansion of a gas into a vacuum, where the external pressure ($p_{ex}$) is zero.

summary: This section explains and derives pressure-volume (PV) work under various thermodynamic conditions, including constant external pressure, multi-step finite changes, and reversible processes. It provides mathematical formulations for isothermal reversible expansion of an ideal gas and analyzes free expansion into a vacuum using the first law of thermodynamics.
title: Applications

evidence_snippets.text: It is defined as: H = U + pV
the heat absorbed by the system at constant pressure is equal to the enthalpy change.
Delta H is negative for exothermic reactions... Delta H is positive for endothermic reactions.
Delta H = Delta U + Delta n_g RT

summary: This section introduces enthalpy as a thermodynamic state function defined by the equation H = U + pV, representing heat flow at constant pressure. It derives the relationship between enthalpy change and internal energy change for general processes and gaseous reactions, and distinguishes between exothermic and endothermic reactions.
title: Enthalpy, H

evidence_snippets.text: An extensive property is one whose value depends on the quantity or size of matter present in the system.
An intensive property is independent of the amount of matter present.
A molar property, $\chi_m = \frac{\chi}{n}$, is the value of an extensive property $\chi$ for 1 mole of the substance and is an intensive property.

summary: This section defines and distinguishes between extensive and intensive thermodynamic properties based on their dependence on the quantity of matter. It also explains how molar properties are intensive and illustrates these concepts using a partitioned gas system.
title: Extensive and Intensive Properties

evidence_snippets.text: Heat capacity ($C$) is a measure of the heat required to raise the temperature of a system.
Molar heat capacity ($C_m$) is the heat capacity for one mole of a substance, defined as $C_m = \frac{C}{n}$.
Specific heat, also called specific heat capacity is the quantity of heat required to raise the temperature of one unit mass
At constant volume, the heat capacity is denoted by $C_v$
at constant pressure, it is denoted by $C_p$
Dividing by $\Delta T$ yields $C_p = C_v + R$, or $C_p - C_v = R$.

summary: This section defines heat capacity and distinguishes between molar and specific heat capacities. It establishes the mathematical relationships for heat transfer under constant volume and constant pressure conditions, and derives the relationship $C_p - C_v = R$ for an ideal gas.
title: Heat Capacity

evidence_snippets.text: Energy changes associated with chemical or physical processes can be measured using an experimental technique called calorimetry.
For chemical reactions, heat absorbed at constant volume is measured in a bomb calorimeter.
Since the bomb calorimeter is sealed, its volume does not change (\Delta V = 0)... energy changes measured at constant volume correspond to q_v = \Delta U.
Measurement of heat change at constant pressure... can be done in a simpler calorimeter. We know that \Delta H = q_p

summary: This section introduces calorimetry as an experimental technique to measure heat changes in chemical and physical processes. It details how internal energy change is measured at constant volume using a bomb calorimeter, and how enthalpy change is measured at constant pressure.
title: MEASUREMENT OF $\Delta U$ AND $\Delta H$: CALORIMETRY

evidence_snippets.text: Vector quantities have both magnitude and direction and obey special rules of vector algebra.
Two vectors A and B can be added graphically using the head-to-tail method or the parallelogram method.
A vector A can be resolved into components along two given vectors a and b in the same plane
Motion in a plane can be treated as the superposition of two separate simultaneous one-dimensional motions along two perpendicular directions.
An object in flight after being projected is a projectile.
When an object follows a circular path at constant speed, the motion of the object is called uniform circular motion.

summary: This section summarizes the kinematics of motion in a plane, covering vector algebra, vector resolution, and analytical addition. It applies these vector concepts to analyze two-dimensional motion, specifically focusing on projectile motion and uniform circular motion.
title: SUMMARY

evidence_snippets.text: The path length traversed by an object between two points is, in general, not the same as the magnitude of displacement.
the average speed of an object is greater than or equal to the magnitude of the average velocity over a given time interval.
The kinematic equations for uniform acceleration do not apply to the case of uniform circular motion.
resultant velocity $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}_1 + \mathbf{v}_2$. This must be distinguished from the relative velocity of object 1 with respect to object 2
The resultant acceleration of an object in circular motion is directed towards the centre (centripetal) only if the speed of the object is constant.
The shape of an object's trajectory is not determined by acceleration alone; it also depends on the initial conditions

summary: This section clarifies key conceptual distinctions in kinematics, such as path length versus displacement, average speed versus average velocity, and resultant versus relative velocity. It also highlights the limitations of kinematic equations in circular motion and explains how initial conditions shape an object's trajectory.
title: Points to Ponder

evidence_snippets.text: In previous chapters, the focus was on describing the motion of a particle quantitatively using velocity and acceleration (kinematics).
Common experience suggests that an external agency is required to change the state of a body.
The external agency of force can be in contact with the object (like hands, wind, or a stream) or it can act from a distance.

summary: This section introduces dynamics by discussing what governs the motion of bodies. It defines force as an external agency required to change a body's state of motion and distinguishes between contact and non-contact forces.
title: INTRODUCTION

evidence_snippets.text: An external force is required to keep a body in motion.
Aristotle's view is flawed because it fails to account for friction.

summary: This section examines Aristotle's fallacy, which incorrectly asserted that an external force is always required to keep a body in motion. It explains that this view is flawed because it fails to account for opposing forces like friction and viscosity present in the natural world.
title: ARISTOTLE’S FALLACY

evidence_snippets.text: an object moving on a frictionless horizontal plane should move with constant velocity, having neither acceleration nor retardation
The property of a body to resist change in its state of rest or uniform motion is called inertia.

summary: This section explores Galileo's experiments with inclined and double inclined planes, which challenged the Aristotelian view of motion. These observations led to the definition of inertia as a body's natural resistance to changing its state of rest or uniform motion.
title: THE LAW OF INERTIA

evidence_snippets.text: Every body continues to be in its state of rest or of uniform motion in a straight line unless compelled by some external force to act otherwise.
If the net external force on a body is zero, its acceleration is zero.
the rest of the body, being deformable and possessing inertia, tends to remain at its original position.

summary: This section introduces Newton's First Law of Motion, which states that an object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by a net external force. It explains the relationship between zero net external force and zero acceleration, explores the concept of inertia through everyday examples, and demonstrates how to infer forces from an object's state of motion.
title: NEWTON’S FIRST LAW OF MOTION

evidence_snippets.text: Momentum of a body is defined as the product of its mass $m$ and velocity $\mathbf{v}$.
The rate of change of momentum of a body is directly proportional to the applied force
One newton is the force that causes an acceleration of $1 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ to a mass of $1 \text{ kg}$.
This product is called impulse: Impulse = Force \times time duration = Change in momentum

summary: This section introduces Newton's second law of motion, defining linear momentum and establishing the quantitative relationship between net external force and acceleration. It explores the vector nature of force, its application to systems of particles, and introduces the concept of impulse.
title: NEWTON’S SECOND LAW OF MOTION

evidence_snippets.text: To every action, there is always an equal and opposite reaction.
Forces always occur in pairs. Force on a body A by B is equal and opposite to the force on the body B by A.
Action and reaction forces act on different bodies, not on the same body.

summary: This section introduces Newton's third law of motion, which states that forces always occur in equal and opposite pairs representing mutual interactions between bodies. It clarifies that action and reaction forces act simultaneously on different bodies, and explains how internal forces cancel out within a system.
title: NEWTON’S THIRD LAW OF MOTION

evidence_snippets.text: The enthalpy change accompanying a chemical reaction is called the reaction enthalpy, denoted by $\Delta_r H$.
The standard enthalpy of reaction ($\Delta_r H^\ominus$) is the enthalpy change for a reaction when all the participating substances are in their standard states.
If a reaction takes place in several steps then its standard reaction enthalpy is the sum of the standard enthalpies of the intermediate reactions

summary: This section defines reaction enthalpy and standard enthalpy, exploring enthalpy changes during phase transformations such as fusion, vaporization, and sublimation. It also introduces thermochemical equations, standard enthalpy of formation, and Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation for calculating overall reaction enthalpies.
title: ENTHALPY CHANGE, $\Delta_r H$ OF A REACTION – REACTION ENTHALPY

evidence_snippets.text: The law of conservation of momentum states that the total momentum of an isolated system of interacting particles is conserved.
By Newton's Third Law, \mathbf{F}_{AB} = -\mathbf{F}_{BA}.
\mathbf{p}'_A + \mathbf{p}'_B = \mathbf{p}_A + \mathbf{p}_B

summary: This section defines and derives the law of conservation of momentum for an isolated system of interacting particles. Using a two-body collision as an application, the law is proven using Newton's second and third laws of motion.
title: CONSERVATION OF MOMENTUM

evidence_snippets.text: Equilibrium of a particle in mechanics refers to the situation when the net external force on the particle is zero.
For three concurrent forces F1, F2, and F3, equilibrium requires the vector sum to be zero: F1 + F2 + F3 = 0.
In terms of components along the x, y, and z axes, this implies: sum F_ix = 0, sum F_iy = 0, and sum F_iz = 0.

summary: This section defines the equilibrium of a particle as the state where the net external force acting on it is zero. It details the conditions for equilibrium under two, three, and general n concurrent forces, and demonstrates how to solve equilibrium problems using component resolution and free-body diagrams.
title: EQUILIBRIUM OF A PARTICLE

evidence_snippets.text: Standard enthalpy of combustion is defined as the enthalpy change per mole (or per unit amount) of a substance when it undergoes complete combustion
Enthalpy of atomization is the enthalpy change on breaking one mole of bonds completely to obtain atoms in the gas phase.
Two terms are used in thermodynamics: (i) Bond dissociation enthalpy and (ii) Mean bond enthalpy.
The lattice enthalpy of an ionic compound is defined as the enthalpy change that occurs when one mole of an ionic compound dissociates
This cycle constructs an enthalpy diagram based on Hess's Law to calculate the lattice enthalpy from other measurable thermodynamic quantities.
The enthalpy of solution (\Delta_{sol}H^\ominus) is the enthalpy change when one mole of a substance dissolves in a specified amount of solvent.

summary: This section defines and explains various types of reaction enthalpies, including standard enthalpy of combustion, enthalpy of atomization, bond enthalpy, lattice enthalpy, and enthalpy of solution. It also introduces the Born-Haber cycle as an indirect method to calculate lattice enthalpy using Hess's Law.
title: ENTHALPIES FOR DIFFERENT TYPES OF REACTIONS

evidence_snippets.text: The gravitational force is pervasive; every object on Earth experiences it, and it governs celestial motion.
Other common forces are contact forces, which arise when bodies are in contact (e.g., a book on a table).
When a spring is compressed or extended, a restoring force is generated, usually proportional to the displacement: $F = -kx$
For an inextensible string, the restoring force is called tension ($T$).
most contact forces in mechanics (friction, tension, normal force) fundamentally arise from electrical forces between the charged constituents

summary: This section introduces the common forces encountered in mechanics, distinguishing between non-contact forces like gravity and contact forces such as normal reaction, friction, tension, and spring forces. It explains the mathematical formulation of spring force and tension, and concludes by explaining that macroscopic contact forces fundamentally arise from microscopic electromagnetic interactions.
title: COMMON FORCES IN MECHANICS

evidence_snippets.text: Spontaneity means 'having the potential to proceed without the assistance of external agency'.
Entropy is a measure of the degree of randomness or disorder in a system.
The third law of thermodynamics states: The entropy of any pure crystalline substance approaches zero as the temperature approaches absolute zero.

summary: This section introduces the concept of spontaneity in chemical and physical processes, demonstrating that neither enthalpy decrease nor entropy increase alone can fully predict spontaneous change. It defines entropy quantitatively, introduces the Second and Third Laws of Thermodynamics, and establishes Gibbs energy change as the definitive criterion for spontaneity at constant temperature and pressure.
title: SPONTANEITY

evidence_snippets.text: Friction is defined as the component of this contact force that acts parallel to the surfaces in contact.
The limiting value of static friction (fs)max is independent of the area of contact and varies with the normal force N as: (fs)max = mu_s N
This force, which opposes relative motion between surfaces in contact, is called kinetic or sliding friction (fk).
At the point where the block is about to slide, tan theta_max = mu_s. This maximum angle, often called the angle of repose...
real surfaces deform slightly, creating a contact area rather than a point, which results in rolling friction.

summary: This section introduces friction as the parallel component of contact force that opposes relative motion between surfaces. It defines and analyzes static, kinetic, and rolling friction, deriving their empirical laws and coefficients. The section also explores practical applications, including motion on inclined planes, accelerating frames, and methods to reduce or utilize friction.
title: Friction

evidence_snippets.text: In a chemical reaction, this means the reaction can proceed in either direction simultaneously, setting up a dynamic equilibrium.
The standard Gibbs energy change, $\Delta_r G^\ominus$, is related to the equilibrium constant $K$ as follows: $\Delta_r G^\ominus = -RT \ln K$
The spontaneity of a reaction depends on the signs of enthalpy and entropy changes as well as the temperature.

summary: This section explains the relationship between standard Gibbs energy change and the chemical equilibrium constant, demonstrating how to predict reaction spontaneity and calculate thermodynamic values. It also details how temperature, enthalpy, and entropy changes collectively determine whether a reaction is spontaneous.
title: GIBBS ENERGY CHANGE AND EQUILIBRIUM

evidence_snippets.text: the force $f_c$ required to provide this acceleration is $f_c = \frac{mv^2}{R}$. This force is called the centripetal force.
The centripetal force is provided by static friction $f$... This leads to a maximum safe speed $v_{max} = \sqrt{\mu_s Rg}$.
To reduce reliance on friction and allow higher speeds, roads are banked (tilted toward the center).
where friction is negligible (\mu_s = 0), the equation for maximum speed simplifies to v_o = (Rg \tan \theta)^{1/2}... optimum speed.

summary: This section analyzes the dynamics of circular motion, focusing on the centripetal force required to keep an object moving in a circular path. It derives the maximum safe speeds for vehicles negotiating turns on both level and banked roads, highlighting how banking reduces reliance on friction.
title: CIRCULAR MOTION

evidence_snippets.text: first law of thermodynamics: \Delta U = q + w
At constant pressure, enthalpy change (\Delta H) is used, where \Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT
Hess's law states that the enthalpy change for a chemical reaction is independent of the path
Entropy (S) measures disorder or randomness.
Gibbs energy (G) is defined as G = H - TS.

summary: This section provides a comprehensive summary of thermodynamics, covering the First Law, enthalpy, entropy, and Gibbs energy. It explains the criteria for spontaneity and equilibrium, as well as methods for calculating reaction enthalpies using Hess's law and bond enthalpies.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: choose a specific part of the assembly as the 'system' and treat the remaining parts as the 'environment'.
Draw a 'free-body diagram' (FBD) for this system, showing all external forces acting on it due to the environment
using Newton's third law to relate forces between interacting systems

summary: This section outlines a systematic methodology for solving mechanics problems by defining a system and its environment. It introduces the construction and application of free-body diagrams (FBDs) and demonstrates how to apply Newton's laws of motion to multi-body systems.
title: SOLVING PROBLEMS IN MECHANICS

evidence_snippets.text: This is a dynamic equilibrium state, not a static one, where the number of molecules leaving the liquid equals the number returning.
The mixture of reactants and products at this stage is called an equilibrium mixture.
the system reaches dynamic equilibrium, where the rates of the forward and reverse reactions are equal.
Equilibrium involving ions in aqueous solutions is specifically referred to as ionic equilibrium.

summary: This section introduces the concept of equilibrium in physical and chemical processes, highlighting its dynamic nature where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal. It classifies chemical equilibria based on reaction extent and introduces ionic equilibrium involving ions in aqueous solutions.
title: Equilibrium

evidence_snippets.text: Every body continues to be in its state of rest or of uniform motion in a straight line, unless compelled by some external force to act otherwise.
Momentum (p) is the product of mass (m) and velocity (v): p = mv.
Newton's second law states the rate of change of momentum is proportional to the applied force and occurs in its direction: F = dp/dt = ma.

Newton's third law states: 'To every action, there is always an equal and opposite reaction.'

summary: This section summarizes the fundamental laws of motion, starting with Galileo's law of inertia and Newton's first law. It defines momentum and impulse, and outlines Newton's second and third laws of motion, highlighting their vector nature and physical implications.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: Equilibrium in physical processes is best understood through phase transformation processes
Equilibrium is possible only in a closed system at a given temperature.
Both opposing processes occur at the same rate, resulting in a dynamic but stable condition.

summary: This section introduces the concept of equilibrium in physical processes, focusing on phase transformations and dissolution processes. It details the dynamic nature of solid-liquid, liquid-vapour, and solid-vapour equilibria, as well as the dissolution of solids and gases in liquids.
title: EQUILIBRIUM IN PHYSICAL PROCESSES

evidence_snippets.text: The force on a body A by body B is equal and opposite to the force on the body B by A.
The total momentum of an isolated system of particles is conserved.
Static friction $f_s$ opposes impending relative motion, while kinetic friction $f_k$ opposes actual relative motion.
Impulse = force $\times$ time = change in momentum

summary: This section summarizes key principles of mechanics, including the properties of Newton's third law, the law of conservation of momentum, and the laws governing static and kinetic friction. It also provides a summary table of essential mechanical quantities such as force, momentum, impulse, and friction.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: When the rates of the forward and reverse reactions become equal, the concentrations of the reactants and products remain constant.
This 'scrambling' of hydrogen and deuterium atoms proves that the forward and reverse reactions continue to occur even after equilibrium is reached.
Equilibrium can be attained from either the reactant side or the product side.

summary: This section introduces chemical equilibrium as a dynamic state where the rates of forward and reverse reactions are equal, resulting in constant concentrations of reactants and products. The dynamic nature of this state is demonstrated experimentally through isotope scrambling in the Haber process and modeled using a water transfer analogy. Additionally, it shows that equilibrium can be reached starting from either the reactant or product side.
title: EQUILIBRIUM IN CHEMICAL PROCESSES – DYNAMIC EQUILIBRIUM

evidence_snippets.text: F is always parallel to the acceleration a.
The force acting on a body at a specific time is determined solely by the conditions at its location at that exact moment.
'centripetal force' is not a distinct type of force but a label for whichever real force... provides the necessary inward radial acceleration

The terms 'action' and 'reaction' in the third law refer to simultaneous mutual forces; action does not cause reaction.

summary: This section provides critical conceptual clarifications on Newtonian mechanics, emphasizing the relationship between force, acceleration, and velocity, the instantaneous nature of force, the true meaning of centripetal force, the self-adjusting nature of static friction, and the simultaneity of action-reaction pairs.
title: POINTS TO PONDER

evidence_snippets.text: concentrations in an equilibrium mixture are related by the equilibrium equation: K_c = [C][D]/[A][B]
The equilibrium equation is also known as the law of mass action, where 'active mass' refers to concentration.
The equilibrium constant for a reverse reaction is the inverse of the equilibrium constant for the reaction in the forward direction.
On multiplying the equation by n... the equilibrium constant for the reaction is equal to K_c^n.

summary: This section introduces the Law of Chemical Equilibrium and defines the equilibrium constant ($K_c$) for reversible reactions. It explains how to formulate the equilibrium constant expression using stoichiometric coefficients and details how the constant changes when a reaction is reversed or multiplied by a factor.
title: LAW OF CHEMICAL EQUILIBRIUM AND EQUILIBRIUM CONSTANT

evidence_snippets.text: in physics, 'work' has a precise and definite meaning.
in physics, it is specifically defined as the capacity to do work.
starting with the mathematical prerequisite of the scalar product of vectors.

summary: This introductory section contrasts the everyday usage of the terms 'work', 'energy', and 'power' with their precise, rigorous definitions in physics. It establishes the foundation for studying these quantities, noting the mathematical prerequisite of the scalar product of vectors.
title: Introduction

evidence_snippets.text: In a homogeneous system, all the reactants and products are in the same phase.
For reactions involving gases, it is usually more convenient to express the equilibrium constant in terms of partial pressure.
the relationship is K_p = K_c(RT)^{\Delta n}

summary: This section defines homogeneous equilibria, where all reactants and products exist in the same phase. It introduces the gaseous equilibrium constant ($K_p$) and establishes its mathematical relationship with $K_c$ using the ideal gas equation.
title: HOMOGENEOUS EQUILIBRIA

evidence_snippets.text: The scalar product or dot product of any two vectors A and B, denoted as A . B, is defined as: A . B = AB cos ̸
Geometrically, the scalar product can be viewed as the product of the magnitude of one vector and the projection of the second vector onto the first.
their scalar product is: A . B = Ax Bx + Ay By + Az Bz.

summary: This section introduces the scalar (or dot) product of two vectors, defining it both algebraically and geometrically. It covers key algebraic properties such as commutativity and distributivity, derives the product in component form, and demonstrates how to calculate the angle and projection between vectors.
title: The Scalar Product

evidence_snippets.text: Equilibrium in a system having more than one phase is called heterogeneous equilibrium.
the molar concentration of a pure solid or liquid is constant (i.e., independent of the amount present).
The thermal dissociation of calcium carbonate is an important example of heterogeneous chemical equilibrium: CaCO3(s) <=> CaO(s) + CO2(g).
Equilibrium constants can be dimensionless if standard states are specified (1 bar for gases, 1 M for solutes).

summary: This section defines heterogeneous equilibrium as a system containing more than one phase and explains why pure solids and liquids are omitted from equilibrium constant expressions. It demonstrates these principles using examples like the thermal dissociation of calcium carbonate and the purification of nickel, while also discussing the units of equilibrium constants.
title: HETEROGENEOUS EQUILIBRIA

evidence_snippets.text: We define Kinetic Energy ($K$) as $K = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$.
We define Work ($W$) done by a force as the product of the displacement and the component of the force along the displacement
The Work-Energy (WE) Theorem states: The change in kinetic energy of a particle is equal to the work done on it by the net force.

summary: This section introduces the concepts of kinetic energy and work, deriving the Work-Energy Theorem for constant forces in both one and three dimensions. It also demonstrates the application of this theorem to physical systems involving gravitational and resistive forces.
title: Notions of Work and Kinetic Energy: The Work-Energy Theorem

evidence_snippets.text: Applications include predicting reaction extent, direction, and calculating equilibrium concentrations.
The magnitude of Kc or Kp indicates the extent of reaction but not the rate.
The reaction quotient Q helps predict reaction direction at any stage.

summary: This section details the practical applications of the equilibrium constant, focusing on predicting the extent and direction of a chemical reaction. It introduces the reaction quotient for determining reaction direction and provides a systematic methodology for calculating equilibrium concentrations from initial conditions.
title: APPLICATIONS OF EQUILIBRIUM CONSTANTS

evidence_snippets.text: the work done is defined as the scalar product of force and displacement: W = F . d = Fd cos theta.
No work is done if: (i) the displacement is zero... (ii) the force is zero... (iii) the force and displacement are mutually perpendicular
If theta is between 0 and 90, cos theta is positive. If theta is between 90 and 180, cos theta is negative.
Work and energy have the same dimensions, [ML^2T^-2]. The SI unit of these is joule (J)

summary: This section defines the physical concept of work done by a constant force as the scalar product of force and displacement. It details the conditions for positive, negative, and zero work, and establishes the dimensions and various units of work.
title: Work

evidence_snippets.text: If ΔG is negative, the reaction is spontaneous and proceeds in the forward direction.
ΔG = ΔG⊖ + RT ln Q
ΔG⊖ = -RT ln K
If ΔG⊖ < 0, then ... K > 1. This implies a spontaneous reaction where products are predominant.

summary: This section establishes the thermodynamic relationship between Gibbs energy change, the reaction quotient, and the equilibrium constant. It derives the fundamental equation relating standard Gibbs energy change to the equilibrium constant and explains how the sign of standard Gibbs energy determines the extent of a reaction.
title: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN EQUILIBRIUM CONSTANT K, REACTION QUOTIENT Q AND GIBBS ENERGY G

evidence_snippets.text: If an object of mass m has velocity v, its kinetic energy K is K = 1/2 m v . v = 1/2 mv^2.
Kinetic energy is a scalar quantity.
The kinetic energy of an object is a measure of the work an object can do by virtue of its motion.

summary: This section defines kinetic energy as a scalar quantity representing the work an object can perform by virtue of its motion, mathematically expressed as K = 1/2 m v^2. It provides typical kinetic energy values for various objects and demonstrates how to calculate changes in speed resulting from a reduction in kinetic energy through a practical ballistics example.
title: KINETIC ENERGY

evidence_snippets.text: When the concentration of any of the reactants or products in a reaction at equilibrium is changed, the composition of the equilibrium mixture changes so as to minimize the effect of concentration changes.
The concentration stress of an added reactant/product is relieved by a net reaction in the direction that consumes the added substance.
In the manufacture of ammonia, the product is liquefied and removed from the reaction mixture to ensure the reaction continuously moves in the forward direction.

summary: This section introduces Le Chatelier's principle and examines how changing concentration conditions disturbs chemical equilibria. It explains the theoretical basis of concentration stress, demonstrates its industrial applications in maximizing product yield, and provides an experimental verification using the iron(III)-thiocyanate system.
title: FACTORS AFFECTING EQUILIBRIA

evidence_snippets.text: for a varying force the work done can be expressed as a definite integral of force over displacement.
The work done is calculated as the area under the force-displacement graph.
Newton's second law... is expressed in vector form... In contrast, the work-energy theorem is formulated in scalar form.

summary: This section explains how to calculate the work done by a variable force using graphical analysis and integration. It represents work as the area under a force-displacement curve and compares the scalar work-energy theorem with the vector form of Newton's second law.
title: WORK DONE BY A VARIABLE FORCE

evidence_snippets.text: increasing the pressure (by decreasing the volume) shifts the equilibrium in the direction that involves a decrease in the number of moles of gas.
Since $Q_c < K_c$, the reaction proceeds in the forward direction to re-establish equilibrium.
the effect of pressure changes on solids and liquids can be ignored because their volume and concentration are nearly independent of pressure.

summary: This section explains how changes in pressure, typically achieved by altering volume, affect chemical equilibrium in gaseous and heterogeneous systems. Applying Le Chatelier's principle, it demonstrates that increasing pressure shifts the equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas, supported by both mathematical analysis of the reaction quotient and qualitative examples.
title: Effect of Pressure Change

evidence_snippets.text: potential energy is the energy stored in a body due to its position or configuration.
The work done by an external agent to lift the object to a height h is mgh, which is stored as gravitational potential energy V(h) = mgh.
Forces like gravity, where the work done depends only on the initial and final positions and not the path, are called conservative forces.
force F(x) can be expressed as the negative derivative of the potential: F(x) = -dV/dx.

summary: This section introduces potential energy as the energy stored in a body due to its position or configuration, focusing on gravitational potential energy. It establishes the mathematical relationship between conservative forces and potential energy, defining conservative forces as those where work done is path-independent.
title: THE CONCEPT OF POTENTIAL ENERGY

evidence_snippets.text: If the volume of the reaction vessel is kept constant and an inert gas such as argon is added, the equilibrium remains undisturbed.
addition of an inert gas at constant volume does not change the partial pressures or the molar concentrations of the reactants and products

summary: This section explains the impact of adding an inert gas to a chemical system at equilibrium. It specifically demonstrates that adding an inert gas at constant volume has no effect on the equilibrium state because the partial pressures and molar concentrations of the reactants and products remain unchanged.
title: Effect of Inert Gas Addition

evidence_snippets.text: This constant sum is the total mechanical energy of the system.

summary: This section introduces the principle of conservation of total mechanical energy, demonstrating that the sum of kinetic and potential energy remains constant under conservative forces. It defines conservative and non-conservative forces, and applies these principles to analyze a freely falling body and a bob undergoing vertical circular motion.
title: THE CONSERVATION OF MECHANICAL ENERGY

evidence_snippets.text: a change in temperature actually changes the value of $K_c$.

For an endothermic reaction (positive $\Delta H$), the equilibrium constant increases as temperature increases.
low temperature is thermodynamically favourable for high yield, but practically, very low temperatures slow down the reaction rate too much

summary: This section explains how temperature changes affect the equilibrium constant and composition of a reaction depending on whether it is exothermic or endothermic. These principles are demonstrated through the industrial synthesis of ammonia and experimental observations of nitrogen dioxide and cobalt complex equilibria.

evidence_snippets.text: This force law is called Hooke’s law and is mathematically stated as: $F_s = -kx$

For an extension or compression $x$, the potential energy is: $V(x) = \frac{kx^2}{2}$

summary: This section introduces Hooke's law and derives the work done by a spring force to define the potential energy of a spring. It demonstrates the conservation of mechanical energy in spring-mass systems and extends the work-energy theorem to include non-conservative forces like friction.
title: THE POTENTIAL ENERGY OF A SPRING

evidence_snippets.text: A catalyst increases the rate of a chemical reaction by providing a new low-energy pathway with a lower activation energy.

summary: This section explains the role of a catalyst in chemical equilibrium, demonstrating that it accelerates both forward and reverse reactions equally without altering the equilibrium composition or constant. It also illustrates these principles using key industrial applications, specifically the Haber and Contact processes.

evidence_snippets.text: Power is defined as the time rate at which work is done or energy is transferred.
P = F . v

It derives the relationship between power, force, and velocity, and introduces standard units of power and energy such as the watt, horsepower, and kilowatt-hour.

evidence_snippets.text: Michael Faraday classified substances into electrolytes (which conduct electricity in aqueous solution) and non-electrolytes

evidence_snippets.text: In an elastic collision, kinetic energy is conserved.

It derives mathematical formulations for both one-dimensional and two-dimensional collisions, and extends the concept to non-contact scattering processes.
title: COLLISIONS

evidence_snippets.text: The reaction between an acid and a base produces a salt.

summary: This section covers the fundamental properties of acids, bases, and salts, explaining how water's high dielectric constant facilitates the dissolution of ionic solids. evidence_snippets.text: acids are substances that dissociate in water to release hydrogen ions

evidence_snippets.text: The work-energy theorem states that the change in kinetic energy of a body is the work done by the net force: $K_f - K_i = W_{net}$.
A force is conservative if the work done by it is path-independent and depends only on endpoints, or if the work done over any closed path is zero.

summary: This section summarizes the fundamental principles of work, energy, and power, including the work-energy theorem, conservative forces, and potential energy. It also reviews the scalar product of vectors and lists the dimensions and units of key physical quantities.

evidence_snippets.text: Work is a scalar but can be negative (e.g., friction).
The Work-Energy (WE) theorem is a scalar form of Newton's Second Law and holds in all inertial frames.
Potential energy is relative; its zero point is a matter of choice
In collisions, linear momentum is conserved at every instant, but kinetic energy conservation in elastic collisions applies only to the states before and after

summary: This section highlights critical conceptual nuances in mechanics, focusing on the properties of work, potential energy, and conservation laws. It clarifies the application of the Work-Energy theorem across inertial frames and distinguishes between momentum and kinetic energy conservation during collisions.
title: Points to Ponder

evidence_snippets.text: an acid is a proton ($H^+$) donor, and a base is a proton acceptor.
An acid and a base that differ by exactly one proton are known as a conjugate acid-base pair.
Water exhibits a dual role, acting as both an acid and a base depending on the reactant.

summary: This section introduces the Brönsted-Lowry theory of acids and bases, defining them in terms of proton transfer. It explains the concept of conjugate acid-base pairs and discusses amphoteric substances, such as water, which can act as both acids and bases.
title: The Brönsted-Lowry Acids and Bases

evidence_snippets.text: An extended body is, in the first place, a system of particles.
Ideally, a rigid body is a body with a perfectly definite and unchanging shape.
In pure translational motion, at any instant of time, all particles of the body have the same velocity.
The rolling motion of a cylinder down an inclined plane is a combination of rotation about a fixed axis and translation.

summary: This section introduces the transition from point mass mechanics to the study of extended systems, specifically defining rigid bodies. It categorizes rigid body motion into pure translation, pure rotation about a fixed or non-fixed axis, and combined motion such as rolling.
title: Introduction

evidence_snippets.text: Strong acids like perchloric acid ($HClO_4$), hydrochloric acid ($HCl$)... dissociate almost completely into their constituent ions in water
Equilibrium shifts toward the direction of the weaker acid and weaker base because the stronger acid has a greater tendency to donate a proton.
Since the concentration of water remains constant in pure liquid form, it is incorporated into the constant to give the ionic product of water, $K_w$
The pH scale is a logarithmic scale used to express hydronium ion concentration. It is defined as the negative logarithm to base 10 of the activity

summary: This section covers the ionization of acids and bases in aqueous media, explaining the behavior of strong and weak electrolytes and conjugate acid-base pairs. It introduces the autoionization of water, the ionic product constant ($K_w$), and the logarithmic pH scale used to measure acidity and basicity.
title: IONIZATION OF ACIDS AND BASES

evidence_snippets.text: The centre of mass of a system of particles is a specific point that represents the average position of the mass.
\vec{R} = \frac{\sum m_i \vec{r}_i}{M}
As n \to \infty and \Delta m_i \to 0, the sums become integrals: X = \frac{1}{M} \int x dm
For homogeneous bodies (uniform mass distribution) of regular shapes, the centre of mass lies at their geometric centres.

summary: This section introduces the concept of the centre of mass as the average position of a system's mass. It derives mathematical formulations for discrete particle systems in one, two, and three dimensions, and extends these to continuous rigid bodies using integration and symmetry principles.
title: Centre of Mass

evidence_snippets.text: The equilibrium constant for this acid-dissociation, called the ionization constant $K_a$, is given by: $K_a = \frac{[H_3O^+][X^-]}{[HX]}$
The pH scale for hydrogen ion concentration is extended to ionization constants using the $pK_a$ notation: $pK_a = -\log(K_a)$.
Percent dissociation is another measure of the strength of a weak acid

summary: This section introduces the acid ionization constant ($K_a$) and the $pK_a$ scale to quantitatively measure the strength of weak acids. It provides a systematic step-wise methodology to calculate the degree of ionization, equilibrium concentrations, percent dissociation, and pH of weak acid solutions.
title: Ionization Constants of Weak Acids

evidence_snippets.text: the equilibrium constant for base ionization is called the base ionization constant, K_b
pK_b = -log(K_b)
If c is the initial concentration and alpha is the degree of ionization, then K_b = c*alpha^2 / (1 - alpha)

summary: This section covers the ionization of weak bases in aqueous solutions, defining the base ionization constant ($K_b$) and its logarithmic representation ($pK_b$). It details how to calculate these constants, the degree of ionization, and the pH of weak base solutions, including mixtures with their conjugate acids.
title: Ionization of Weak Bases

evidence_snippets.text: This can be rewritten as $M\mathbf{V} = m_1\mathbf{v}_1 + m_2\mathbf{v}_2 + \dots + m_n\mathbf{v}_n$
$M\mathbf{A} = m_1\mathbf{a}_1 + m_2\mathbf{a}_2 + \dots + m_n\mathbf{a}_n$
According to Newton's third law, internal forces occur in equal and opposite pairs, so their contribution to the total sum is zero.
$M\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{F}_{ext}$
the centre of mass of a system of particles moves as if all the mass of the system was concentrated at the centre of mass

key_terms: Newton's second law for a system of particles
summary: This section derives and explains the motion of the centre of mass for a system of particles. It establishes that the velocity and acceleration of the centre of mass depend on individual particle motions, and shows that the centre of mass moves under the influence of external forces alone, as if the entire mass were concentrated at that point.
title: Motion of Centre of Mass

evidence_snippets.text: for any conjugate acid-base pair, K_a x K_b = K_w.
The equilibrium constant for a net reaction obtained after adding two (or more) reactions equals the product of the equilibrium constants
By taking the negative logarithm of both sides of the equation K_a x K_b = K_w, we obtain: pK_a + pK_b = pK_w.

summary: This section establishes the mathematical relationship between the ionization constants of a conjugate acid-base pair, showing that their product equals the auto-ionization constant of water. It also generalizes the rule for equilibrium constants of net reactions and derives the logarithmic relationship between pKa and pKb.
title: Relation between Ka and Kb

evidence_snippets.text: the total linear momentum P is the vector sum of individual momenta: P = p1 + p2 + ... = MV
the rate of change of total linear momentum of a system is equal to the net external force acting on it.
If the sum of external forces acting on a system is zero, then P = Constant.

summary: This section defines the total linear momentum of a system of particles and relates it to the velocity of its centre of mass. It extends Newton's second law to show that the rate of change of total momentum equals the net external force, leading to the law of conservation of linear momentum when external forces are zero.
title: Linear Momentum of a System of Particles

evidence_snippets.text: The vector product (or cross product) of two vectors a and b is a vector c defined by: (i) Magnitude c = ab sin theta
if a right-handed screw is turned from a to b, the tip advances in the direction of c.
The vector product is not commutative, a x b != b x a.
i x j = k, j x k = i, k x i = j. These follow the cyclic order.
This is easily remembered in determinant form

summary: This section defines the vector product (or cross product) of two vectors, detailing its magnitude, direction, and key algebraic properties such as non-commutativity and distributivity. It explains the vector product of orthogonal unit vectors and demonstrates how to compute the cross product using a determinant representation in component form.
title: Vector Product of Two Vectors

evidence_snippets.text: Acids like oxalic acid ($H_2C_2O_4$), sulphuric acid ($H_2SO_4$), and phosphoric acid ($H_3PO_4$) have more than one ionizable proton per molecule
The equilibrium constants for the stepwise ionization of a polyprotic acid are denoted as $K_{a_1}, K_{a_2}, K_{a_3}$, etc.
Higher order ionization constants ($K_{a_2}, K_{a_3}$) are typically smaller than the first ionization constant ($K_{a_1}$).

summary: This section introduces polybasic (or polyprotic) acids and polyacidic bases, describing their stepwise ionization process. It explains how successive ionization constants decrease in value due to electrostatic forces.
title: Di- and Polybasic Acids and Di- and Polyacidic Bases

evidence_snippets.text: The extent of dissociation of an acid depends on the strength and polarity of the H-A bond.
As the size of atom A increases down the group, the H-A bond strength decreases, leading to an increase in acid strength.
As the electronegativity of atom A increases across the row, the polarity of the H-A bond increases, resulting in higher acid strength.

summary: This section explains how bond strength and bond polarity determine the extent of acid dissociation. It describes periodic trends, showing that acid strength increases down a group due to decreasing bond strength and increases across a row due to increasing bond polarity.
title: Factors Affecting Acid Strength

evidence_snippets.text: In circular motion, the magnitude of linear velocity v is related to the angular velocity ΔΘ by the simple relation v = ΔΘ r
Pure rotation is characterized by all parts of the body having the same angular velocity at any instant of time.
The linear velocity v of a particle at position r is given by the vector product v = ΔΘ x r.

summary: This section defines angular velocity and angular acceleration for a rotating rigid body, establishing both scalar and vector relationships with linear velocity. It demonstrates that all particles in a rigid body share a common angular velocity and expresses the linear velocity vector as the cross product of angular velocity and the position vector.
title: Angular Velocity and its Relation with Linear Velocity

evidence_snippets.text: The common ion effect occurs when the concentration of an ion involved in an equilibrium is increased by adding it from an external source.
common ion effect is a phenomenon based on the Le Chatelier's principle
To evaluate the pH of a solution resulting from the addition of $0.05M$ acetate ion to $0.05M$ acetic acid solution

summary: This section defines the common ion effect as the suppression of the ionization of a weak electrolyte by the addition of an ion already present in the equilibrium. It explains this phenomenon using Le Chatelier's principle and demonstrates how to calculate the pH of acidic and basic solutions in the presence of a common ion.
title: Common Ion Effect in the Ionization of Acids and Bases

evidence_snippets.text: Torque is the rotational analogue of force.
the torque \boldsymbol{\tau} is defined as: \boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{F}.
angular momentum \mathbf{l} is defined as: \mathbf{l} = \mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{p}.
d\mathbf{l}/dt = \boldsymbol{\tau}
If the total external torque on a system of particles is zero... the total angular momentum remains constant.

summary: This section introduces torque and angular momentum as the rotational analogues of force and linear momentum, defining them mathematically using vector products. It derives the fundamental dynamical relationships between torque and angular momentum for both single particles and systems of particles, culminating in the law of conservation of angular momentum.
title: Torque and Angular Momentum

evidence_snippets.text: The cations or anions formed may exist as hydrated ions or interact with water to reform corresponding acids or bases, a process called hydrolysis.
Salts of strong bases and strong acids (e.g., Na+, K+, Cl-, NO3-) only get hydrated and do not hydrolyse; their solutions are neutral (pH = 7).
The acetate ion undergoes hydrolysis: CH3COO-(aq) + H2O(l) ⇌ CH3COOH(aq) + OH-(aq).
The ammonium ion undergoes hydrolysis: NH4+(aq) + H2O(l) ⇌ NH4OH(aq) + H+(aq).
The pH is determined by pKa and pKb values: pH = 7 + 1/2(pKa - pKb).

summary: This section explains salt hydrolysis, where cations or anions of a salt interact with water to affect the solution's pH. It details the hydrolysis behavior and pH determination for salts derived from various combinations of strong and weak acids and bases.
title: Hydrolysis of Salts and the pH of their Solutions

evidence_snippets.text: A rigid body is said to be in mechanical equilibrium if both its linear momentum and angular momentum are not changing with time.
Translational Equilibrium: The vector sum of external forces is zero
Rotational Equilibrium: The vector sum of external torques is zero
A rigid body can be in partial equilibrium, meaning it may satisfy the conditions for translational equilibrium but not rotational equilibrium
A pair of forces of equal magnitude but acting in opposite directions with different lines of action is known as a couple

summary: This section defines the conditions for the mechanical equilibrium of a rigid body, which requires both translational and rotational equilibrium. It explores the scalar equations of equilibrium, contrasts rigid body equilibrium with particle equilibrium, and introduces the concepts of partial equilibrium and couples.
title: Equilibrium of a Rigid Body

evidence_snippets.text: Solutions which resist change in pH on dilution or with the addition of small amounts of acid or alkali are called Buffer Solutions.
An acidic buffer is prepared using a weak acid ($HA$) and its salt with a strong base.
This gives the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation: $pH = pK_a + \log \frac{[Salt]}{[Acid]}$.
For a basic buffer (weak base $B$ and its conjugate acid $BH^+$), the relationship is: $pOH = pK_b + \log \frac{[Conjugate acid, BH^+]}{[Base, B]}$.

summary: This section defines buffer solutions as mixtures that resist changes in pH upon dilution or the addition of small amounts of acid or alkali. It derives the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation for calculating the pH of acidic buffers and establishes the corresponding equations for basic buffers.
title: BUFFER SOLUTIONS

evidence_snippets.text: An ideal lever is a light rod (negligible mass) pivoted at a fixed point called the fulcrum.
For translational equilibrium: R - F1 - F2 = 0. For rotational equilibrium about the fulcrum: d1 F1 - d2 F2 = 0.
The principle of moments for a lever states that in equilibrium: load arm x load = effort arm x effort
The Mechanical Advantage (M.A.) is defined as the ratio of the load to the effort: M.A. = F1/F2 = d2/d1.

summary: This section introduces the lever as a simple machine and explains its equilibrium conditions. It defines the principle of moments and mechanical advantage, demonstrating how a lever can be used to lift heavy loads with minimal effort.
title: Principle of moments

evidence_snippets.text: Solubility depends on factors such as lattice enthalpy and solvation enthalpy.
Salts are classified into three categories based on their molar solubility: Soluble, Slightly Soluble, and Sparingly Soluble.
The equilibrium constant is K = {[Ba2+][SO42-]} / [BaSO4]... leading to the solubility product constant, Ksp
Based on Le Chatelier's principle, increasing the concentration of an ion in a saturated solution causes precipitation until Qsp = Ksp.
Solubility of salts of weak acids increases at lower pH because the anion concentration decreases due to protonation.

summary: This section covers the solubility equilibria of sparingly soluble salts, introducing the solubility product constant (Ksp) and its relation to molar solubility. It also discusses factors affecting solubility, including lattice and solvation enthalpies, the common ion effect, and pH variations.
title: SOLUBILITY EQUILIBRIA OF SPARINGLY SOLUBLE SALTS

evidence_snippets.text: The centre of gravity (CG) of a body is the point where the entire weight of the body is considered to act.
the centre of gravity coincides with the centre of mass in a uniform gravitational field.
The CG of an irregular flat body can be found by suspending it from several points.

summary: This section defines the centre of gravity (CG) as the point where the entire weight of a body acts and where the net gravitational torque is zero. It explores the relationship between the centre of gravity and the centre of mass, explaining that they coincide in a uniform gravitational field. Additionally, it details experimental methods to locate the CG and applies equilibrium conditions to solve mechanical problems.
title: Centre of gravity

evidence_snippets.text: At equilibrium, the rates of the forward and reverse reactions are equal.
Le Chatelier’s principle states that if a change is applied to a system at equilibrium... the equilibrium will shift
Three main concepts define acids and bases: 1. Arrhenius Concept... 2. Brönsted-Lowry Concept... 3. Lewis Concept
The pH scale is used to express hydrogen ion concentration, defined as $pH = -\log[H^+]$.
Buffer solutions are those that resist changes in pH upon the addition of small amounts of acid or base.
Solubility equilibrium for sparingly soluble salts is described by the solubility product constant ($K_{sp}$).

summary: This section provides a comprehensive summary of chemical and physical equilibrium, detailing the equilibrium constant and Le Chatelier's principle. It also covers ionic equilibrium, including acid-base theories, the pH scale, buffer solutions, and the solubility product of sparingly soluble salts.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: oxidation is defined as the addition of oxygen or an electronegative element to a substance, or the removal of hydrogen or an electropositive element
reduction is defined as the removal of oxygen or an electronegative element from a substance, or the addition of hydrogen or an electropositive
Because these processes always occur together, the term 'redox' was coined to describe this class of chemical reactions.

summary: This section introduces the classical definitions of oxidation and reduction based on the transfer of oxygen, hydrogen, and electronegative or electropositive elements. It highlights that these two processes always occur simultaneously, forming the basis of redox reactions.
title: CLASSICAL IDEA OF REDOX REACTIONS – OXIDATION AND REDUCTION REACTIONS

evidence_snippets.text: The moment of inertia $I$ of a rigid body about a given axis is defined as $I = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i^2$.
The total kinetic energy $K$ of the body is the sum of the kinetic energies of all particles: $K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i
The radius of gyration $k$ of a body about an axis is the distance from the axis at which a point mass, equal to the total mass of the body

summary: This section introduces the moment of inertia as the rotational analogue of mass, characterizing a rigid body's resistance to rotational acceleration. It derives the rotational kinetic energy of a rigid body and defines the radius of gyration, while providing formulas for the moments of inertia of common geometric shapes.
title: MOMENT OF INERTIA

evidence_snippets.text: Many redox reactions involve the formation of ionic compounds through the transfer of electrons.
Half-reactions that involve loss of electrons are called oxidation reactions, while those involving gain of electrons are called reduction reactions.
The competition for the release of electrons among metals allows for the creation of a metal activity series or electrochemical series.

summary: This section defines redox reactions in terms of electron transfer, introducing oxidation as electron loss and reduction as electron gain. It explains how to split redox reactions into oxidation and reduction half-reactions and demonstrates competitive electron transfer among metals to establish the metal activity series.
title: REDOX REACTIONS IN TERMS OF ELECTRON TRANSFER REACTIONS

evidence_snippets.text: There is a direct analogy between rotational motion about a fixed axis and translational motion.
For rotational motion with uniform angular acceleration (Α = \text{constant}), the kinematic equations are analogous to those for linear motion.

summary: This section establishes a direct analogy between translational and rotational kinematics about a fixed axis. It introduces rotational kinematic variables and derives the kinematic equations for uniform angular acceleration.
title: KINEMATICS OF ROTATIONAL MOTION ABOUT A FIXED AXIS

evidence_snippets.text: In rotational motion, moment of inertia and torque play the same roles as mass and force, respectively, in linear motion.
The effective torque due to \mathbf{F}_1 is \tau_1 = r_1 F_1 \sin \alpha_1... Thus, dW_1 = \tau_1 d\theta.
The instantaneous power P is the rate at which work is done... P = \tau \omega.
Equating the rate of work done to the rate of increase in kinetic energy, \tau \omega = I \omega \alpha, which gives \tau = I \alpha.

summary: This section establishes the dynamics of rotational motion about a fixed axis by drawing analogies to linear motion. It derives the expressions for work done by a torque, rotational power, and the rotational analogue of Newton's second law.
title: DYNAMICS OF ROTATIONAL MOTION ABOUT A FIXED AXIS

evidence_snippets.text: To keep track of these shifts, the concept of oxidation number is used.
Oxidation number denotes the oxidation state of an element in a compound ascertained according to a set of rules...
In this system, the oxidation number of the metal is expressed as a Roman numeral in parentheses immediately following the symbol of the metal

summary: This section introduces the concept of oxidation number (or oxidation state) as a bookkeeping tool for electron shifts in compounds. It details the rules for assigning oxidation numbers, explains periodic trends in oxidation states, introduces Stock notation, and redefines redox processes in terms of oxidation number changes.
title: OXIDATION NUMBER

evidence_snippets.text: For such a reaction to be a redox reaction, at least one of the reactants (A or B) must be in its elemental form.
A decomposition is a redox reaction if at least one of the products is in the elemental state.
In a displacement reaction, an ion or atom in a compound is replaced by an ion or atom of another element
a single element in a specific oxidation state is simultaneously oxidized and reduced.
Fractional oxidation numbers (e.g., 4/3 for C in C3O2) are average values that do not reflect the actual state of individual atoms.

summary: This section classifies redox reactions into combination, decomposition, displacement, and disproportionation reactions, detailing their mechanisms and conditions. It also addresses the paradox of fractional oxidation numbers by demonstrating how structural analysis reveals distinct whole-number oxidation states in molecules and mixed oxides.
title: Types of Redox Reactions

evidence_snippets.text: The total angular momentum $\mathbf{L}$ of a system of $n$ particles is the vector sum of the angular momenta of individual particles
$\mathbf{L}_z = (\sum m_i r_i^2) \omega \mathbf{\hat{k}} = I \omega \mathbf{\hat{k}}$
for rotation about a fixed axis, the component of angular momentum perpendicular to the fixed axis remains constant.
$\tau = I\alpha$ (Eq. 6.41), which is the rotational analogue of Newton's second law ($F = ma$)

key_terms: Rotational Analogue of Newton's Second Law
summary: This section analyzes the angular momentum of a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis, decomposing it into components parallel and perpendicular to the axis. It derives the relation between the axial angular momentum, moment of inertia, and angular velocity, establishing the rotational analogue of Newton's second law.
title: ANGULAR MOMENTUM IN CASE OF ROTATION ABOUT A FIXED AXIS

evidence_snippets.text: A rigid body is defined as one where the distances between its constituent particles remain unchanged despite external forces.
The centre of mass (CM) of a system of n particles is the point whose position vector is R = \frac{\sum m_i \mathbf{r}_i}{M}.
the total torque is \boldsymbol{\tau} = \sum \mathbf{r}_i \times \mathbf{F}_i.
The total angular momentum of the system is \mathbf{L} = \sum \mathbf{r}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i
A rigid body is in mechanical equilibrium if it satisfies two conditions: (1) Translational equilibrium... and (2) Rotational equilibrium...
The moment of inertia I about an axis is defined as I = \sum m_i r_i^2

summary: This section summarizes the kinematics and dynamics of rigid bodies and systems of particles. It covers key concepts such as the centre of mass, torque, angular momentum, mechanical equilibrium, and moment of inertia.
title: Summary of Systems of Particles and Rotational Motion

evidence_snippets.text: Chemical equations for redox processes are balanced using two main methods: the Oxidation Number Method and the Half-Reaction Method.
The Oxidation Number Method relies on tracking changes in oxidation states
the Half-Reaction Method splits the process into oxidation and reduction components.

summary: This section explains the two primary methods for balancing redox reactions: the Oxidation Number Method and the Half-Reaction (Ion-Electron) Method. It provides step-by-step procedures and worked examples for balancing equations in both acidic and basic media.
title: Balancing of Redox Reactions

evidence_snippets.text: Redox titrations determine the strength of a reductant or oxidant using a redox-sensitive indicator.
Self-indicators: Reagents like permanganate ion (\text{MnO}_4^-) are intensely coloured.
External indicators: Reagents like diphenylamine are used when there is no dramatic auto-colour change
Specific indicators: Starch is used for titrations involving iodine.

summary: This section explains the principles of redox titrations, which are used to determine the concentration of reductants or oxidants. It details three primary methods for detecting the titration end-point: self-indicators, external indicators, and specific indicators like starch.
title: Redox Reactions as the Basis for Titrations

evidence_snippets.text: modern chemistry visualises oxidation as a decrease in electron density and reduction as an increase in electron density

summary: This section outlines the limitations of using formal oxidation numbers and explains the modern evolution of redox concepts. It defines oxidation and reduction in terms of changes in electron density around atoms.
title: Limitations of Concept of Oxidation Number

evidence_snippets.text: A redox couple is defined as having together the oxidised and reduced forms of a substance taking part in an oxidation or reduction half reaction.
The solutions are connected by a salt bridge, which is a U-tube containing a solution of potassium chloride (KCl) or ammonium nitrate (NH4NO3)
The potential associated with each electrode is known as electrode potential.

summary: This section introduces electrode processes by separating oxidation and reduction half-reactions into distinct compartments to form electrochemical cells like the Daniell cell. It defines redox couples, explains the role of a salt bridge, and introduces electrode potential and standard electrode potential as measures of the relative tendencies of species to undergo reduction or oxidation.
title: REDOX REACTIONS AND ELECTRODE PROCESSES

evidence_snippets.text: Redox reactions involve simultaneous oxidation and reduction.
redox equations can be balanced using the oxidation number or ion-electron methods.
Redox reactions are classified into four categories: combination, decomposition, displacement, and disproportionation.
The concepts of redox couples and electrode processes are fundamental to the study of electrochemical cells

summary: This section provides a comprehensive summary of redox reactions, detailing their conceptualization through electron transfer and oxidation numbers. It outlines the methods for balancing redox equations, classifies redox reactions into four main categories, and introduces redox couples and electrode processes.
title: SUMMARY

evidence_snippets.text: Carbon has the unique property called catenation, which allows it to form covalent bonds with other carbon atoms.
This theory was debunked in 1828 by F. Wohler, who synthesized urea (an organic compound) from an inorganic compound, ammonium cyanate, by heating

summary: This section introduces organic chemistry, highlighting carbon's unique ability to undergo catenation and form covalent bonds. It explores the biological and industrial importance of organic compounds and traces the historical shift from the Vital Force Theory to laboratory synthesis, initiated by F. Wohler's synthesis of urea.
title: GENERAL INTRODUCTION

evidence_snippets.text: Carbon atoms utilize sp3, sp2, and sp hybrid orbitals to form molecules.
An sp hybrid orbital has more s character (50%) compared to sp2 (33.3%) and sp3 (25%).
A higher s character in the hybrid orbitals leads to greater electronegativity
A pi bond is formed by the sideways overlap of parallel p orbitals on adjacent atoms.

summary: This section explains how the tetravalence of carbon and its hybridization states determine the shapes, bond lengths, bond enthalpies, and electronegativity of organic molecules. It also describes the formation, geometry, restricted rotation, and high reactivity of pi bonds in carbon compounds.
title: TETRAVALENCE OF CARBON: SHAPES OF ORGANIC COMPOUNDS

evidence_snippets.text: Complete structural formulas are based on Lewis structures where every two-electron covalent bond is represented by a dash (–).
Condensed structural formulas abbreviate the representation by omitting some or all of the dashes representing covalent bonds
In bond-line structural representations, carbon and hydrogen atoms are not explicitly shown. Carbon-carbon bonds are drawn as zig-zag lines.
A solid wedge indicates a bond projecting out of the plane of the paper towards the observer. A dashed wedge indicates a bond projecting... away
Molecular models are physical or virtual devices used to visualize the 3-D shapes of molecules.

summary: This section details the different methods used to represent organic molecules, including complete, condensed, and bond-line structural formulas. It also introduces the three-dimensional wedge-and-dash representation and various types of physical molecular models.
title: STRUCTURAL REPRESENTATIONS OF ORGANIC COMPOUNDS

evidence_snippets.text: Heterocyclic aromatic compounds are cyclic compounds that contain at least one heteroatom... within the aromatic ring.
A functional group is an atom or a group of atoms joined to the carbon chain... responsible for the characteristic chemical properties
A homologous series is a group or series of organic compounds that contain the same characteristic functional group

summary: This section explains the classification of organic compounds, focusing on heterocyclic aromatic compounds, functional groups, and homologous series. It defines these key classifications and describes how they determine the chemical properties and structural relationships of organic molecules.
title: CLASSIFICATION OF ORGANIC COMPOUNDS

evidence_snippets.text: Before the development of the IUPAC system, organic compounds were given trivial or common names based on their origin or specific properties.
The IUPAC system provides a systematic method where names are directly correlated with the chemical structure.
An alkyl group is formed by removing one hydrogen atom from a saturated hydrocarbon.
Rule 1: Identify the longest carbon chain in the molecule. This chain is designated as the parent or root chain.
A saturated monocyclic compound is named by adding the prefix 'cyclo-' to the name of the corresponding straight-chain alkane.

summary: This section introduces the nomenclature of organic compounds, contrasting historical trivial names with the systematic IUPAC system. It details the step-by-step rules for naming straight-chain, branched-chain, and cyclic alkanes, including the identification of parent chains, numbering of locants, and alphabetical ordering of substituents.
title: NOMENCLATURE OF ORGANIC COMPOUNDS

evidence_snippets.text: A functional group is an atom or a group of atoms bonded together in a unique manner that serves as the site of chemical reactivity.
In polyfunctional compounds, one functional group is chosen as the principal functional group, which determines the suffix.
The choice of the principal group follows a specific order of priority: -COOH > -SO3H > -COOR...
If more than one functional group of the same type is present, their number is indicated by adding numerical prefixes like 'di', 'tri', etc.
To derive the structural formula from an IUPAC name, identify the parent hydrocarbon chain length from the word root.

summary: This section outlines the IUPAC nomenclature rules for organic compounds containing one or more functional groups. It explains how to identify the principal functional group based on a priority hierarchy, name polyfunctional compounds, and derive structural formulas from IUPAC names.
title: Nomenclature of Organic Compounds having Functional Group(s)

evidence_snippets.text: For IUPAC nomenclature of substituted benzene compounds, the substituent is placed as a prefix to the word 'benzene'.
In the trivial (common) system, prefixes ortho (o), meta (m), and para (p) are used for 1,2-, 1,3-, and 1,4- positions respectively.
For tri- or higher substituted benzene derivatives, the ortho, meta, para prefixes cannot be used.
When a benzene ring is attached to an alkane chain that contains a functional group, the benzene ring is treated as a substituent... called a phenyl group

summary: This section explains the IUPAC and common nomenclature systems for monosubstituted, disubstituted, and polysubstituted benzene derivatives. It details the use of locant numbering, ortho/meta/para prefixes, and the designation of benzene as a phenyl substituent when attached to an alkane chain with a functional group.
title: Nomenclature of Substituted Benzene Compounds

evidence_snippets.text: The phenomenon of existence of two or more compounds possessing the same molecular formula but different properties is known as isomerism.
Isomerism is broadly classified into two types: Structural Isomerism and Stereoisomerism.
Structural isomerism includes chain isomerism, position isomerism, functional group isomerism, and metamerism.

summary: This section introduces the concept of isomerism, where compounds share the same molecular formula but exhibit different physical or chemical properties. It outlines the classification of isomerism into structural isomerism and stereoisomerism, along with their respective sub-types.
title: ISOMERISM

evidence_snippets.text: Compounds having the same molecular formula but different structures... are classified as structural isomers.
When two or more compounds have the same molecular formula but different carbon skeletons (branching), they are referred to as chain isomers.
When two or more compounds differ in the position of a substituent atom or functional group on the same carbon skeleton, they are called position isomers.
Two or more compounds having the same molecular formula but different functional groups are called functional isomers.
