Metamerism arises due to different alkyl chains on either side of the functional group in the molecule.

summary: This section defines structural isomerism and explains its various types. It covers chain isomerism, position isomerism, functional group isomerism, and metamerism, providing specific chemical examples for each type.
title: Structural Isomerism

evidence_snippets.text: The compounds that have the same constitution and sequence of covalent bonds but differ in relative positions of their atoms or groups in space
This special type of isomerism is called stereoisomerism and can be classified as geometrical and optical isomerism.

summary: This section defines stereoisomerism, where compounds share the same chemical constitution and covalent bond sequence but differ in the spatial arrangement of their atoms or groups. It also introduces the classification of stereoisomerism into geometrical and optical isomerism.
title: Stereoisomerism

evidence_snippets.text: Organic molecule (Substrate) + Attacking Reagent → [Intermediate] → Product(s) + Byproducts.
A sequential account of each step, describing details of electron movement, energetics during bond cleavage and bond formation

summary: This section introduces the fundamental framework of organic reactions, detailing the general reaction scheme involving substrates, attacking reagents, intermediates, and products. It defines a reaction mechanism as the step-by-step description of electron movement, energetics, and kinetics during bond cleavage and formation.
title: FUNDAMENTAL CONCEPTS IN ORGANIC REACTION MECHANISM

evidence_snippets.text: In heterolytic cleavage, the bond breaks in such a fashion that the shared pair of electrons remains with one of the fragments.
A species having a carbon atom possessing sextet of electrons and a positive charge is called a carbocation
Such a carbon species carrying a negative charge on carbon atom is called carbanion.
In homolytic cleavage, one of the electrons of the shared pair in a covalent bond goes with each of the bonded atoms.
Such cleavage results in the formation of neutral species (atom or group) which contains an unpaired electron, called free radicals.

summary: This section explains the two primary modes of covalent bond fission: heterolytic and homolytic cleavage. It details the formation, structure, and stability of the resulting reactive intermediates, namely carbocations, carbanions, and free radicals.
title: Fission of a Covalent Bond

evidence_snippets.text: A molecule whose carbon is involved in new bond formation is called substrate and the other one is called reagent.
A reagent that brings an electron pair to the reactive site is called a nucleophile
A reagent that takes away an electron pair from reactive site is called electrophile
the carbon atom becomes an electrophilic centre at which a nucleophile can attack.

summary: This section defines substrates and reagents in organic chemical reactions. It classifies reagents into nucleophiles and electrophiles based on their electron-donating or electron-accepting properties, and explains how to identify electrophilic centers in polar molecules.
title: Substrate and Reagent

evidence_snippets.text: The movement of electrons in organic reactions can be shown by curved-arrow notation.
Movement of single electron is indicated by a single barbed ‘fish hooks’ (i.e. half headed curved arrow).

summary: This section explains how electron movement in organic reactions is represented using curved-arrow notation. It covers the depiction of electron pair shifts using full-headed curved arrows and single electron shifts using half-headed fish-hook arrows.
title: Electron Movement in Organic Reactions

evidence_snippets.text: The electron displacements due to the influence of an atom or a substituent group present in the molecule cause permanent polarisation of the bond.
Temporary electron displacement effects are seen in a molecule when a reagent approaches to attack it.

summary: This section introduces the concept of electron displacement in covalent bonds of organic molecules. It classifies these displacements into permanent effects, like inductive and resonance effects, and temporary effects, such as the electromeric effect.
title: Electron Displacement Effects in Covalent Bonds

evidence_snippets.text: all bodies, regardless of their masses, are accelerated towards the earth with a constant acceleration.
Ptolemy, about 2000 years ago, proposed the 'geocentric' model where all celestial objects... revolved around the earth
the 'heliocentric' model, where the Sun is the center... Copernicus's model proposed that planets moved in circles around a fixed central sun.
Kepler extracted three elegant laws from this data, known as Kepler's laws

summary: This section introduces the historical development of gravity and planetary motion theories. It covers Galileo's experiments on constant gravitational acceleration, the transition from the geocentric to the heliocentric model, and the observational work of Tycho Brahe and Johannes Kepler.
title: Introduction

evidence_snippets.text: All planets move in elliptical orbits with the Sun situated at one of the foci.
The line that joins any planet to the sun sweeps equal areas in equal intervals of time.
The square of the time period of revolution of a planet is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis
The areal velocity is ΔA / Δt = L / (2m). Since L is constant for a central force, the areal velocity is constant.

summary: This section introduces Kepler's three laws of planetary motion: the Law of Orbits, the Law of Areas, and the Law of Periods. It explains the geometry of elliptical orbits and derives the constancy of areal velocity from the conservation of angular momentum under central forces.
title: Kepler's Laws

evidence_snippets.text: Every body in the universe attracts every other body with a force which is directly proportional to the product of their masses
Here, G is the universal gravitational constant
For a collection of point masses, the force on any one of them is the vector sum of the gravitational forces exerted by the other point masses.
The force of attraction on a point mass outside the shell is as if the entire mass of the shell is concentrated at its centre.
The force of attraction on a point mass situated inside the shell is zero.

summary: This section introduces Newton's Universal Law of Gravitation, detailing its mathematical formulation, vector representation, and the universal gravitational constant. It also explains the principle of superposition for calculating net gravitational forces from multiple point masses and presents the shell theorems for uniform spherical shells.
title: Universal Law of Gravitation

evidence_snippets.text: The value of the universal gravitational constant G was first determined experimentally by Henry Cavendish in 1798.
At equilibrium, the gravitational torque equals the restoring torque of the wire: G (Mm/d^2) L = tau theta
The currently accepted value is: G = 6.67 x 10^-11 N m^2/kg^2

summary: This section describes the experimental determination of the universal gravitational constant G by Henry Cavendish using a torsion balance. It explains the balance of gravitational and restoring torques in the experiment and provides the currently accepted value of G.
title: The Gravitational Constant

evidence_snippets.text: According to the shell theorem, for a point mass outside the Earth, each shell exerts a force as if its mass were concentrated at the center.
inside the earth, the gravitational force is directly proportional to the distance r from the centre.
we obtain the standard formula for acceleration due to gravity at the surface: g = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{GM_E}{R_E^2}.
enables one to estimate the mass of the earth M_E from the relation M_E = \frac{g R_E^2}{G}.

summary: This section explains how to calculate the gravitational force exerted by the Earth on objects located both outside and inside its volume using the shell theorem. It derives the formula for the acceleration due to gravity (g) at the Earth's surface and demonstrates how this value can be used to estimate the Earth's mass.
title: Acceleration Due to Gravity of the Earth

evidence_snippets.text: The acceleration experienced by the point mass is g(h) = GM_E / (R_E + h)^2.
Using the binomial expression for h/R_E << 1, we get the approximation: g(h) = g(1 - 2h/R_E).
The acceleration due to gravity at depth d is g(d) = g(1 - d/R_E).

summary: This section derives and analyzes the variation of acceleration due to gravity both above and below the Earth's surface. It demonstrates that gravity is at its maximum on the surface and decreases with both altitude and depth.
title: Acceleration Due to Gravity Below and Above the Surface of Earth

evidence_snippets.text: Potential energy is the energy stored in a body at its given position.
We can associate a potential energy $W(h) = mgh + W_0$, where $W_0$ is a constant representing potential energy at the surface ($h=0$).
the potential energy at a point is the work done in displacing the particle from infinity to that point: $W(r) = -\frac{G M_E m}{r}$.
The gravitational potential at a point is defined as the potential energy of a particle of unit mass at that point.
For a system of particles, the total potential energy is the sum of energies for all possible pairs of its constituent particles

summary: This section introduces gravitational potential energy, deriving its expressions both near the Earth's surface and for variable gravitational forces at arbitrary distances. It also defines gravitational potential and explains how to calculate the total potential energy of a multi-particle system using the superposition principle.
title: Gravitational Potential Energy

evidence_snippets.text: The minimum value of $V_i$ required for an object to escape the Earth's gravitational pull corresponds to the case where the total energy is zero.
$(V_i)_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{2 G M_E}{R_E}}$
This low escape speed is the primary reason the Moon has no atmosphere, as gas molecules formed there often have thermal velocities exceeding this limit
neutral point $N$ where the gravitational forces from both spheres cancel out.

summary: This section defines and derives the escape speed of an object from a celestial body using the principle of conservation of energy. It calculates the escape speed for Earth and the Moon, discusses its implications for planetary atmospheres, and analyzes projectile motion between two spheres using the concept of a neutral point.
title: Escape Speed

evidence_snippets.text: Earth satellites are objects that revolve around the Earth in circular or elliptical orbits.
Equating these gives the orbital speed: $V^2 = \frac{G M_E}{(R_E + h)}$
The time period $T$ is the time taken to complete one revolution: $T = \frac{2\pi(R_E + h)}{V}$.

summary: This section introduces Earth satellites, explaining their motion in circular or elliptical orbits governed by Kepler's laws. It derives the mathematical expressions for a satellite's orbital speed and time period, with special focus on satellites orbiting close to the Earth's surface.
title: Earth Satellites

evidence_snippets.text: kinetic energy is K.E = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{G m M_E}{2(R_E + h)}
P.E = -\frac{G m M_E}{(R_E + h)}
The total energy is negative, indicating that the satellite is in a 'bound state' with the Earth.
The energy required is the difference \Delta E = E_f - E_i

summary: This section derives and explains the kinetic, potential, and total mechanical energy of a satellite in a circular orbit. It introduces the concept of a bound state characterized by negative total energy and demonstrates how to calculate energy changes during orbit transfers.
title: Energy of an Orbiting Satellite

evidence_snippets.text: Newton's law states that the gravitational force of attraction between two particles of masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ separated by distance $r$ is $F = G \frac{m_1 m_2}{r^2}$.
Kepler's laws describe planetary orbits: (a) Orbits are elliptical with the Sun at one focus.
The acceleration due to gravity $g$ changes with position relative to Earth's surface.
gravitational potential energy $V = -\frac{G m_1 m_2}{r}$, where $V \to 0$ as $r \to \infty$.
The escape speed from Earth's surface is $v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2 G M_E}{R_E}} = \sqrt{2 g R_E}$

summary: This section summarizes the fundamental principles of gravitation, including Newton's law of universal gravitation, Kepler's laws of planetary motion, and the variation of acceleration due to gravity. It also covers gravitational potential energy, the energy of orbiting satellites, escape speed, and the gravitational shell theorems.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: In central force motion, angular momentum and total mechanical energy are conserved, while linear momentum is not.
Weightlessness in satellites is due to the state of 'free fall'.
Unlike electrical forces, gravitational forces cannot be shielded.

summary: This section highlights key conceptual points regarding gravitation, including conservation laws in central force motion, the origin of Kepler's laws, and the nature of weightlessness in satellites. It also clarifies the standard reference point for gravitational potential energy and explains why gravitational forces cannot be shielded.
title: Points to Ponder

evidence_snippets.text: The following figure is used for exercises 7.10 and 7.11 regarding gravitational intensity in a hemispherical shell.

summary: This section provides an illustrative diagram for exercises concerning the direction of gravitational intensity inside a hemispherical shell. It depicts points at the center and along the axis of symmetry to help analyze gravitational field directions.
title: Exercises

evidence_snippets.text: The property of a body, by virtue of which it tends to regain its original size and shape when the applied force is removed, is known as elasticity.
substances like putty or mud have no tendency to regain their previous shape... such substances are called plastic
The elastic behaviour of materials is crucial in engineering design.

summary: This section introduces the concepts of elasticity and plasticity, distinguishing between materials that regain their shape after deformation and those that deform permanently. It also highlights the engineering applications of understanding the elastic behavior of materials.
title: Introduction

evidence_snippets.text: The restoring force per unit area is known as stress.
Longitudinal strain = \frac{\Delta L}{L}
The restoring force per unit area developed due to this tangential force is known as tangential or shearing stress.
Volume strain = \frac{\Delta V}{V}

summary: This section defines stress as the internal restoring force per unit area and strain as the fractional change in dimension. It details three distinct types of stress and strain: longitudinal, shearing, and hydraulic, depending on the direction and nature of the applied deforming forces.
title: Stress and Strain

evidence_snippets.text: For small deformations, the stress and strain are proportional to each other. This is known as Hooke's law.
stress = k \times strain
k is the proportionality constant known as the modulus of elasticity.

summary: This section introduces Hooke's law, which states that stress and strain are directly proportional to each other for small deformations. It also defines the modulus of elasticity as the constant of proportionality in this relationship.
title: Hooke's Law

evidence_snippets.text: A graph is then plotted between the stress (magnitude of applied force per unit area) and the strain produced.
Proportional Limit (O to A): Stress is linear to strain; Hooke's law is obeyed.
Yield Point (B): Also known as the elastic limit.
Ultimate Tensile Strength (D): The maximum stress the material can withstand.
If points D and E are close together, the material is said to be brittle. If points D and E are far apart, the material is said to be ductile.
Substances like the tissue of the aorta and rubber, which can be stretched to cause large strains, are specifically called elastomers.

summary: This section describes the experimental determination of the relationship between stress and strain using a tensile test. It analyzes the typical stress-strain curve of a metal, identifying key regions such as the proportional limit, yield point, ultimate tensile strength, and fracture point. It also classifies materials as ductile, brittle, or elastomers based on their stress-strain behavior.
title: Stress-Strain Curve

evidence_snippets.text: Many organic molecules cannot be explained by a single Lewis structure.
Benzene is represented as a hybrid of two energetically identical resonance structures (I and II).
The actual structure is a resonance hybrid of the two canonical forms.
The difference in energy between the actual structure and the lowest energy resonance structure is the resonance stabilisation energy.

summary: This section introduces the concept of resonance in organic molecules, explaining how some molecules are best represented as a hybrid of multiple canonical structures. It defines resonance stabilization energy and outlines the rules for drawing and determining the relative stability of resonance structures.
title: Resonance Structure

evidence_snippets.text: To prevent permanent deformation, the stress on the rope must not exceed its yield strength
A beam of length l, breadth b, and depth d, supported at its ends and loaded at the center with weight W, will sag by an amount \delta
a very deep and thin beam may bend sideways, a phenomenon called buckling.
The maximum height of a mountain on Earth is limited to approximately 10 km due to the elastic properties of rocks.

summary: This section explores the practical applications of the elastic behavior of materials in structural engineering and geology. It covers the design of crane ropes, the bending and buckling of beams, the structural design of pillars, and the theoretical calculation of the maximum height of mountains on Earth.
title: APPLICATIONS OF ELASTIC BEHAVIOUR OF MATERIALS

evidence_snippets.text: Stress is the restoring force per unit area and strain is the fractional change in dimension.
For many materials, stress is directly proportional to strain for small deformations, known as Hooke’s law.
Young’s modulus ($Y$): For objects under tension or compression, Hooke’s law takes the form $F/A = Y \Delta L/L$

summary: This section summarizes the fundamental concepts of elasticity, including stress, strain, and Hooke's law. It defines the three primary elastic moduli—Young's modulus, shear modulus, and bulk modulus—along with their mathematical expressions.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: Hooke’s law is valid only in the linear part of the stress-strain curve.
Young’s modulus and shear modulus are relevant only for solids, while bulk modulus applies to solids, liquids, and gases.
In physics, a material that stretches less for a given load is considered more elastic
Longitudinal strain can produce lateral strain (change in radius), described by the Poisson ratio.
Stress is not a vector quantity because it cannot be assigned a specific direction like a force.

summary: This section provides key conceptual clarifications on elasticity, detailing the limits of Hooke's law, the applicability of different elastic moduli across states of matter, and the physical definition of elasticity. It also explains the non-vector nature of stress and introduces the role of Poisson's ratio in describing lateral strain.
title: Points to Ponder

evidence_snippets.text: It is defined as the complete transfer of a shared pair of \pi-electrons to one of the atoms joined by a multiple bond on the demand of an attacking reagent.
In this effect the \pi-electrons of the multiple bond are transferred to that atom to which the reagent gets attached.
In this effect the \pi-electrons of the multiple bond are transferred to that atom to which the attacking reagent does not get attached.
When inductive and electromeric effects operate in opposite directions in a molecule, the electromeric effect predominates over the inductive effect.

summary: This section defines and explains the electromeric effect (E effect), a temporary electronic effect involving the complete transfer of a shared pair of pi-electrons in the presence of an attacking reagent. It distinguishes between the positive (+E) and negative (-E) electromeric effects and describes how the electromeric effect predominates when in competition with the inductive effect.
title: Electromeric Effect (E effect)

evidence_snippets.text: Fluids are substances that can flow, a category that includes both liquids and gases.
Unlike solids, fluids do not have a definite shape of their own.
fluids offer very little resistance to shear stress; their shape changes significantly even under the application of very small shear stress.

summary: This section introduces fluids as substances that can flow, encompassing both liquids and gases. It compares the physical properties of solids, liquids, and gases, focusing on shape, volume, compressibility, and resistance to shear stress.
title: Introduction

evidence_snippets.text: Pressure is defined by the relationship between a force and the area over which it is applied.
When an object is submerged in a fluid at rest, the fluid exerts a force on its surface that is always normal (perpendicular) to the surface
average pressure P_av is defined as the normal force acting per unit area: P_av = F/A
Density rho for a fluid of mass m occupying volume V is defined as: rho = m/V

summary: This section introduces the concept of pressure as the normal force exerted per unit area, explaining its scalar nature, SI units, and behavior in static fluids. It also defines fluid density and relative density, illustrating these concepts with physical examples and data.
title: Pressure

evidence_snippets.text: Hyperconjugation is a general stabilising interaction. It involves delocalisation of $\sigma$ electrons of C—H bond of an alkyl group
This type of overlap stabilises the carbocation because electron density from the adjacent $\sigma$ bond helps in dispersing the positive charge.
relative stability of carbocations follows the order: $(CH_3)_3 \overset{+}{C} > (CH_3)_2 \overset{+}{C}H > CH_3 \overset{+}{C}H_2 > \overset{+}{C}H_3$.
Because there is no formal bond between the proton and the carbon... hyperconjugation is also termed as 'no bond resonance'.

summary: This section introduces hyperconjugation as a permanent stabilizing interaction involving the delocalization of C-H sigma electrons. It explains the mechanism of hyperconjugation in carbocations and alkenes, and details how it determines the relative stability of carbocations.
title: Hyperconjugation

evidence_snippets.text: Pascal's Law states that the pressure in a fluid at rest is the same at all points if they are at the same height.
This confirms pressure is the same in all directions and is a scalar.

summary: This section introduces Pascal's Law, which states that pressure in a fluid at rest is equal at all points of the same height. It provides a geometric proof using a prismatic fluid element in static equilibrium, demonstrating that pressure is isotropic and a scalar quantity.
title: Pascal's Law

evidence_snippets.text: Organic reactions can be classified into the following four major categories: (i) Substitution reactions, (ii) Addition reactions...

summary: This section introduces the classification of organic reactions into four major categories: substitution, addition, elimination, and rearrangement reactions.
title: Types of Organic Reactions and Mechanisms

evidence_snippets.text: P_2 - P_1 = \rho g h
P = P_a + \rho g h
The excess pressure, P - P_a, at depth h is defined as the gauge pressure at that point.
liquid pressure is identical at all points at the same horizontal level (same depth). This phenomenon is known as the hydrostatic paradox.

summary: This section derives the linear relationship between fluid pressure and depth, showing that pressure increases with depth due to the weight of the fluid. It defines absolute and gauge pressure and explains the hydrostatic paradox, where pressure depends only on depth and not on the shape of the container.
title: Variation of Pressure with Depth

evidence_snippets.text: The common techniques used for purification are: (i) Sublimation, (ii) Crystallisation, (iii) Distillation, (iv) Differential extraction and (v) Chromatography.

summary: This section introduces various physical methods used to purify organic compounds based on their specific chemical and physical properties. It covers key techniques including sublimation, crystallisation, and several types of distillation such as simple, fractional, vacuum, and steam distillation.
title: METHODS OF PURIFICATION OF ORGANIC COMPOUNDS

evidence_snippets.text: When an organic compound is present in an aqueous medium, it is separated by shaking it with an organic solvent in which it is more soluble
The organic solvent and the aqueous solution should be immiscible with each other so that they form two distinct layers
In this technique, the same solvent is repeatedly used for extraction of the compound.

summary: This section describes the principle and process of differential extraction, which separates organic compounds from aqueous solutions using immiscible organic solvents. It also introduces continuous extraction as an alternative technique when the compound has low solubility in the organic solvent.
title: Differential Extraction

evidence_snippets.text: Atmospheric pressure at any point is equal to the weight of a column of air of unit cross-sectional area
Evangelista Torricelli (1608–1647) devised the 'mercury barometer' to measure this.
A pressure equivalent of 1 mm Hg is called a torr: 1 torr = 133 Pa.
An open tube manometer measures pressure differences.
The gauge pressure P - Pa is proportional to the height difference h of the liquid columns: P - Pa = \rho g h.

summary: This section explains the measurement of atmospheric pressure using Torricelli's mercury barometer and introduces various pressure units like torr and bar. It also describes the open-tube manometer for measuring gauge pressure, which is the difference between absolute and atmospheric pressure.
title: Atmospheric Pressure and Gauge Pressure

evidence_snippets.text: Chromatography is an important technique extensively used to separate mixtures into their components, purify compounds
Adsorption chromatography is based on the fact that different compounds are adsorbed on an adsorbent to different degrees.
Column chromatography involves the separation of a mixture over a column of adsorbent (stationary phase) packed in a glass tube.
Thin layer chromatography (TLC) is a type of adsorption chromatography involving the separation of substances over a thin layer
Partition chromatography is based on the continuous differential partitioning of components between stationary and mobile phases.

summary: This section introduces chromatography as a versatile technique for separating, purifying, and testing the purity of compounds. It details the principles and applications of adsorption chromatography (including column and thin-layer chromatography) and partition chromatography.
title: Chromatography

evidence_snippets.text: whenever external pressure is applied on any part of a fluid contained in a vessel, it is transmitted undiminished and equally in all directions.
The ratio A_2 / A_1 is called the mechanical advantage of the device, allowing it to lift heavy loads like cars with minimal effort.
since water is considered perfectly incompressible, the volume displaced by the smaller piston moving inwards must equal the volume moved outwards

summary: This section introduces hydraulic machines as practical applications of Pascal's law for pressure transmission in fluids. It explains the mechanics of hydraulic lifts and hydraulic brakes, deriving how force multiplication is achieved through differences in piston cross-sectional areas.
title: Hydraulic Machines

evidence_snippets.text: The study of fluids in motion is known as fluid dynamics. When fluid flow is smooth, it is often characterized as steady flow.
The path taken by a fluid particle under steady flow is called a streamline.
Av = constant. This is called the equation of continuity, representing the conservation of mass in the flow of incompressible fluids.
Beyond a limiting value called the critical speed, the flow loses steadiness and becomes turbulent.

summary: This section introduces fluid dynamics, distinguishing between steady (streamline) flow and turbulent flow. It defines streamlines and derives the equation of continuity, which expresses the conservation of mass for incompressible fluid flow.
title: Streamline Flow

evidence_snippets.text: Qualitative analysis involves detecting the elements present in an organic compound.
Carbon and hydrogen are detected by heating the organic compound with copper(II) oxide (CuO).
Nitrogen, sulphur, halogens, and phosphorus are detected using Lassaigne’s test.
The sodium fusion extract is boiled with iron(II) sulphate (FeSO4) and acidified with concentrated sulphuric acid.
Sulphur can be detected by two methods: (a) Acidifying the extract with acetic acid and adding lead acetate
The sodium fusion extract is acidified with nitric acid and treated with silver nitrate (AgNO3).

summary: This section covers the qualitative analysis of organic compounds, focusing on the detection of constituent elements. It details the oxidation method for detecting carbon and hydrogen, and Lassaigne's test for detecting nitrogen, sulphur, halogens, and phosphorus.
title: QUALITATIVE ANALYSIS OF ORGANIC COMPOUNDS

evidence_snippets.text: Bernoulli's principle relates the pressure difference between two points in a pipe to both velocity changes and elevation changes.
Bernoulli's equation states: $P + \frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 + \rho g h = \text{constant}$.
By the work-energy theorem, $(P_1 - P_2) \Delta V = \frac{1}{2} \rho \Delta V (v_2^2 - v_1^2) + \rho g \Delta V (h_2 - h_1)$.

summary: This section introduces Bernoulli's principle, which represents the conservation of energy for steady, incompressible, and non-viscous fluid flow. It derives Bernoulli's equation relating fluid pressure, kinetic energy density, and potential energy density, and outlines its physical limitations.
title: Bernoulli's Principle

evidence_snippets.text: The word efflux means fluid outflow.
we derive the speed of efflux as $v_1 = \sqrt{2g h + \frac{2(P - P_a)}{\rho}}$.
Torricelli's law, which states that the speed of efflux from an open tank is equal to the speed a free-falling body would acquire

summary: This section defines efflux as fluid outflow and derives the formula for the speed of efflux from a tank using Bernoulli's equation and the equation of continuity. It presents Torricelli's law, which states that the speed of efflux from an open tank equals the speed of a body falling freely through the same height.
title: Speed of Efflux: Torricelli's Law

evidence_snippets.text: Quantitative analysis determines the mass percentage of each element in a compound, which is essential for calculating empirical and molecular formulas.
Carbon is converted to CO2 and hydrogen to H2O. The masses of CO2 and H2O produced are used to calculate the percentages of C and H.
In the Dumas method, the nitrogen containing organic compound, when heated with copper oxide in an atmosphere of carbon dioxide, yields free nitrogen
In Kjeldahl's method, the nitrogen-containing compound is heated with concentrated sulphuric acid, converting nitrogen to ammonium sulphate.
The Carius method is used for the estimation of halogens in an organic compound.

summary: This section covers quantitative analysis methods used to determine the mass percentage of elements in organic compounds. It details classical chemical techniques for estimating carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, halogens, sulphur, phosphorus, and oxygen, alongside modern automated elemental analysis.
title: QUANTITATIVE ANALYSIS

evidence_snippets.text: Dynamic lift is the force that acts on a body, such as an airplane wing, a hydrofoil, or a spinning ball, by virtue of its motion through a fluid.
The Magnus effect explains the dynamic lift on a spinning ball.
An aerofoil is a solid piece shaped to provide upward dynamic lift when moving horizontally through air.

summary: This section explains dynamic lift, which is the force exerted on an object due to its motion through a fluid. It uses Bernoulli's principle to analyze the pressure differences that generate lift on spinning balls (the Magnus effect) and aircraft wings (aerofoils).
title: Dynamic Lift

evidence_snippets.text: Their nature is described using the orbitals hybridisation concept, where carbon can have $sp^3$, $sp^2$, and $sp$ hybridised orbitals.
Systematic naming follows rules laid down by the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC)
Mechanisms involve the breaking and making of covalent bonds, which can occur in heterolytic... or homolytic... fashion.
Electron displacement effects like inductive, resonance, electromeric, and hyperconjugation effects polarise bonds.
Purification methods include sublimation, distillation, and differential extraction
Qualitative analysis involves detecting elements... Quantitative analysis determines the elemental composition.

summary: This section provides a comprehensive summary of the fundamental principles of organic chemistry, covering structure, bonding, and shapes of organic molecules. It also outlines the classification, IUPAC nomenclature, reaction mechanisms, electron displacement effects, and various qualitative and quantitative analytical techniques used to purify and analyze organic compounds.
title: Summary of Organic Chemistry Principles

evidence_snippets.text: This resistance, which acts like internal friction between layers of fluid in relative motion, is called viscosity.
In laminar flow, layers of liquid slide over one another.
the stress in a flowing fluid depends on the 'rate of change of strain' or 'strain rate'
The coefficient of viscosity (denoted by the Greek letter \eta, 'eta') for a fluid is defined as the ratio of shearing stress to the strain rate

summary: This section introduces viscosity as the internal friction of fluids and explains laminar flow using the concept of strain rate. It defines the coefficient of viscosity, details its temperature dependence, and provides a practical method for its measurement.
title: Viscosity

evidence_snippets.text: Hydrocarbons are compounds composed exclusively of carbon and hydrogen.
Hydrocarbons are classified into three main categories based on the types of carbon-carbon bonds present

summary: This section introduces hydrocarbons as compounds composed exclusively of carbon and hydrogen, highlighting their applications as fuels and polymers. It classifies hydrocarbons into saturated, unsaturated, and aromatic categories based on the types of carbon-carbon bonds present.
title: CLASSIFICATION

evidence_snippets.text: Alkanes are saturated open-chain hydrocarbons.
They form a homologous series with the general formula CnH2n+2.
methane has a tetrahedral structure where the carbon atom is at the center and four hydrogen atoms lie at the corners of a regular tetrahedron.
The C-C and C-H σ bonds are formed by the head-on overlapping of sp3 hybrid orbitals of carbon and 1s orbitals of hydrogen atoms.

summary: This section introduces alkanes as saturated open-chain hydrocarbons, defining their general formula, homologous series, and historical designation as paraffins. It also details the tetrahedral structure of methane and alkanes, explaining their bonding through sp3 hybridization and specific bond parameters.
title: ALKANES

evidence_snippets.text: for a sphere of radius $a$ moving with velocity $v$ through a fluid of viscosity $\eta$, the viscous drag force $F$ is given by $F = 6\pi\eta av$.
The sphere then descends with a constant velocity called the terminal velocity $v_t$.
In equilibrium, $6\pi\eta av_t = (4\pi/3) a^3 (\rho - \sigma)g$

summary: This section introduces Stokes' law, which defines the viscous drag force acting on a spherical body moving through a fluid. It derives the expression for terminal velocity when gravitational, buoyant, and viscous forces reach equilibrium, and demonstrates its application.
title: Stokes' Law

evidence_snippets.text: compounds, which have the same molecular formula but differ in the chain of carbon atoms, are known as chain isomers
Carbon atoms in alkanes are classified based on the number of other carbon atoms they are attached to
Alkyl groups are substituents or functional groups derived from alkanes by the removal of one hydrogen atom.

summary: This section covers the nomenclature and structural isomerism of alkanes, including the classification of carbon atoms as primary, secondary, tertiary, or quaternary. It details the systematic IUPAC rules for naming branched alkanes, drawing structures from IUPAC names, and correcting erroneous names.
title: Nomenclature and Isomerism

evidence_snippets.text: S is the magnitude of surface tension, defined as the force per unit length or the surface energy per unit area.
This extra energy associated with the surface is called surface energy.
The angle between the tangent to the liquid surface at the point of contact and the solid surface inside the liquid is termed the angle of contact
Surface tension causes the pressure inside a spherical drop to be higher than the pressure outside.
One consequence of the pressure difference across a curved interface is capillary rise, where water rises in a narrow tube against gravity.

summary: This section introduces surface tension and surface energy as molecular phenomena arising from unbalanced attractive forces on liquid surface molecules. It derives the relationship between surface tension, contact angle, and interfacial equilibrium, and explains physical consequences such as excess pressure in drops/bubbles and capillary action.
title: SURFACE TENSION

evidence_snippets.text: Alkanes can be prepared by the addition of dihydrogen gas ($H_2$) to alkenes and alkynes in the presence of finely divided catalysts.
Alkyl halides (with the exception of fluorides) can be reduced to alkanes using zinc and dilute hydrochloric acid.
Alkyl halides, when treated with sodium metal in a dry ethereal (moisture-free) solution, yield higher alkanes.
Sodium salts of carboxylic acids, when heated with soda lime... undergo decarboxylation to give alkanes containing one carbon atom less
An aqueous solution of a sodium or potassium salt of a carboxylic acid, on electrolysis, yields an alkane containing an even number of carbon atoms

summary: This section details various chemical methods for preparing alkanes. These include catalytic hydrogenation of unsaturated hydrocarbons, reduction of alkyl halides, the Wurtz reaction, decarboxylation of carboxylic acids, and Kolbe's electrolytic method.
title: Preparation

evidence_snippets.text: average pressure $P_{av}$ is defined as the ratio of the force to area: $P_{av} = F/A$.
pressure in a fluid at rest is the same at all points which are at the same height.
This equation is essentially the conservation of energy applied to non-viscous fluid motion in steady state.
The ratio of the shear stress to the time rate of shearing strain is known as the coefficient of viscosity, $\eta$.
the viscous drag force $\mathbf{F}$ on a sphere of radius $a$ moving with velocity $\mathbf{v}$
Surface tension is defined as the force per unit length (or surface energy per unit area) acting in the plane of the interface

summary: This section summarizes the fundamental properties of fluids, including pressure variation, Pascal's law, and Bernoulli's principle. It also covers fluid dynamics and transport phenomena such as viscosity, Stokes' law, and surface tension.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: Pressure is a scalar quantity. Although the definition 'force per unit area' might suggest it is a vector, the 'force' in the numerator refers specifically to the component of the force normal to the area
The expression for pressure $P = P_a + \rho gh$ holds true if the fluid is incompressible.
Gauge pressure ($P_g$) is the difference between the actual pressure ($P$) and the atmospheric pressure ($P_a$): $P - P_a = P_g$.
In steady flow, two streamlines cannot intersect, as intersection would imply the fluid particle could have two different velocities at that point.
Bernoulli's principle does not strictly hold in the presence of viscous drag.
Surface tension arises due to the excess potential energy of molecules on the surface compared to those in the interior.

summary: This section provides critical conceptual clarifications on fluid mechanics, highlighting the scalar nature of pressure, the conditions for the pressure-depth relation, and the properties of streamline flow. It also addresses the limitations of Bernoulli's principle under viscous drag and explains the molecular origin of surface tension.
title: Points to Ponder

evidence_snippets.text: Alkanes are almost non-polar molecules due to the covalent nature of C-C and C-H bonds
The boiling point (b.p.) of alkanes increases steadily with molecular mass
Halogenation proceeds via a free radical chain mechanism.
Alkanes undergo complete oxidation upon heating in the presence of air or oxygen
n-Alkanes on heating in the presence of anhydrous aluminium chloride and hydrogen chloride gas isomerise
decomposition reaction into smaller fragments by the application of heat is called pyrolysis

summary: This section covers the physical and chemical properties of alkanes. It explains physical trends such as boiling point and solubility, and details chemical reactions including halogenation, combustion, controlled oxidation, isomerisation, aromatization, and pyrolysis.
title: Properties

evidence_snippets.text: focusing on the application of the equation of continuity, surface tension, and pressure differences in drops and bubbles.
A diagram illustrating fluid flow through a horizontal pipe with a constriction. Two vertical manometer tubes are attached

summary: This section contains exercises and illustrations applying key fluid mechanics principles. It focuses on the application of the equation of continuity, surface tension, and pressure differences in drops and bubbles using horizontal pipes and wire frames.
title: Exercises

evidence_snippets.text: These experiences occur due to the discharge of electric charges through the body, which were accumulated because of the rubbing of insulating surfaces.
Electrostatics is defined as the study of forces, fields, and potentials arising from static charges.

summary: This section introduces the phenomenon of static electricity through everyday examples of electric discharge caused by friction. It defines electrostatics as the study of physical phenomena associated with static electric charges.
title: Introduction

evidence_snippets.text: This rotation results in different spatial arrangements of atoms in space which can change into one another. Such spatial arrangements are called conformations
weak repulsive interaction between adjacent bonds, known as torsional strain.
Eclipsed conformation: Hydrogen atoms... as close together as possible. 2. Staggered conformation: Hydrogen atoms are as far apart as possible.
In this projection, the molecule is viewed along the molecular axis. The central C–C bond is drawn as a long straight line, slightly tilted.
In this projection, the molecule is viewed head-on at the C–C bond. The front carbon is represented by a point, and the rear carbon by a circle.
The angle of rotation is called the dihedral angle or torsional angle.

summary: This section introduces the concept of conformations arising from the rotation around carbon-carbon single bonds, using ethane as a primary example. It explains eclipsed, staggered, and skew conformations, their representation via Sawhorse and Newman projections, and their relative stability based on torsional strain.
title: Conformations

evidence_snippets.text: Bodies that acquire these properties are said to be electrified or to possess an electric charge.
The property that differentiates the two kinds of charges is called the polarity of charge.
The gold-leaf electroscope is a simple apparatus used to detect the presence and magnitude of charge on a body.
In solids, charging occurs through the transfer of electrons, which are less tightly bound to the atom.

summary: This section introduces the fundamental concept of electric charge, detailing its historical discovery and experimental observations of attraction and repulsion. It explains the classification of charges into positive and negative, the operation of a gold-leaf electroscope, and the microscopic origin of charging via electron transfer.
title: Electric Charge

evidence_snippets.text: Conductors are materials that readily allow electricity (electrons) to pass through them
Insulators are materials that offer high resistance to the passage of electricity
When charge is transferred to a conductor, it readily distributes itself over the entire surface
if charge is placed on an insulator, it stays at the same location
electroscopes, which are devices used to detect and measure electric charge

summary: This section classifies materials into conductors, insulators, and semiconductors based on their electrical conductivity. It explains how electric charge distributes across the surface of conductors but remains localized on insulators, and introduces the electroscope as a charge detection device.
title: Conductors and Insulators

evidence_snippets.text: A carbon-carbon double bond consists of one strong sigma (σ) bond... and one weak pi (π) bond
Geometrical isomerism is a type of stereoisomerism that arises due to restricted rotation around the carbon-carbon double bond (C=C).
Alkyl halides (R-X) on heating with alcoholic potash... eliminate one molecule of halogen acid to form alkenes.
the negative part of the addendum... gets attached to that carbon atom of the double bond which possesses the lesser number of hydrogen atoms.
In the presence of peroxides... the addition of HBr to unsymmetrical alkenes like propene occurs contrary to the Markovnikov rule.
Ozonolysis involves the addition of ozone (O3) to an alkene to form an ozonide, followed by cleavage with Zn-H2O

summary: This section covers alkenes, which are unsaturated hydrocarbons characterized by a carbon-carbon double bond. It details their structure, nomenclature, structural and geometrical isomerism, methods of preparation, and key chemical reactions including electrophilic additions, oxidation, and ozonolysis.
title: ALKENES

evidence_snippets.text: If the sizes of charged bodies are very small as compared to the distances between them, we treat them as point charges.
the total charge of the system is obtained simply by adding algebraically q_1 and q_2
the total charge of the isolated system is always conserved.
The fact that electric charge is always an integral multiple of e is termed as quantisation of charge.
In the International System (SI) of Units, a unit of charge is called a coulomb and is denoted by the symbol C.

summary: This section introduces the fundamental properties of electric charge, focusing on its additivity, conservation, and quantisation. It defines point charges, establishes the coulomb as the SI unit of charge, and explains the difference between macroscopic and microscopic quantisation.
title: Basic Properties of Electric Charge

evidence_snippets.text: Alkynes are unsaturated hydrocarbons containing at least one carbon-carbon triple bond ($C \equiv C$).
Their general formula is $C_nH_{2n-2}$.
The first member is ethyne ($C_2H_2$), known as acetylene, used in oxyacetylene flames for welding.

summary: This section introduces alkynes as unsaturated hydrocarbons characterized by at least one carbon-carbon triple bond. It covers their general molecular formula, the simplest member ethyne (acetylene), and their general utility.
title: ALKYNES

evidence_snippets.text: In the common system, alkynes are named as derivatives of acetylene.
In the IUPAC system, the suffix '-ane' of the alkane is replaced by '-yne'.
Alkynes exhibit position isomerism... and chain isomerism...

summary: This section explains the nomenclature and structural isomerism of alkynes. It details both the common and IUPAC naming systems and describes how alkynes exhibit position and chain isomerism.
title: Nomenclature and Isomerism

evidence_snippets.text: In ethyne, each carbon is $sp$ hybridised.
The molecule is linear with a $180^\circ$ bond angle.
The $C \equiv C$ triple bond consists of one sigma and two pi bonds, with a cylindrically symmetrical electron cloud.
The $C \equiv C$ bond is stronger (enthalpy $823 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$) and shorter (120 pm) than $C=C$ and $C-C$.

summary: This section explains the electronic structure and geometry of a carbon-carbon triple bond using ethyne as the primary example. It details the sp hybridization of carbon, the formation of one sigma and two pi bonds, and the resulting linear geometry and bond parameters.
title: Structure of Triple Bond

evidence_snippets.text: Industrially, ethyne is prepared by treating calcium carbide ($CaC_2$) with water.
Vicinal dihalides on treatment with alcoholic potassium hydroxide undergo dehydrohalogenation.

summary: This section outlines the preparation methods for alkynes, specifically focusing on the industrial synthesis of ethyne from calcium carbide and the preparation of alkynes from vicinal dihalides via dehydrohalogenation.
title: Preparation

evidence_snippets.text: Physical properties of alkynes follow the same trend as alkenes and alkanes.
This acidity is due to the sp hybridisation of carbon atoms in ethyne (50% s-character)
These are typically electrophilic addition reactions. The addition product formed depends on the stability of the vinylic cation.
Alkynes react with water on warming with mercuric sulphate (HgSO4) and dilute sulphuric acid at 333 K to form carbonyl compounds.
Alkynes undergo two types of polymerisation: (a) Linear polymerisation... (b) Cyclic polymerisation...

summary: This section covers the physical and chemical properties of alkynes, highlighting their physical trends, acidic character due to sp hybridization, and various addition reactions including hydrogenation, halogenation, hydration, and polymerisation.
title: Properties

evidence_snippets.text: Aromatic hydrocarbons are also known as 'arenes'.
Compounds containing a benzene ring are 'benzenoids', while those without are 'non-benzenoids'.

summary: This section introduces aromatic hydrocarbons, also known as arenes, which are characterized by their unique stability and the presence of a benzene ring. It distinguishes between benzenoid and non-benzenoid aromatic compounds and provides common examples such as benzene, toluene, naphthalene, and biphenyl.
title: AROMATIC HYDROCARBON

evidence_snippets.text: All six hydrogen atoms in benzene are equivalent, so it forms only one type of monosubstituted product.
For disubstituted products, three position isomers exist: 1,2 (ortho, o-), 1,3 (meta, m-), and 1,4 (para, p-).
dimethylbenzene (xylene) exists in three isomeric forms: 1,2-dimethylbenzene (ortho-xylene), 1,3-dimethylbenzene (meta-xylene), and 1,4-dimethylbenzene (para-xylene).

summary: This section explains the nomenclature and position isomerism in benzene derivatives, focusing on monosubstituted and disubstituted products. It details the ortho, meta, and para positions using dimethylbenzene (xylene) isomers as primary examples.
title: Nomenclature and Isomerism

evidence_snippets.text: Its molecular formula was determined to be $C_6H_6$, which indicates a high degree of unsaturation.
Kekulé proposed a cyclic structure for benzene consisting of six carbon atoms with alternating single and double bonds
Benzene is a resonance hybrid of two main contributing structures
In benzene, all six carbon atoms are $sp^2$ hybridized.
X-ray diffraction data confirms that benzene is a planar molecule where all six $C-C$ bond lengths are identical at $139$ pm.

summary: This section details the structural elucidation of benzene, starting from Kekulé's cyclic proposal to modern resonance and orbital descriptions. It explains benzene's stability, planar geometry, and uniform bond lengths through sp2 hybridization and pi-electron delocalization.
title: Structure of Benzene

evidence_snippets.text: For a compound to be classified as 'aromatic', it must possess the following characteristics: (i) Planarity of the ring system.
Presence of (4n + 2) π electrons in the ring, where n is an integer (n = 0, 1, 2, ...). This third requirement is known as the Hückel Rule.
Aromaticity is found in various cyclic systems: - Benzene: n=1, 6π electrons.

summary: This section outlines the criteria required for a cyclic compound to be classified as aromatic, focusing on planarity, complete pi-electron delocalisation, and the Hückel Rule. It also provides key examples of aromatic systems, including benzene, cyclopentadienyl anion, and polycyclic hydrocarbons like naphthalene.
title: Aromaticity

evidence_snippets.text: Benzene is commercially isolated from coal tar.
passing ethyne through a red-hot iron tube at 873 K yields benzene.
Heating the sodium salt of benzoic acid (sodium benzoate) with sodalime (a mixture of NaOH and CaO) produces benzene.
Phenol can be reduced to benzene by passing its vapours over heated zinc dust.

summary: This section details the laboratory and commercial methods used to prepare benzene. It covers three primary chemical pathways: the cyclic polymerisation of ethyne, the decarboxylation of sodium benzoate, and the reduction of phenol using zinc dust.
title: Preparation of Benzene

evidence_snippets.text: Arenes are primarily characterized by electrophilic substitution reactions.
The second step involves the attack of the electrophile on the benzene ring, resulting in the formation of a ̄-complex or arenium ion.
When benzene is treated with an alkyl halide in the presence of anhydrous aluminium chloride (a Lewis acid), an alkylbenzene is formed.

summary: This section details the physical and chemical properties of aromatic hydrocarbons, focusing on electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions such as nitration, halogenation, sulphonation, and Friedel-Crafts reactions. It explains the step-by-step mechanism of these substitutions via a resonance-stabilized arenium ion intermediate, alongside addition and combustion reactions of benzene.
title: Properties

evidence_snippets.text: When a monosubstituted benzene undergoes further substitution, the existing substituent determines the position of the incoming group.
Groups that direct the incoming group to the ortho and para positions are called ortho and para directing groups.
Groups that direct the incoming electrophile to the meta position are called meta directing groups.

summary: This section explains how substituents on a monosubstituted benzene ring direct incoming electrophiles to specific positions (ortho, para, or meta) and alter the ring's reactivity. It details the mechanisms of activating and deactivating groups using resonance and inductive effects.
title: Directive influence of a functional group in monosubstituted benzene

evidence_snippets.text: Benzene and polynuclear hydrocarbons containing more than two fused benzene rings are toxic and possess cancer-producing (carcinogenic) properties.
These polynuclear hydrocarbons are formed during the incomplete combustion of organic materials

summary: This section explains the toxicity and carcinogenic properties of benzene and polynuclear hydrocarbons with more than two fused benzene rings. It describes their formation via incomplete combustion of organic matter and their biochemical impact on DNA.
title: CARCINOGENICITY AND TOXICITY

evidence_snippets.text: Solutions are homogeneous mixtures of two or more than two components.
The component present in the largest quantity is known as the solvent, which determines the physical state in which the solution exists.
One or more components present in the solution other than the solvent are called solutes.
Solutions are classified into three main types based on the physical state of the solvent: Gaseous Solutions, Liquid Solutions, and Solid Solutions.

summary: This section defines solutions as homogeneous mixtures and identifies their components as solutes and solvents. It also classifies solutions into gaseous, liquid, and solid types based on the physical state of the solvent, providing examples for each of the nine possible combinations.
title: Types of Solutions

evidence_snippets.text: The composition of a solution can be described qualitatively or quantitatively.
Mass percentage, parts per million (ppm), mole fraction, and molality are independent of temperature because they are based on mass
molarity is a function of temperature because it depends on the volume of the solution

summary: This section covers various quantitative methods for expressing the concentration of solutions, including mass percentage, volume percentage, parts per million, mole fraction, molarity, and molality. It also explains how temperature affects these concentration units depending on whether they are based on mass or volume.
title: Expressing Concentration of Solutions

evidence_snippets.text: Solubility of a substance is its maximum amount that can be dissolved in a specified amount of solvent at a specified temperature.
A state of dynamic equilibrium is reached when the rate of dissolution equals the rate of crystallisation.
the partial pressure of the gas in vapour phase (p) is proportional to the mole fraction of the gas (x) in the solution

summary: This section defines solubility and examines the factors that influence the solubility of solids and gases in liquid solvents, including temperature, pressure, and molecular interactions. It details the dynamic equilibrium of dissolution and crystallisation, and introduces Henry's Law to quantitatively describe gas solubility.
title: Solubility

evidence_snippets.text: the partial vapour pressure of each component is directly proportional to its mole fraction.
the total pressure ($p_{total}$) exerted by the solution is the sum of the partial pressures of its components
the vapour pressure of such a solution is always lower than that of the pure solvent at the same temperature.

summary: This section explains the vapour pressure of binary liquid solutions, focusing on liquid-liquid and solid-liquid mixtures. It introduces Raoult's law to relate partial vapour pressure to mole fraction, derives total vapour pressure using Dalton's law, and explains the mechanism of vapour pressure lowering by non-volatile solutes.
title: Vapour Pressure of Liquid Solutions

evidence_snippets.text: The solutions which obey Raoult's law over the entire range of concentration are known as ideal solutions.
When a solution does not obey Raoult's law over the entire range of concentration, it is called a non-ideal solution.
If the vapour pressure is higher, the solution exhibits positive deviation; if it is lower, it exhibits negative deviation.
azeotropes, which are binary mixtures having the same composition in both the liquid and vapour phases and boil at a constant temperature.

summary: This section classifies liquid-liquid solutions into ideal and non-ideal solutions based on their adherence to Raoult's law. It explains the molecular basis of ideal behavior and deviations (positive and negative) from Raoult's law, and introduces azeotropes as constant-boiling binary mixtures.
title: Ideal and Non-ideal Solutions

evidence_snippets.text: Such properties are called colligative properties (from Latin: 'co' means together, 'ligare' means to bind).
The relative lowering of vapour pressure is defined as the ratio of the lowering of vapour pressure to the vapour pressure of the pure solvent
The increase in the boiling point ΔT_b = T_b - T_b^0 is known as elevation of boiling point.
The decrease in freezing point ΔT_f = T_f^0 - T_f is known as depression in freezing point.

summary: This section introduces colligative properties as solution properties depending solely on the number of solute particles. It details the physical basis, mathematical derivations, and molar mass determination for three key properties: relative lowering of vapour pressure, elevation of boiling point, and depression of freezing point.
title: Colligative Properties and Determination of Molar Mass

evidence_snippets.text: These membranes, which can be natural... or synthetic... contain a network of submicroscopic pores... called semipermeable membranes (SPM).
This spontaneous flow of the solvent is called osmosis.
The minimum excess pressure that just stops the flow of solvent is called the osmotic pressure (\Pi) of the solution.
This relationship allows for the determination of molar mass by measuring osmotic pressure, a method particularly useful for large molecules

summary: This section introduces osmosis and semipermeable membranes, defining osmotic pressure as the excess pressure required to halt solvent flow. It establishes osmotic pressure as a colligative property, derives the equation to calculate solute molar mass, and classifies solutions as isotonic, hypertonic, or hypotonic.
title: Osmosis and Osmotic Pressure

evidence_snippets.text: The direction of osmosis can be reversed if a pressure larger than the osmotic pressure is applied to the solution side.
When pressure exceeding the osmotic pressure is applied to sea water, pure water is squeezed out through the membrane, leaving the salts behind.
A common workable porous membrane is a film of cellulose acetate placed over a suitable support.

summary: This section explains the mechanism of reverse osmosis, which occurs when a pressure greater than the osmotic pressure is applied to a solution, reversing the natural flow of solvent. It highlights its practical application in the desalination of sea water using specialized polymer membranes like cellulose acetate.
title: Reverse Osmosis and Water Purification

evidence_snippets.text: the force on any charge due to a number of other charges is the vector sum of all the forces on that charge due to the other charges
the total force \vec{F}_1 on q_1 is the vector sum: \vec{F}_1 = \vec{F}_{12} + \vec{F}_{13}.
The total force \vec{F}_1 on charge q_1 due to all other charges is the vector sum of the individual forces

summary: This section introduces the principle of superposition to calculate the net electrostatic force acting on a charge due to multiple other charges. It explains how the total force is the vector sum of individual pairwise Coulomb forces, generalizing the formulation to a system of n charges and demonstrating applications with symmetric triangular charge configurations.
title: Forces between Multiple Charges

evidence_snippets.text: When ionic compounds dissolve in water, they dissociate into cations and anions.
Some solutes undergo association in certain solvents. For example, ethanoic acid (acetic acid) molecules dimerise in benzene
In 1880, van't Hoff introduced a factor i to account for the extent of dissociation or association.
The inclusion of the van't Hoff factor modifies the equations for colligative properties

summary: This section explains how the association or dissociation of solute particles in a solution leads to abnormal molar masses. It introduces the van't Hoff factor to quantify this behavior and presents modified equations for colligative properties.
title: Abnormal Molar Masses

evidence_snippets.text: A solution is a homogeneous mixture of two or more substances, classified as solid, liquid, or gaseous.
The solubility of a gas in a liquid is governed by Henry's law, stating it is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas.
For liquid solutions, Raoult's law states that the relative lowering of vapor pressure of the solvent over a solution is equal to the mole fraction
Colligative properties depend only on the number of solute particles and include relative lowering of vapor pressure, elevation of boiling point

summary: This section provides a comprehensive summary of solutions, including their classification and methods for expressing concentration. It covers solubility laws like Henry's and Raoult's laws, ideal and non-ideal behavior, colligative properties, and the application of the van't Hoff factor.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: A point charge $Q$ placed in vacuum produces an electric field everywhere in its surroundings.
$\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{r}) = q \mathbf{E}(\mathbf{r})$
The charge $Q$ which produces the electric field is called the 'source charge', and the charge $q$ which tests the effect of the source charge is called the 'test charge'.
The electric field $\mathbf{E}$ is independent of the test charge $q$; it is a characteristic of the source charge $Q$ and the position $\mathbf{r}$.

summary: This section introduces the concept of an electric field to explain action-at-a-distance between charges. It defines the electric field mathematically, relates it to electrostatic force, distinguishes between source and test charges, and details its physical properties.
title: Electric Field

evidence_snippets.text: By the superposition principle, the total electric field E at r due to the system of charges is the vector sum of the individual fields
E(r) = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{q_i}{r_{iP}^2} \hat{\mathbf{r}}_{iP}

summary: This section explains how to determine the total electric field at a point due to a system of multiple point charges. It introduces the superposition principle for electric fields, showing that the total field is the vector sum of the individual fields.
title: Electric field due to a system of charges

evidence_snippets.text: Chemically, carbohydrates are defined as optically active polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones, or compounds that produce such units on hydrolysis.
Carbohydrates that reduce Fehling's solution and Tollens' reagent are referred to as reducing sugars.
Prolonged heating with HI results in the formation of n-hexane, suggesting that all six carbon atoms are linked in a straight chain
In carbohydrates, these notations refer to their relation with glyceraldehyde.
the -OH group at C5 is involved in ring formation with the C1 aldehyde carbon, resulting in a six-membered ring.

summary: This section introduces carbohydrates, defining them as optically active polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones and classifying them into monosaccharides, oligosaccharides, and polysaccharides. It details the chemical preparation, open-chain structural elucidation, and D/L configuration of glucose, while addressing its limitations to establish its cyclic hemiacetal (pyranose) structure and anomeric forms.
title: Carbohydrates

evidence_snippets.text: Fructose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$) is an important ketohexose obtained along with glucose by the hydrolysis of sucrose.
It belongs to the D-series and is a laevorotatory compound, hence it is written as D-(-)-fructose.
This results in a five-membered ring called a furanose structure, named after its similarity to furan
The two anomers, \alpha and \beta, differ in the configuration at the anomeric carbon (C2).

summary: This section introduces fructose as an important ketohexose monosaccharide, detailing its open-chain structure as a laevorotatory D-sugar. It also explains its cyclic furanose structure and the corresponding alpha and beta Haworth projections.
title: Fructose

evidence_snippets.text: Disaccharides are carbohydrates that, upon hydrolysis with dilute acids or enzymes, yield two molecules of monosaccharides
This specific linkage between two monosaccharide units through an oxygen atom is known as a glycosidic linkage.
If these functional groups remain free, the sugar is a reducing sugar, such as maltose and lactose.

summary: This section defines disaccharides and explains the formation of glycosidic linkages between monosaccharide units. It classifies disaccharides into reducing and non-reducing sugars, and details the structures and properties of sucrose, maltose, and lactose.
title: Disaccharides

evidence_snippets.text: Polysaccharides consist of a large number of monosaccharide units joined by glycosidic linkages.
Starch is the main storage polysaccharide of plants and a vital dietary source for humans.
Amylose (15-20%) is water-soluble and forms a long unbranched chain of 200-1000 \alpha-D-(+)-glucose units
Amylopectin (80-85%) is water-insoluble and is a branched chain polymer
Cellulose is... a straight-chain polysaccharide composed of \beta-D-glucose units
Glycogen, known as 'animal starch', is the storage form of carbohydrates in animals

summary: This section introduces polysaccharides as complex carbohydrates composed of numerous monosaccharide units joined by glycosidic linkages. It details the structures, properties, and biological roles of key polysaccharides: starch (comprising amylose and amylopectin), cellulose, and glycogen.
title: Polysaccharides

evidence_snippets.text: They serve as storage molecules (starch in plants, glycogen in animals) and structural components (cellulose in cell walls).
specific aldopentoses like D-ribose and 2-deoxy-D-ribose are fundamental components of nucleic acids.

summary: This section outlines the biological and industrial significance of carbohydrates. It highlights their roles as energy sources, storage molecules like starch and glycogen, structural components like cellulose, and essential constituents of nucleic acids.
title: Importance of Carbohydrates

evidence_snippets.text: Chemically, all proteins are polymers of $\alpha$-amino acids.
Globular proteins result when the chains of polypeptides coil around to give a spherical shape.
This specific sequence of amino acids is defined as the primary structure of the protein.
The secondary structure refers to the shape in which a long polypeptide chain exists, arising from regular folding of the backbone
The tertiary structure represents the overall folding of the polypeptide chains, which is a further folding of the secondary structure.
The spatial arrangement of these subunits with respect to each other is called the quaternary structure.

summary: This section introduces proteins as essential biomolecules and polymers of alpha-amino acids. It details their classification into globular proteins and explains the four levels of protein structure: primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary.
title: Proteins

evidence_snippets.text: Amino acids are organic compounds containing both an amino ($-NH_2$) and a carboxyl ($-COOH$) functional group.
Proteins are composed exclusively of $\alpha$-amino acids.
The general structure of an $\alpha$-amino acid is $R-CH(NH_2)-COOH$, where $R$ is a side chain.

summary: This section introduces amino acids as organic compounds containing both amino and carboxyl functional groups, focusing on the structure and nomenclature of alpha-amino acids which make up proteins. It details their classification, representation using three-letter and one-letter codes, and lists the twenty natural amino acids with their specific side chains.
title: Amino Acids

evidence_snippets.text: A protein in its biological system with a unique 3D structure and biological activity is called a native protein.
The protein loses its biological activity in a process called denaturation.
During this process, secondary and tertiary structures are destroyed, but the primary structure remains intact.

summary: This section defines native proteins and explains the process of denaturation, which occurs when physical or chemical changes disrupt a protein's hydrogen bonds. It describes how denaturation destroys secondary and tertiary structures while leaving the primary structure intact, using examples like egg white coagulation and milk curdling.
title: Denaturation of Proteins

evidence_snippets.text: Enzymes are biocatalysts that coordinate chemical reactions in living organisms.
Almost all enzymes are globular proteins.
The names generally end with the suffix '-ase'.

summary: This section introduces enzymes as highly specific biocatalysts that are almost always globular proteins. It explains their nomenclature based on substrates or reaction types, typically ending with the suffix '-ase'.
title: Enzymes

evidence_snippets.text: Enzymes are required in small quantities and function by reducing the magnitude of activation energy for a reaction.
the activation energy for the acid hydrolysis of sucrose is 6.22 kJ mol-1, but it drops to only 2.15 kJ mol-1 when catalyzed by sucrase.

summary: This section explains the mechanism of enzyme action, focusing on how enzymes increase reaction efficiency by lowering the activation energy. It illustrates this concept using the comparative activation energies of sucrose hydrolysis with and without the sucrase enzyme.
title: Mechanism of Enzyme Action

evidence_snippets.text: Vitamins are organic compounds required in the diet in small amounts to perform specific biological functions
Vitamins are classified into two groups depending upon their solubility in water or fat.
Fat soluble vitamins: Vitamins which are soluble in fat and oils but insoluble in water
Water soluble vitamins: B group vitamins and vitamin C are soluble in water

summary: This section defines vitamins as essential organic compounds required in small amounts for growth and health. It classifies them into fat-soluble and water-soluble categories and details their dietary sources and deficiency diseases.
title: Vitamins

evidence_snippets.text: These are mainly of two types, the deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and ribonucleic acid (RNA).
In DNA molecules, the sugar moiety is ̢-D-2-deoxyribose whereas in RNA molecule, it is ̢-D-ribose.
Nucleotides are joined together by phosphodiester linkage between 5' and 3' carbon atoms of the pentose sugar.

summary: This section introduces nucleic acids, focusing on their chemical composition, structures, and biological functions. It details the formation of nucleosides and nucleotides, the phosphodiester linkages that form polynucleotides, the double helix structure of DNA, and applications like DNA fingerprinting.
title: Nucleic Acids

evidence_snippets.text: Hormones are molecules that function as intercellular messengers. They are produced by endocrine glands in the body and are secreted directly into the bloodstream
Chemically, hormones are diverse: some are steroids (e.g., estrogens and androgens), some are polypeptides (e.g., insulin and endorphins)
insulin is released by the pancreas in response to a rapid rise in blood glucose levels... glucagon tends to increase glucose levels
Thyroxine, produced in the thyroid gland, is an iodinated derivative of the amino acid tyrosine.

summary: This section introduces hormones as intercellular messengers secreted by endocrine glands and classifies them chemically into steroids, polypeptides, and amino acid derivatives. It details their physiological roles, focusing on glucose homeostasis, thyroid hormones like thyroxine, and adrenal and gonadal steroid hormones.
title: Hormones

evidence_snippets.text: Electrochemistry is the study of the production of electricity from energy released during spontaneous chemical reactions
The Daniell cell is a specific type of electrochemical cell that converts the chemical energy liberated during a spontaneous redox reaction
The cell now functions as an electrolytic cell, using electrical energy to drive a non-spontaneous chemical reaction.

summary: This section introduces electrochemistry and details the operation of the Daniell cell as a representative galvanic cell. It also explains how applying an external opposite potential can alter the cell's behavior, transitioning it from a galvanic to an electrolytic cell.
title: Electrochemical Cells

evidence_snippets.text: A galvanic cell is an electrochemical cell that converts the Gibbs energy of a spontaneous redox reaction into electrical work.
At equilibrium, a separation of charges results in a potential difference between the electrode and the electrolyte, known as the electrode potential.
The potential difference between the two electrodes of a galvanic cell is called the cell potential

summary: This section introduces galvanic cells, explaining how they convert chemical energy from spontaneous redox reactions into electrical energy. It covers cell construction, electrode potentials, anode/cathode identification, and standard cell representation and emf calculation.
title: Galvanic Cells

evidence_snippets.text: According to convention, the standard hydrogen electrode (SHE)... is assigned a zero potential at all temperatures.
If the concentrations of the species in the right-hand half-cell are unity, the cell potential equals the standard electrode potential
Metals like platinum or gold are used as inert electrodes. They do not participate in the chemical reaction but provide a surface

summary: This section explains how to measure individual electrode potentials using the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) as a reference. It details the calculation of standard cell potentials, the role of inert electrodes, and the significance of standard electrode potentials in determining the strength of oxidizing and reducing agents.
title: Measurement of Electrode Potential

evidence_snippets.text: The Nernst equation describes how electrode potential depends on the concentration of the species involved.
Table 2.1 provides the standard electrode potentials ($E^\circ$) at 298 K for various redox couples
In a Daniell cell, the electrode potential for any given concentration of $Cu^{2+}$ and $Zn^{2+}$ ions can be written as follows.
the equation for the Daniell cell reduces to $E_{(cell)} = E^\circ_{(cell)} - \frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{[Zn^{2+}]}{[Cu^{2+}]}$
For a general reaction $aA + bB \xrightarrow{ne^-} cC + dD$, the Nernst equation is...

summary: This section introduces the Nernst equation, which describes how electrode and cell potentials depend on the concentrations of the reacting chemical species. It provides standard electrode potentials for various redox couples and demonstrates how to calculate cell potentials under non-standard conditions using the Daniell cell and other examples.
title: Nernst Equation

evidence_snippets.text: At equilibrium, the concentrations of ions stop changing and the voltmeter reads zero ($E_{(cell)} = 0$).
$E^\circ_{(cell)} = \frac{2.303RT}{nF} \log K_c$
This allows the calculation of equilibrium constants from standard cell potentials.

summary: This section derives the relationship between the standard cell potential and the chemical equilibrium constant using the Nernst equation. It explains that at equilibrium, the cell potential becomes zero, allowing the calculation of the equilibrium constant from standard cell potentials.
title: Equilibrium Constant from Nernst Equation

evidence_snippets.text: The reversible work done by a galvanic cell is equal to the decrease in its Gibbs energy.
$\Delta_r G = -nFE_{(cell)}$
$E_{(cell)}$ is an intensive parameter... $\Delta_r G$ is an extensive thermodynamic property
$\Delta_r G^o = -RT \ln K$

summary: This section explains the relationship between the electrical work of a galvanic cell and its Gibbs energy change.
