title: Summary of Electrostatic Potential and Capacitance

evidence_snippets.text: each charge is kept at rest by some unspecified force that opposes the net Coulomb force acting on it.
A capacitor is designed to confine electric field lines within a small region of space.
the electric potential is continuous across the surface, maintaining a value equal to $q/4\pi\epsilon_0 R$ at the surface.
The torque $\mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{E}$ acting on a dipole causes it to oscillate about the direction of the electric field $\mathbf{E}$.
The potential due to a point charge $q$ at its own location is not defined; mathematically, it tends toward infinity
A cavity inside a conductor is shielded from external electrical influences.

summary: This section provides critical conceptual clarifications and nuances regarding electrostatics, including electrostatic equilibrium, field confinement in capacitors, and the continuity of electric potential. It also addresses dipole dynamics, the undefined self-potential of point charges, and the directional nature of electrostatic shielding.
title: Points to Ponder

evidence_snippets.text: charges in motion constitute an electric current.
This chapter explores the basic laws governing steady electric currents.

summary: This section introduces the concept of electric current, defined as charges in motion. It contrasts transient currents like lightning with the steady currents that power everyday devices.
title: Introduction

evidence_snippets.text: For a steady current, this net charge is proportional to the time $t$, and the current $I$ is defined as the quotient: $I = \frac{q}{t}$
The instantaneous current $I(t)$ is defined as the limit of the ratio of $\Delta Q$ to $\Delta t$ as $\Delta t$ tends to zero
In SI units, current is measured in ampere (A). The ampere is defined through the magnetic effects of currents.

summary: This section defines electric current as the rate of flow of electric charge across a given area. It introduces the mathematical formulations for both steady current and time-varying instantaneous current, and establishes the ampere as the SI unit of current.
title: Electric Current

evidence_snippets.text: In solid conductors, atoms are tightly bound, and the current is carried by negatively charged electrons
In the absence of an electric field, electrons in a conductor move due to thermal motion
To maintain a steady current, mechanisms like cells or batteries are required

summary: This section explains the mechanism of electric currents in conductors, focusing on the nature of charge carriers in different materials. It describes how thermal motion results in zero net current in the absence of an electric field, and how an external electric field drives a current, which requires a battery or cell to remain steady.
title: Electric Currents in Conductors

evidence_snippets.text: Ohm's law is a fundamental principle regarding the flow of currents. It states that the current I flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference V across its ends: V = RI
R = \rho \frac{l}{A} where the constant of proportionality \rho depends on the material of the conductor but not on its dimensions. \rho is called resistivity.
Current per unit area (taken normal to the current), I/A, is called current density and is denoted by j.
\mathbf{j} = \sigma \mathbf{E} where \sigma = 1/\rho is called the conductivity. Ohm’s law is often stated in an equivalent form, Eq. (3.13)

summary: This section introduces Ohm's law, which states that the electric current flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference across its ends. It defines electrical resistance, derives its dependence on the conductor's physical dimensions to introduce resistivity, and establishes the vector form of Ohm's law using current density and conductivity.
title: Ohm's Law

evidence_snippets.text: This is the phenomenon of drift and the velocity \mathbf{v}_d in Eq. (3.17) is called the drift velocity.
The average value of t_i then is \tau (known as relaxation time).
I \Delta t = +ne A |v_d| \Delta t
identify the conductivity \sigma as \sigma = \frac{ne^2}{m} \tau

summary: This section explains the microscopic mechanism of electrical conduction in metals, introducing the concepts of drift velocity and relaxation time. It derives the mathematical relationship between electric current and drift velocity, leading to a microscopic derivation of Ohm's law and electrical conductivity.
title: Drift of Electrons and the Origin of Resistivity

evidence_snippets.text: Mobility $\mu$ is defined as the magnitude of the drift velocity per unit electric field: $\mu = \frac{|\mathbf{v}_d|}{E}$
The SI unit of mobility is $\text{m}^2/\text{Vs}$.
we obtain $\mu = \frac{v_d}{E} = \frac{e\tau}{m}$ (3.25), where $\tau$ is the average collision time for electrons.

summary: This section defines mobility as the magnitude of drift velocity per unit electric field and derives its relation to relaxation time. It also specifies the SI units of mobility and identifies the charge carriers in different materials.
title: Mobility

evidence_snippets.text: In many materials, the linear relationship where $V$ is proportional to $I$ fails, especially at higher currents.
In certain devices like diodes, the magnitude of current depends on the sign of the applied voltage.
These materials can also show a 'negative resistance' region where current decreases as voltage increases.

summary: This section examines the limitations of Ohm's law, detailing how the linear relationship between voltage and current fails in various materials and devices. It highlights deviations from linearity at high currents, directional dependence of current in diodes, and non-unique V-I relationships including negative resistance in materials like Gallium Arsenide.
title: Limitations of Ohm's Law

evidence_snippets.text: Materials are broadly classified into three categories based on their electrical resistivity: conductors, semiconductors, and insulators.
Metals possess low resistivities, typically ranging from $10^{-8} \Omega m$ to $10^{-6} \Omega m$.
A unique characteristic of semiconductors is that their resistivity decreases as temperature rises.

summary: This section classifies materials into conductors, insulators, and semiconductors based on their electrical resistivity. It compares their characteristic resistivity ranges and highlights how semiconductor resistivity decreases with temperature and impurity addition.
title: Resistivity of Various Materials

evidence_snippets.text: The resistivity of a material is dependent on temperature, though the nature of this dependence varies across different materials.
For metallic conductors, over a limited and relatively small temperature range, the resistivity is approximately related by a linear equation.
Certain materials, particularly alloys like Nichrome, exhibit a very weak dependence of resistivity on temperature.
Unlike metals, the resistivities of semiconductors decrease as temperature increases.

summary: This section examines how the electrical resistivity of different materials, including metals, alloys, and semiconductors, varies with temperature. It introduces the temperature coefficient of resistivity and explains these variations microscopically using electron relaxation time and carrier density.
title: Temperature Dependence of Resistivity

evidence_snippets.text: The change in potential energy \Delta U_{pot} is: \Delta U_{pot} = -I V \Delta t
The energy dissipated as heat in the conductor during time \Delta t is: \Delta W = I V \Delta t ... This phenomenon is known as Joule heating.
The energy dissipated per unit time is defined as the power dissipated, P. It is given by: P = I V
The power dissipated (wasted) in the connecting wires is P_c = I^2 R_c = \frac{P^2 R_c}{V^2}

summary: This section explains the potential energy changes of charges moving through a conductor and how collisions lead to energy dissipation as heat, known as Joule heating. It defines electric power and ohmic loss, and demonstrates how transmitting power at high voltages minimizes energy waste in transmission lines.
title: Electrical Energy, Power

evidence_snippets.text: A simple device to maintain a steady current in an electric circuit is the electrolytic cell.
The potential difference between P and N is V_+ - (-V_-) = V_+ + V_-. This difference is called the electromotive force (emf)
The electrolyte through which a current flows has a finite resistance r, called the internal resistance.
The potential difference V between P and N is V = V_+ + V_- - Ir = \varepsilon - Ir

summary: This section introduces the electrolytic cell as a device to maintain steady current, defining its electromotive force (emf) and internal resistance. It derives the relationship between terminal potential difference, emf, and internal resistance, and calculates the current in a simple circuit.
title: Cells, EMF, Internal Resistance

evidence_snippets.text: The equivalent emf of a series combination of n cells is just the sum of their individual emfs
The equivalent internal resistance of a series combination of n cells is just the sum of their internal resistances.
\epsilon_{eq}/r_{eq} = \sum_{i=1}^n \epsilon_i/r_i

summary: This section explains how to combine multiple electrochemical cells in series and parallel configurations. It derives the formulas for the equivalent electromotive force (emf) and equivalent internal resistance for both types of combinations.
title: Cells in Series and in Parallel

evidence_snippets.text: The network of resistors in a cube is not reducible to simple series and parallel combinations.
using Kirchhoff's first rule and the symmetry in the problem.
the first rule of Kirchhoff is used at every junction to assign the unknown current in each branch.
applying the second rule of Kirchhoff to three different closed loops.

summary: This section demonstrates the practical application of Kirchhoff's rules to solve complex electrical networks. It covers the simplification of symmetric resistor networks, such as a cube, and details the systematic multi-loop analysis of general circuits using simultaneous equations.
title: Kirchhoff's Rules

evidence_snippets.text: The bridge has four resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$ and $R_4$. Across one pair of diagonally opposite points... a source is connected.
Of special interest, is the case of a balanced bridge where the resistors are such that $I_g = 0$.
The Wheatstone bridge and its balance condition provide a practical method for determination of an unknown resistance.
To find the current through the galvanometer, we apply Kirchhoff's rules to the Wheatstone bridge meshes.

summary: This section introduces the Wheatstone bridge, a circuit consisting of four resistors used to determine an unknown resistance. It derives the balanced condition where no current flows through the galvanometer and demonstrates how to analyze both balanced and unbalanced bridge circuits using Kirchhoff's rules.
title: Wheatstone Bridge

evidence_snippets.text: Electric current is defined as the rate of charge flow through a specific area.
Ohm's law states that current is proportional to the applied voltage, with resistance $R$ as the constant ($V = RI$).
current density $\mathbf{j}$ relates to drift velocity $\mathbf{v}_d$ and charge carrier density $n$ as $\mathbf{j} = nq\mathbf{v}_d$.
Resistivity typically increases with temperature, characterized by a coefficient $\alpha$.
Complex circuits are analyzed using Kirchhoff's rules: the junction rule (charge conservation) and the loop rule (energy conservation).
The Wheatstone bridge null condition is $R_1/R_2 = R_3/R_4$.

summary: This section provides a comprehensive summary of current electricity, covering macroscopic concepts like electric current, electromotive force, Ohm's law, and resistance. It also details the microscopic mechanisms of resistivity and drift velocity, temperature dependence, and circuit analysis techniques including Kirchhoff's rules and the Wheatstone bridge.
title: Summary

evidence_snippets.text: Although current is indicated with arrows to show direction in circuit diagrams, it is a scalar quantity because it does not obey vector addition laws.
Ohm’s law specifically asserts that the plot of I versus V is linear, meaning R is independent of V.
Therefore, the drift velocity v_d is solely a result of the applied electric field acting on the electrons.
The relation j = \rho v must be applied to each type of charge carrier separately.
Kirchhoff’s junction rule is based on the principle of conservation of charge.

summary: This section provides critical conceptual clarifications on electric current, Ohm's law, electron drift, and Kirchhoff's junction rule. It emphasizes the scalar nature of current, the physical interpretation and limits of Ohm's law, the components of electron motion, and the charge conservation basis of the junction rule.
title: Points to Ponder

evidence_snippets.text: Oersted concluded that moving charges or currents produce a magnetic field in the surrounding space.
iron filings sprinkled around the wire arrange themselves in concentric circles with the wire as the centre.
A current or a field emerging out of the plane of the paper is depicted by a dot

summary: This section introduces the connection between electricity and magnetism through Oersted's discovery that electric currents produce magnetic fields. It also establishes the standard dot and cross notation conventions for representing three-dimensional field directions on a two-dimensional plane.
title: Introduction

evidence_snippets.text: moving charges or currents produce a magnetic field, denoted by B(r).
The total force F on the charge is the sum of the electric and magnetic forces: F = q [E(r) + v x B(r)]
The magnetic force component q [v x B] has several key features
This unit is called tesla (T) named after Nikola Tesla
we have F = I l x B

summary: This section introduces the concept of magnetic fields produced by moving charges and defines the Lorentz force acting on a charge in the presence of electric and magnetic fields. It details the features of magnetic force, defines its SI unit (tesla), and derives the magnetic force acting on a current-carrying conductor.
title: Magnetic Force

evidence_snippets.text: Since power P = F . v = 0, the magnetic force does no work on the particle.
The perpendicular force, q v x B, acts as a centripetal force and produces a circular motion perpendicular to the magnetic field.
The frequency of rotation is ̓ = ω/(2π) = qB/(2π m). Notably, the frequency is independent of the velocity or energy of the particle.
The combination of uniform linear motion along the field and circular motion perpendicular to it produces a helical motion.

summary: This section analyzes the motion of a charged particle in a uniform magnetic field. It explains why the magnetic force does no work, derives the radius and frequency of circular motion when velocity is perpendicular to the field, and describes helical motion when there is a parallel velocity component.
title: Motion in a Magnetic Field

evidence_snippets.text: The Biot-Savart law describes the magnetic field produced by a current-carrying conductor.
The constant $\mu_0$ is called the permeability of free space (or vacuum).
The direction of the field is determined by the Right Hand Screw rule
The Biot-Savart law for magnetic fields has several similarities and differences compared to Coulomb's law

summary: This section introduces the Biot-Savart law, which mathematically describes the magnetic field generated by an infinitesimal current element. It defines the permeability of free space, compares magnetic fields with electrostatic fields, and establishes the fundamental relationship between electromagnetic constants and the speed of light.
title: Magnetic Field due to a Current Element, Biot-Savart Law

evidence_snippets.text: The final expression for the magnetic field at a point P on the axis is B = B_x i = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(x^2 + R^2)^{3/2}} i.
At the centre, the distance x from the centre along the axis is zero (x = 0).
Curl the palm of your right hand around the circular wire such that your fingers point in the direction of the current flow.

summary: This section derives the magnetic field along the axis of a circular current-carrying loop using the Biot-Savart law. It also explores special cases, including the magnetic field at the center of the loop, the field of a multi-turn coil, and the right-hand thumb rule for determining field direction.
title: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Circular Current Loop

evidence_snippets.text: Ampere’s law states that the line integral of the magnetic field B around any closed path is equal to μ0 times the total current I
This loop is chosen such that at every point, the magnetic field B is either: (i) tangential... (ii) normal... or (iii) zero.
Applying Ampere's law: B x 2πr = μ0 I, which gives B = μ0 I / 2πr.

summary: This section introduces Ampere's Circuital Law as an alternative formulation of the Biot-Savart law for calculating magnetic fields in highly symmetric systems. It defines the mathematical statement of the law, the concept of an Amperian loop, and applies it to derive the magnetic fields of infinite straight wires and thick wires.
title: Ampere’s Circuital Law

evidence_snippets.text: A solenoid consists of a long wire wound in the form of a helix where the neighboring turns are closely spaced.
For a finite solenoid (Fig 4.15b), the field at the interior mid-point P is uniform, strong, and directed along the axis of the solenoid.
For a very long solenoid, the magnetic field inside becomes perfectly uniform and parallel to the axis, while the field outside becomes negligible.
Thus, l/a = 50 i.e., l >> a. Hence, we can use the long solenoid formula, namely, Eq. (4.20): B = μ_0 n I

summary: This section defines a solenoid as a helical coil of wire and describes its magnetic field pattern. It derives the formula for the uniform magnetic field inside a very long solenoid using Ampere's circuital law and demonstrates its calculation.
title: The Solenoid

evidence_snippets.text: Parallel currents attract, and antiparallel currents repel.
The magnitude of this force is given by: $F_{ba} = I_b L B_a = \frac{μ_0 I_a I_b}{2\pi d} L$.
would produce on each of these conductors a force equal to $2 \times 10^{-7}$ newtons per metre of length.
When a steady current of 1A is set up in a conductor, the quantity of charge that flows through its cross-section in 1s is one coulomb (1C).

summary: This section derives the magnetic force between two parallel current-carrying conductors, showing that parallel currents attract and antiparallel currents repel. It uses this force to define the SI base unit of current, the ampere, and subsequently defines the coulomb in terms of the ampere.
title: Force Between Two Parallel Currents, The Ampere

evidence_snippets.text: A rectangular current-carrying loop placed in a uniform magnetic field experiences a torque. It does not experience a net force.
We define the magnetic moment $\mathbf{m}$ of the current loop as $\mathbf{m} = I \mathbf{A}$
The torque $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ on a current loop in a uniform magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$ is given by $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{m} \times \mathbf{B}$.

summary: This section derives the torque on a current-carrying loop in a uniform magnetic field and defines the magnetic dipole moment. It establishes a mathematical analogy between magnetic and electric dipoles, analyzing their equilibrium states and noting the non-existence of magnetic monopoles.
title: Torque on Current Loop, Magnetic Dipole

evidence_snippets.text: The moving coil galvanometer (MCG) is a device used to detect or measure small electric currents and voltages.
In equilibrium, $k\phi = NIAB$, which leads to the deflection formula: $\phi = \left( \frac{NAB}{k} \right) I$.
To convert a galvanometer into an ammeter, a very small resistance $r_s$, called a shunt resistance, is connected in parallel
To ensure this, a large resistance $R$ is connected in series with the galvanometer.
Current sensitivity is defined as the deflection per unit current, $\phi/I$.
We define the voltage sensitivity as the deflection per unit voltage.

summary: This section introduces the Moving Coil Galvanometer (MCG), explaining its construction, radial magnetic field, and torque balance principle. It details how the MCG is converted into an ammeter using a parallel shunt resistance or into a voltmeter using a series resistance, and defines current and voltage sensitivities.
title: The Moving Coil Galvanometer

evidence_snippets.text: The total force on a charge $q$ moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ in the presence of magnetic and electric fields $\vec{B}$ and $\vec{E}$ is called the Lorentz force
Its frequency of uniform circular motion is called the cyclotron frequency and is given by $\nu_c = \frac{qB}{2\pi m}$.
The Biot-Savart law asserts that the magnetic field $d\vec{B}$ due to an element $d\vec{l}$ carrying a steady current $I$ at a point $P$
Ampere's Circuital Law states that for an open surface $S$ bounded by a loop $C$, $\oint_C \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I$
A planar loop carrying current $I$ with $N$ turns and area $A$ has a magnetic moment $\vec{m} = N I \vec{A}$.
In a moving coil galvanometer, the magnetic torque is balanced by a counter-torque from a spring: $k\phi = NIAB$

summary: This section summarizes the fundamental principles of moving charges and magnetism, including the Lorentz force, Biot-Savart law, and Ampere's circuital law. It also outlines the magnetic fields of various current configurations, magnetic dipole moments, and the operational principles of a moving coil galvanometer.
title: Summary of Moving Charges and Magnetism

evidence_snippets.text: In contrast, magnetic field lines always form closed loops.
This velocity-dependent force implies that if one switches to a frame with instantaneous velocity v, the magnetic part of the force vanishes.
Ampere’s Circuital law is not independent of the Biot-Savart law; it can be derived from it.

summary: This section provides critical conceptual clarifications on electromagnetism, emphasizing that magnetic field lines form closed loops, the Lorentz force is frame-dependent, and Ampere's Circuital Law can be derived from the Biot-Savart Law.
title: Points to Ponder

evidence_snippets.text: Magnetic phenomena are universal in nature, permeating everything from vast, distant galaxies to tiny invisible atoms
Magnetic monopoles do not exist; if a bar magnet is broken, it results in two smaller magnets
The Earth behaves as a magnet with a magnetic field pointing approximately from geographic south to north.

summary: This section introduces the history and universal nature of magnetism, highlighting its connection to moving charges. It also outlines the fundamental properties of magnets, such as pole alignment, attraction and repulsion, and the non-existence of isolated magnetic monopoles.
title: Introduction

evidence_snippets.text: The filings arrange themselves in a pattern that suggests the magnet has two poles, similar to the positive and negative charges of an electric dipole.
Magnetic field lines provide a visual and intuitive realization of the magnetic field.
They form continuous closed loops, unlike electric field lines which begin at a positive charge and end at a negative charge
This is also the far axial magnetic field of a bar magnet, confirming that a bar magnet and a solenoid produce similar magnetic fields.
When a compass needle of magnetic moment m is placed in a uniform magnetic field B, it experiences a torque given by: \tau = m \times B
The magnetic potential energy Um is obtained by integrating the torque: ... Um = -\mathbf{m} \cdot \mathbf{B}

summary: This section explores the physical properties of a bar magnet, visualizing its magnetic field lines and establishing its equivalence to a current-carrying solenoid. It also analyzes the behavior of a magnetic dipole in a uniform magnetic field, including torque and potential energy, and highlights the mathematical analogy between electrostatic and magnetic dipoles.
title: The Bar Magnet

evidence_snippets.text: In contrast, magnetic field lines are continuous and always form closed loops.
This equation represents Gauss's law for magnetism. It states that the net magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero.
isolated magnetic poles, or monopoles, do not exist in nature.

summary: This section introduces Gauss's law for magnetism, which states that the net magnetic flux through any closed surface is always zero. This law mathematically reflects the physical absence of isolated magnetic monopoles, meaning magnetic field lines must always form continuous, closed loops.
title: Magnetism and Gauss's Law

evidence_snippets.text: We define magnetisation M of a sample to be equal to its net magnetic moment per unit volume
H = B/mu_0 - M
M = chi H, where chi is the dimensionless magnetic susceptibility

summary: This section introduces the concepts of magnetisation and magnetic intensity to describe how materials respond to external magnetic fields. It defines key parameters including magnetic susceptibility, relative magnetic permeability, and total magnetic permeability, demonstrating their application in a solenoid with a material core.
title: Magnetisation and Magnetic Intensity

evidence_snippets.text: Materials are classified as diamagnetic, paramagnetic, or ferromagnetic based on their susceptibility $\chi$.
A material is diamagnetic if $\chi$ is negative, paramagnetic if $\chi$ is positive and small, and ferromagnetic if $\chi$ is large and positive.

summary: This section classifies magnetic materials into diamagnetic, paramagnetic, and ferromagnetic categories based on their magnetic susceptibility and permeability. It defines the quantitative ranges of these properties for each material class.
title: Magnetic Properties of Materials

evidence_snippets.text: Diamagnetic substances have a tendency to move from stronger to weaker parts of an external magnetic field; they are repelled by magnets.
This phenomenon of perfect diamagnetism is known as the Meissner effect.
Superconductors are metals cooled to extremely low temperatures that exhibit perfect conductivity and perfect diamagnetism.

summary: This section defines diamagnetism, explaining how diamagnetic substances are repelled by external magnetic fields due to induced atomic magnetic moments opposing the field. It details the underlying mechanism of electron orbital motion, lists common examples, and introduces superconductors and the Meissner effect as cases of perfect diamagnetism.
title: Diamagnetism

evidence_snippets.text: Haloalkanes contain halogen atom(s) attached to the $sp^3$ hybridised carbon atom of an alkyl group
haloarenes contain halogen atom(s) attached to $sp^2$ hybridised carbon atom(s) of an aryl group.
Several halogenated compounds have significant biological and medical roles

summary: This section introduces haloalkanes and haloarenes, defining them by the hybridization of the carbon atom bonded to the halogen. It also highlights the biological, medical, and industrial significance of various halogenated organic compounds.
title: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes

evidence_snippets.text: Haloalkanes and haloarenes may be classified as mono, di, or polyhalogen (tri-, tetra-, etc.) compounds
Alkyl halides or haloalkanes (R—X): The halogen atom is bonded to an alkyl group (R).
Allylic halides: These are compounds in which the halogen atom is bonded to an sp3-hybridised carbon atom adjacent to a carbon-carbon double bond
Benzylic halides: These are compounds in which the halogen atom is bonded to an sp3-hybridised carbon atom attached to an aromatic ring.
Vinylic halides: These are compounds in which the halogen atom is bonded to an sp2-hybridised carbon atom of a carbon-carbon double bond
Aryl halides: These are compounds in which the halogen atom is directly bonded to the sp2-hybridised carbon atom of an aromatic ring.

summary: This section outlines the classification of haloalkanes and haloarenes. Compounds are categorized based on the number of halogen atoms they contain, as well as the hybridization state (sp3 or sp2) of the carbon atom bonded to the halogen.
title: Classification

evidence_snippets.text: In the IUPAC system, they are named as halosubstituted hydrocarbons.
Common names of alkyl halides are derived by naming the alkyl group followed by the name of the halide.
They are classified as geminal halides (or gem-dihalides) when both halogen atoms are present on the same carbon atom
vicinal halides or vic-dihalides when halogen atoms are present on adjacent carbon atoms.
Naming haloalkenes requires identifying the longest chain containing the double bond and numbering it to give the double bond the lowest possible locant

summary: This section details the common and IUPAC nomenclature systems for haloalkanes, haloarenes, and dihalogen derivatives. It explains the classification of geminal and vicinal dihalides and provides rules for naming haloalkenes and structural isomers.
title: Nomenclature

evidence_snippets.text: Ferromagnetic substances are those that get strongly magnetised in an external magnetic field.
they interact such that they spontaneously align in a common direction over macroscopic volumes called domains.
In some ferromagnetic materials, magnetisation persists even after the external field is removed; these are called hard magnetic materials
materials where magnetisation disappears upon removal of the field are called soft magnetic materials
At high enough temperatures, the domain structure disintegrates due to thermal agitation, and a ferromagnet becomes a paramagnet.

summary: This section introduces ferromagnetism, explaining how ferromagnetic substances get strongly magnetised in external fields. It details the domain theory of ferromagnetism, the distinction between hard and soft magnetic materials, and the temperature dependence of these properties.
title: Ferromagnetism

evidence_snippets.text: the carbon-halogen bond of alkyl halide is polarised
carbon-halogen bond length also increases from C—F to C—I
Some typical bond lengths, bond enthalpies and dipole moments are given in Table 6.2.

summary: This section explains the polarized nature of the carbon-halogen (C-X) bond in alkyl halides, arising from the electronegativity difference between carbon and halogens. It details how bond length, bond enthalpy, and dipole moments vary down the group from fluorine to iodine.
title: Nature of C-X Bond

evidence_snippets.text: Alkyl halides are best prepared from alcohols, which are easily accessible.
Free radical chlorination or bromination of alkanes gives a complex mixture of isomeric mono- and polyhaloalkanes
An alkene is converted to the corresponding alkyl halide by reaction with hydrogen chloride, hydrogen bromide, or hydrogen iodide.
the addition of bromine in carbon tetrachloride (CCl4) to an alkene results in the discharge of the reddish-brown color of bromine.

summary: This section describes the primary laboratory and industrial methods for preparing haloalkanes (alkyl halides). It details synthesis routes starting from alcohols using halogen acids, phosphorus halides, or thionyl chloride, as well as preparation from hydrocarbons via free radical halogenation of alkanes and addition reactions of alkenes.
title: Methods of Preparation of Haloalkanes

evidence_snippets.text: Paramagnetic substances are those that become weakly magnetised when placed in an external magnetic field.
The individual atoms, ions, or molecules of a paramagnetic material possess a permanent magnetic dipole moment.
For these materials, both the magnetic susceptibility $\chi$ and relative permeability $\mu_r$ depend on the material and the sample temperature.
eventually reaching a saturation value where all dipoles are perfectly aligned with the field.

summary: This section defines paramagnetic substances as materials that become weakly magnetised in an external magnetic field and are weakly attracted to magnets. It explains their atomic origin due to permanent atomic magnetic dipoles and describes how their magnetisation depends on temperature and external field strength, eventually reaching saturation.
title: Paramagnetism

evidence_snippets.text: torque on it is $\mathbf{m} \times \mathbf{B}$; (c) its potential energy is $-\mathbf{m} \cdot \mathbf{B}$
Gauss’s law for magnetism states that the net magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero
magnetic intensity is defined as $\mathbf{H} = \frac{\mathbf{B}_0}{\mu_0}$. The magnetisation $\mathbf{M}$... is its dipole moment per unit volume.
$\mathbf{M} = \chi \mathbf{H}$, where $\chi$ is the magnetic susceptibility... $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ and $\mu_r = 1 + \chi$.
magnetic monopoles do not exist. Slicing a magnet in half results in two smaller magnets
Superconductors are metals at very low temperatures that act as perfect diamagnets. In this state, the magnetic susceptibility $\chi = -1$

summary: This section summarizes the fundamental principles of magnetism and matter, including the behavior of a magnetic dipole in a uniform magnetic field, Gauss's law for magnetism, and the non-existence of magnetic monopoles. It also defines key magnetic properties of materials such as magnetisation, magnetic intensity, susceptibility, and permeability, alongside special states like superconductivity.
title: Chapter Summary

evidence_snippets.text: moving electric charges produce magnetic fields.
electric currents are induced in closed coils when subjected to changing magnetic fields.
The phenomenon in which electric current is generated by varying magnetic fields is appropriately called electromagnetic induction.

summary: This section introduces the historical context and definition of electromagnetic induction, highlighting how changing magnetic fields generate electric currents. It traces the connection between electricity and magnetism from Oersted's discoveries to the pioneering experiments of Faraday and Henry.
title: Introduction

evidence_snippets.text: This shows that it is the relative motion between the magnet and the coil that is responsible for the generation (induction) of electric current
This confirms that it is the relative motion between the coils that induces the electric current.
Faraday showed that relative motion is not an absolute requirement for induction.
the deflection increases dramatically when an iron rod is inserted into the coils along their axis, which enhances the magnetic coupling

summary: This section describes the foundational experiments of Michael Faraday and Joseph Henry that led to the discovery of electromagnetic induction. It demonstrates that electric currents are induced in a coil either through relative motion with a magnet or current-carrying coil, or by varying the current in a stationary neighboring coil.
title: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry

evidence_snippets.text: Magnetic flux through a plane of area A placed in a uniform magnetic field B can be written as \Phi_B = B \cdot A = BA \cos \theta
total magnetic flux is the sum of the flux through all infinitesimal area elements d\mathbf{A}_i that make up the surface.
Magnetic flux is a scalar quantity. Its SI unit is the weber (Wb), which is equivalent to a tesla meter squared (T m^2).

summary: This section introduces the concept of magnetic flux, defining it mathematically for both uniform and non-uniform magnetic fields. It also establishes magnetic flux as a scalar quantity and defines its SI unit, the weber.
title: Magnetic Flux

evidence_snippets.text: the magnitude of the induced emf in a circuit is equal to the time rate of change of magnetic flux through that circuit.
The induced emf ε is mathematically expressed as ε = -dΦ_B/dt.
The total induced emf is therefore the sum of the emfs in each individual turn: ε = -N dΦ_B/dt.

summary: This section introduces Faraday's Law of Induction, which states that a change in magnetic flux through a circuit induces an electromotive force (emf) proportional to its rate of change. It provides the mathematical formulation for single and multi-turn coils, identifies the variables influencing flux change, and demonstrates practical calculations of induced emf and current.
title: Faraday's Law of Induction

evidence_snippets.text: The polarity of induced emf is such that it tends to produce a current which opposes the change in magnetic flux that produced it.
Lenz's law is thus consistent with the principle of conservation of energy.
This energy is dissipated by Joule heating produced by the induced current.

summary: Lenz's law determines the polarity of an induced electromotive force (emf), showing that it always opposes the change in magnetic flux that created it. This opposition ensures that electromagnetic induction is fully consistent with the law of conservation of energy, converting mechanical work into electrical energy.
title: Lenz's Law and Conservation of Energy

evidence_snippets.text: The induced emf $Blv$ is called motional emf.
The work done in moving the charge from P to Q is W = qvBl. Since emf is the work done per unit charge, \epsilon = W/q = Blv.
to explain the existence of induced emf or induced current, we must assume that a time-varying magnetic field generates an electric field.
The magnitude of the emf generated across a length dr of the rod as it moves at right angles to the magnetic field is given by d\epsilon = Bv dr.

summary: This section introduces motional electromotive force (emf) and derives its expression using both Faraday's law of induction and the Lorentz force on free charge carriers. It also discusses how time-varying magnetic fields generate electric fields and applies these concepts to rotating conductors.
title: Motional Electromotive Force

evidence_snippets.text: The constant of proportionality in this relation is called inductance.
we find $M_{12} = M_{21} = M$ (Eq. 6.12). This equality is a general property known as the reciprocity theorem.
Self-induction is the phenomenon where an emf is induced in a single isolated coil due to a change of flux through the coil
When considering the energy per unit volume, known as magnetic energy density $u_B$, the expression becomes $u_B = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$.

summary: This section introduces the concept of inductance as the constant of proportionality between magnetic flux linkage and current. It explores mutual inductance between coupled coils, including the reciprocity theorem, and self-inductance of a single coil, deriving expressions for the induced back emf, stored magnetic energy, and magnetic energy density.
title: Inductance

evidence_snippets.text: The AC generator is a primary technological application of electromagnetic induction, used to generate alternating current (ac).
An AC generator consists of a coil, known as the armature, mounted on a rotor shaft.
Performing the differentiation, we obtain \epsilon = NBA \omega \sin(\omega t).
\epsilon_0 = NBA \omega represents the maximum or peak value of the emf.

summary: This section explains the principles, components, and operation of an AC generator, which utilizes electromagnetic induction to produce alternating current. It derives the formula for the sinusoidally varying induced electromotive force (emf) in a rotating coil and describes its commercial applications.
title: AC Generator

evidence_snippets.text: Magnetic flux $\Phi_B$ through a surface of area $\mathbf{A}$ in a field $\mathbf{B}$ is $\Phi_B = \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{A} = BA \cos \theta$.
Faraday's laws state that induced emf $\varepsilon = -N \frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$.
Lenz's law states the polarity of induced emf opposes the change in magnetic flux.
Motional emf for a rod of length $l$ moving at velocity $v$ perpendicular to field $B$ is $\varepsilon = Blv$.
Self-induced emf is $\varepsilon = -L \frac{dI}{dt}$, where $L$ is self-inductance.
Mutual inductance $M_{12}$ relates emf in coil 1 to current change in coil 2: $\varepsilon_1 = -M_{12} \frac{dI_2}{dt}$.

summary: This section provides a comprehensive summary of the key principles of electromagnetic induction, including Faraday's and Lenz's laws, motional EMF, self and mutual inductance, and the operation of AC generators.
title: Summary of Electromagnetic Induction

evidence_snippets.text: Electricity and magnetism are intimately related; moving charges produce magnetic fields, and moving magnets can induce electric currents.
In closed circuits, induced currents oppose the change in flux, consistent with the law of conservation of energy.
In open circuits, an emf is still induced across the ends.
Motional emf can be derived from the Lorentz force on moving charges.
even for stationary charges, a time-varying magnetic field induces an emf, suggesting a symmetry that hints at the principle of relativity.

summary: This section highlights key conceptual nuances of electromagnetic induction, including the relationship between electricity and magnetism, the conservation of energy in induced currents, and the physical origins of motional and induced electromotive force.
title: Conceptual Nuances in Induction

evidence_snippets.text: applying Lenz's law and Faraday's law to various physical configurations.
direction of induced current must be predicted based on changes in magnetic flux.
effects of changing the shape and area of a loop within a uniform magnetic field.

summary: This section provides practical exercises applying Lenz's law and Faraday's law to determine the direction of induced currents. It features visual scenarios involving moving magnets, changing loop shapes, and varying currents to reinforce the core principles of electromagnetic induction.
title: Exercises

evidence_snippets.text: alternating voltage (ac voltage) and alternating current (ac current) are more common in daily life.
The electric mains supply in homes and offices provides a voltage that varies as a sine function of time.
A key advantage of ac voltage is that it can be easily converted from one voltage level to another using transformers

summary: This section introduces alternating current (AC) and alternating voltage, contrasting them with direct current (DC). It highlights the practical advantages of AC, including efficient long-distance transmission via transformers and frequency-dependent circuit behaviors.
title: Introduction

evidence_snippets.text: in a pure resistor, the voltage and current are in phase with each other
The average value of power \bar{p} over a cycle is: \bar{p} = \frac{1}{2} i_m^2 R
The relationship between the rms current and the peak current i_m is: I = i_m / \sqrt{2}
the rms voltage or effective voltage V is defined as v_m / \sqrt{2}
the relationship between voltage and current in an AC circuit follows Ohm's law: V = IR

summary: This section analyzes an AC circuit containing a single resistor, deriving the relationship between alternating voltage and current using Kirchhoff's loop rule. It demonstrates that voltage and current are in phase, introduces the concepts of RMS current and voltage, and derives the average power dissipation.
title: AC Voltage Applied to a Resistor

evidence_snippets.text: Consider a circuit consisting of a resistor R connected to an ac source that produces a sinusoidally varying potential difference
Therefore, in a pure resistor, the voltage and current are in phase with each other
Thus, the average power is: \bar{p} = \frac{1}{2} i_m^2 R
The relationship between the rms current and the peak current i_m is: I = i_m / \sqrt{2}

summary: This section analyzes an AC voltage source connected to a pure resistor, demonstrating that current and voltage remain in phase. It derives the average power dissipation and introduces root mean square (RMS) current and voltage to express AC power and Ohm's law in forms identical to DC circuits.
title: AC Voltage Applied to a Resistor

evidence_snippets.text: A phasor is a vector that rotates about the origin with an angular speed $\omega$.
In a purely resistive circuit, the voltage and current phasors are collinear because they are in phase

summary: This section introduces phasors as rotating vectors used to simplify the analysis of AC circuits. It explains how phasor diagrams represent the amplitude and phase relationships of voltage and current, specifically detailing their application in purely resistive circuits.
title: Representation of AC Current and Voltage by Rotating Vectors — Phasors

evidence_snippets.text: A phasor is a vector that rotates about the origin with an angular speed \omega.
In a purely resistive circuit, the voltage and current phasors are collinear because they are in phase...

summary: This section introduces phasors as rotating vectors used to represent alternating current and voltage, simplifying AC circuit analysis. It explains how phasor diagrams are constructed and illustrates their application to purely resistive circuits where voltage and current are in phase.
title: Representation of AC Current and Voltage by Rotating Vectors — Phasors

evidence_snippets.text: A purely inductive circuit consists of an AC source connected to an inductor with negligible resistance.
The quantity \omega L is known as the inductive reactance, denoted by X_L.
in a purely inductive circuit, the current lags the voltage by \pi/2 radians (or 90^\circ).
the average power supplied to an inductor over one complete cycle is zero.

summary: This section analyzes the behavior of an alternating voltage applied to a purely inductive circuit. It derives the current equation, introduces the concept of inductive reactance, and demonstrates that current lags voltage by 90 degrees, resulting in zero average power consumption over a complete cycle.
title: AC Voltage Applied to an Inductor

evidence_snippets.text: According to Kirchhoff's loop rule, the sum of the source voltage $v$ and the self-induced emf $-L(di/dt)$ must be zero
The quantity $\omega L$ is known as the inductive reactance, denoted by $X_L$.
in a purely inductive circuit, the current lags the voltage by $\pi/2$ radians (or $90^\circ$).
the average power supplied to an inductor over one complete cycle is zero.

summary: This section analyzes the behavior of a purely inductive AC circuit, deriving the relationship between voltage and current. It introduces inductive reactance as the opposition to AC flow and demonstrates that current lags voltage by 90 degrees. Finally, it proves that the average power consumed by a pure inductor over a complete cycle is zero.
title: AC Voltage Applied to an Inductor

evidence_snippets.text: When a capacitor is connected to a DC voltage source, current flows only for the short time required to charge the capacitor.
the term (1/̉ω C) plays the role of resistance and is called capacitive reactance, denoted by X_C
the current leads the voltage by π/2.
the average power supplied to a capacitor over one complete cycle is zero.

summary: This section analyzes the behavior of a capacitor connected to an AC voltage source, deriving the resulting alternating current and defining capacitive reactance. It establishes the phase relationship where current leads voltage by ninety degrees, represents this using phasor diagrams, and demonstrates that the average power supplied to a capacitor over a complete cycle is zero.
title: AC Voltage Applied to a Capacitor

evidence_snippets.text: A series LCR circuit consists of a resistor R, an inductor L, and a capacitor C connected in series to an AC source.
we define the impedance Z of an AC circuit as Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_C - X_L)^2}
Resonance occurs at a specific frequency \omega_0 where the capacitive reactance equals the inductive reactance (X_C = X_L).

summary: This section analyzes the behavior of a series LCR circuit driven by an AC voltage source using phasor diagrams to determine its impedance, current amplitude, and phase relationships. It also introduces the phenomenon of electrical resonance, deriving the resonant frequency and discussing its practical applications in tuning circuits.
title: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit

evidence_snippets.text: The average power P over a complete cycle... P = V I cos phi.
The term cos phi is defined as the power factor.
In purely inductive or capacitive circuits... the current is called 'wattless current'.

summary: This section derives and analyzes average power dissipation in AC circuits, introducing the concept of the power factor. It explores power dissipation across different circuit elements, methods to improve the power factor in transmission, and applications of LCR resonance.
title: Power in AC Circuit: The Power Factor

evidence_snippets.text: A transformer is a static electrical device designed to transfer electrical energy between circuits through electromagnetic induction
we derive the fundamental transformer equation: $\frac{v_s}{v_p} = \frac{N_s}{N_p}$.
If the secondary coil has more turns than the primary ($N_s > N_p$), the voltage is increased ($V_s > V_p$), and it is called a step-up transformer.
if the secondary coil has fewer turns than the primary ($N_s < N_p$), it is a step-down transformer where $V_s < V_p$ and $I_s > I_p$
Actual transformers experience small energy losses due to several physical factors: (i) Flux Leakage... (ii) Resistance... (iii) Eddy currents
To minimize $I^2R$ power losses during transmission over long distances, the voltage from the generator is stepped up to a high value

summary: This section introduces transformers, explaining their construction, working principle, and mathematical equations for voltage and current. It details step-up and step-down configurations, energy loss mechanisms, and their application in long-distance power transmission.
title: Transformers

evidence_snippets.text: In this case, the current is in phase with the applied voltage.
the root mean square (rms) current I is used, defined as I = i_m/\sqrt{2}
The current in the inductor lags the voltage by \pi/2.
The current through the capacitor is \pi/2 ahead of the applied voltage.
The quantity Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_C - X_L)^2} is called the impedance of the circuit.
The phase relationship between current and voltage in an AC circuit is represented by rotating vectors called phasors.

summary: This section provides a comprehensive summary of alternating current (AC) circuits, detailing the behavior of resistors, inductors, and capacitors under AC voltage. It defines key parameters such as RMS values, reactance, impedance, power factor, and resonant frequency, while also outlining the principles of phasors and transformers.
title: Summary of Alternating Current

summary: This section highlights key conceptual insights, subtle nuances, and potential misconceptions regarding alternating current (AC) circuits, power factor, and resonance.
title: Points to Ponder

evidence_snippets.text: When a value is given for ac voltage or current, it is ordinarily the rms value.
The power rating of an element used in ac circuits refers to its average power rating.
the ac ampere is defined in terms of Joule heating: one ampere of rms ac current produces the same average heating effect as one ampere of dc current

summary: This section explains that standard AC voltage and current measurements are specified as RMS values, and power ratings represent average power. It also defines the AC ampere based on the equivalent Joule heating effect of direct current.
title: RMS Values and Power Ratings

evidence_snippets.text: In an ac circuit, voltages across different elements must be added considering their phases.
Although phasors are represented as vectors in diagrams, they represent scalar quantities that vary harmonically.
Pure capacitances and pure inductances do not associate with power losses in an ac circuit; only the resistive element dissipates energy.
In a series RLC circuit, resonance occurs when $X_L = X_C$, leading to \omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}.

summary: This section explains the dynamics of AC circuits using phasors, which represent harmonically varying scalar quantities as rotating vectors. It details phasor addition of voltages in RC circuits, energy dissipation occurring only in resistive elements, and the conditions required for resonance in series RLC circuits.
title: Phasors and Circuit Dynamics

evidence_snippets.text: The power factor in an RLC circuit measures how close the circuit is to expending maximum power.
motors convert electrical to mechanical energy, while generators convert mechanical to electrical energy.
A step-up transformer changes a low-voltage into a high-voltage.
This does not violate the law of conservation of energy, as the current is reduced by the same proportion to maintain the power balance.

summary: This section explains the role of the power factor in RLC circuits and energy transformation in electrical machines like motors and generators. It also describes how step-up transformers change voltage levels while conserving energy by proportionally adjusting current.
title: Power Factor and Machines

evidence_snippets.text: Maxwell noticed a fundamental inconsistency. To resolve this, he suggested the existence of an additional current term... 'displacement current'.
The most significant prediction from these equations is the existence of electromagnetic waves
Maxwell calculated the speed of these waves to be approximately 3 x 10^8 m/s, matching the measured speed of light.

summary: This section introduces the historical context of electromagnetism, highlighting Maxwell's hypothesis of displacement current to resolve inconsistencies in Ampere's law. It outlines Maxwell's equations and their prediction of electromagnetic waves, which propagate at the speed of light.
title: Introduction

evidence_snippets.text: This contradiction indicates that Ampere's circuital law is incomplete and requires an additional term.
The new term, $i_d = \epsilon_0 \frac{d\Phi_E}{dt}$, which arises due to a changing electric field... is called the 'displacement current'
The generalised Ampere’s circuital law, known as the Ampere-Maxwell law, states that...

summary: This section introduces the concept of displacement current to resolve an inconsistency in Ampere's circuital law during the charging of a capacitor. It derives the displacement current from a time-varying electric flux and formulates the generalized Ampere-Maxwell law, culminating in the presentation of Maxwell's four fundamental equations of electromagnetism.
title: Displacement Current

evidence_snippets.text: Electromagnetic waves are produced by accelerated charges.
Maxwell’s equations show that electric and magnetic fields in an electromagnetic wave are perpendicular to each other
E_x = E_0 \sin(kz - \omega t)
c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \epsilon_0} is the speed of light in vacuum.
The peak magnitude of the magnetic field B_0 is related to the peak magnitude of the electric field E_0 by the equation B_0 = E_0/c.
A key characteristic of electromagnetic waves is their ability to transport energy across space.

summary: This section introduces electromagnetic waves, explaining their production by accelerated charges and their transverse nature where electric and magnetic fields oscillate perpendicularly to each other and the direction of propagation. It provides the mathematical equations governing these waves, details their propagation in vacuum and material media, and highlights their ability to transport energy.
title: Electromagnetic Waves

evidence_snippets.text: The electromagnetic spectrum is the orderly classification of electromagnetic waves according to their frequency or wavelength.
Radio waves are generated by the accelerated motion of electric charges within conducting wires.
Microwaves are short-wavelength radio waves with frequencies in the gigahertz (GHz) range.
Infrared waves are produced by hot bodies and molecules.
Ultraviolet (UV) radiation covers wavelengths from about 400 nm ($4 \times 10^{-7}$ m) down to 0.6 nm ($6 \times 10^{-10}$ m).
X-rays lie beyond the UV region and cover wavelengths from approximately $10^{-8}$ m (10 nm) down to $10^{-13}$ m ($10^{-4}$ nm).

summary: This section introduces the electromagnetic spectrum, classifying electromagnetic waves by their frequency and wavelength. It details the production, detection, properties, and practical applications of major spectral regions, including radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays.
title: Electromagnetic Spectrum

evidence_snippets.text: Maxwell identified an inconsistency in Ampere's law and proposed the existence of displacement current ($i_d$) to resolve it.
Electromagnetic waves are produced by accelerating charges.
In 1887, Hertz successfully produced and detected electromagnetic waves with wavelengths of a few meters in a laboratory setting.
The speed $c$ of an electromagnetic wave in a vacuum is determined by the free space permeability ($\mu_0$) and permittivity ($\epsilon_0$)
The electromagnetic spectrum theoretically extends over an infinite range of wavelengths, from $10^{-2}$ Å ($10^{-12}$ m) to $10^6$ m.

summary: This section provides a comprehensive summary of electromagnetic waves, detailing their production by accelerating charges, experimental verification by Hertz, and propagation speed in vacuum and media. It also introduces displacement current to resolve Ampere's law inconsistency and outlines the regions of the electromagnetic spectrum.
title: Chapter Summary
